# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Substantial Equivalence" in the context of FDA 510(k) submission for an assistive medical device. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Substantial Equivalence (SE)** is a regulatory concept defined under **21 CFR 807.92** of the US FDA, used during the **510(k) Premarket Notification** process. A new device is considered substantially equivalent to a legally marketed **predicate device** if it has:
1. The **same intended use** as the predicate, **AND**
2. The **same technological characteristics**, **OR** different technological characteristics that do not raise new questions of safety and effectiveness, and the submitter demonstrates that the device is at least as safe and effective as the predicate.

For an assistive device such as a powered wheelchair, SE means the new wheelchair must be compared to an already-cleared wheelchair in terms of intended use (mobility assistance), materials, power source, control mechanism, and performance — and the new device must not introduce new safety concerns.

*(Word count: ~120 words. Marks split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark criteria, 1 mark example.)*

---

### Question 2: List and briefly explain the four principles of biomedical ethics as defined by Beauchamp and Childress. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four principles of biomedical ethics (Beauchamp & Childress, **Principles of Biomedical Ethics, 1979**) are:

1. **Autonomy** — Respecting the patient's right to make informed, voluntary decisions about their own healthcare.
2. **Beneficence** — The obligation of the engineer/clinician to act in the best interest of the patient, promoting their well-being.
3. **Non-maleficence** — "First, do no harm"; ensuring the device does not cause physical, psychological, or social injury.
4. **Justice** — Fair, equitable, and non-discriminatory distribution of healthcare resources and assistive technology.

*(Word count: ~90 words. 3/4 mark for listing + 1.25 marks for brief explanation each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Convention:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** Describe the FDA classification system for medical devices. With examples of assistive devices, explain Class I, Class II, and Class III categories and the corresponding regulatory pathway for each. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The US FDA classifies medical devices into three regulatory classes based on the level of risk and the degree of regulatory control needed to provide reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness. This classification is codified in **21 CFR Part 860** and is the foundation of the FDA's risk-based regulatory approach.

**Class I — Low Risk (2 marks):**
Class I devices pose the lowest risk and are subject only to **General Controls** (registration, listing, good manufacturing practices, labeling). Most Class I devices are **510(k)-exempt**. Examples of assistive Class I devices include:
- Manual wheelchairs
- Walking canes and crutches
- Reach extension devices
- Elastic bandages

**Class II — Moderate Risk (2 marks):**
Class II devices require General Controls **plus Special Controls** (performance standards, post-market surveillance, patient registries, guidance documents). Most Class II devices require a **510(k) Premarket Notification**. Examples include:
- Powered wheelchairs
- Hearing aids
- Surgical drapes
- Infusion pumps
- Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) machines

**Class III — High Risk (2 marks):**
Class III devices are life-supporting, life-sustaining, or implantable. They require General Controls **plus Premarket Approval (PMA)**, which involves extensive clinical trials and scientific evidence of safety and effectiveness. Examples of assistive Class III devices include:
- Cochlear implants
- Implantable pacemakers
- Deep brain stimulators for Parkinson's
- Ventricular assist devices (VADs)

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
As the risk level increases from Class I to Class III, the regulatory burden, evidence requirements, and time-to-market also increase. This risk-based stratification ensures that regulatory scrutiny is proportional to the potential harm to patients.

**Key Exam Diagram Tip:** Draw the 3-column table (Class | Risk | Pathway | Example) — this visual will likely earn **1 extra mark** in KTU valuation.

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain the ISO 14971 risk management process for medical devices. Describe the seven stages with relevant examples from assistive device design. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
**ISO 14971:2019** is the international standard that specifies the terminology, principles, and process for **risk management** of medical devices, including software as a medical device. It provides manufacturers with a systematic framework to identify, evaluate, control, and monitor risks throughout the product life cycle.

**The Seven Stages (1 mark per stage = 6 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Risk Management Process Definition:**
The manufacturer establishes a **Risk Management Plan**, defines the device's intended use, identifies characteristics that could impact safety, and assigns responsibilities. *Example:* For a powered wheelchair, intended use = indoor/outdoor mobility for users with limited ambulation; hazards include battery fire, tipping, pinch points.

**Stage 2 — Risk Analysis:**
Hazards and hazardous situations are identified using techniques like **FMEA**, **FTA**, and **HAZOP**. *Example:* For a prosthetic limb, hazards include skin breakdown at the socket interface, structural fatigue, allergic reaction to materials.

**Stage 3 — Risk Evaluation:**
Each hazard is scored on the **Risk Acceptability Matrix** (Severity × Probability). The estimated risk is compared to the company's risk policy. *Example:* A "Probable" mechanical failure leading to "Serious" injury yields Risk = 4 × 3 = 12 → **Unacceptable**, requiring control.

**Stage 4 — Risk Control:**
Risk control measures are applied in a strict hierarchy:
1. **Inherent safety by design** (preferred) — e.g., use of low-voltage lithium iron phosphate batteries.
2. **Protective measures** — e.g., insulation, tilt sensors on wheelchairs.
3. **Information for safety** — e.g., weight limits, contraindications in the user manual.

**Stage 5 — Residual Risk Evaluation:**
After controls, the residual risk is recalculated. If still unacceptable, additional controls are applied iteratively until **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) is achieved.

**Stage 6 — Risk Management Review:**
A formal review confirms that the overall residual risk is acceptable and the device's benefits outweigh the risks. This review is documented in the **Risk Management Report**.

**Stage 7 — Production and Post-Production Information:**
Real-world data from complaints, adverse events, and literature is continuously fed back into Stage 1 to update the risk management file throughout the device's commercial life.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
ISO 14971 is a **lifecycle** process — risk management does not stop at market launch. It is a living document that evolves with new information.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** Discuss the EU MDR 2017/745 classification rules for medical devices. How does it differ from the US FDA classification? Provide examples for each EU class. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **European Union Medical Device Regulation (EU) 2017/745**, commonly called **EU MDR**, came into full effect on **26 May 2021**, replacing the older MDD 93/42/EEC. The EU MDR uses a **rule-based** classification system described in **Annex VIII**, which contains 22 classification rules.

**The Four EU MDR Classes (4 marks):**

| Class | Risk Level | Conformity Assessment | Examples (Assistive) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Low | Self-declaration (mostly) | Walking frames, manual wheelchairs, reachers |
| **Class IIa** | Low-Moderate | Notified Body involvement | Hearing aids, contact lens cleaners, surgical gloves |
| **Class IIb** | Moderate-High | Notified Body involvement | Ventilators, defibrillators, some implants |
| **Class III** | High | Notified Body + extensive review | Cochlear implants, implantable pacemakers, neurostimulators |

**Key Differences from US FDA (2 marks):**

| Aspect | US FDA | EU MDR |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Product-code-based (enumerated list) | Rule-based (22 rules) |
| **Number of Classes** | 3 (I, II, III) | 4 (I, IIa, IIb, III) |
| **Body Responsible** | FDA CDRH | Notified Body (e.g., TÜV SÜD) |
| **Pre-Market** | 510(k) or PMA | CE Marking via Annex IX/X/XI |
| **Post-Market** | MAUDE database | EUDAMED, PSUR |
| **Software** | SaMD guidance + IEC 62304 | MDR Annex VIII Rule 11 (software classification) |

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
While both systems are risk-based, the EU MDR is more **prescriptive and rule-driven**, while the US FDA relies heavily on **predicate device comparison**. Manufacturers selling globally must comply with both.

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the Belmont Report (1979) and its three core principles. Discuss how these principles apply to the design and clinical evaluation of an assistive device such as a brain-computer interface (BCI) for locked-in patients. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction to Belmont Report (1 mark):**
The **Belmont Report** was published in **1979** by the U.S. National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research. It is a foundational ethical document that established **three core principles** for research involving human subjects. Although originally written for clinical research, these principles are routinely applied to **clinical evaluation of assistive medical devices**.

**The Three Belmont Principles (1.5 marks):**

1. **Respect for Persons:**
   - Treat individuals as **autonomous agents**.
   - Protect those with **diminished autonomy** (e.g., children, cognitively impaired).
   - Requires **informed consent**.

2. **Beneficence:**
   - "Do not harm" and "maximize possible benefits while minimizing possible harms."
   - Requires rigorous **risk-benefit assessment** before initiating the study.

3. **Justice:**
   - Fair distribution of **research burdens and benefits**.
   - No group should bear the burden of research without reaping its benefits.

**Application to a BCI for Locked-In Patients (4 marks):**

A **Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)** that allows locked-in patients (e.g., from ALS) to communicate raises profound ethical questions.

- **Respect for Persons / Autonomy:**
  The patient must provide **informed consent**. However, locked-in patients may have limited ability to communicate, so the consent process must be carefully designed — using eye-blink responses, family proxies, or advance directives. **Assent** must be continuously re-confirmed.

- **Beneficence:**
  The BCI must demonstrate **genuine improvement** in the patient's quality of life — e.g., ability to communicate "yes/no," control a wheelchair, or type letters. The device must be rigorously validated to ensure the **benefit outweighs the surgical and psychological risks** of electrode implantation.

- **Non-maleficence (extends Beneficence):**
  Surgical risks (infection, hemorrhage, CSF leak), device failure (electrode migration, software glitches), and psychological harm (frustration, identity issues) must be minimized. A robust **Risk Management File per ISO 14971** is mandatory.

- **Justice:**
  BCI technology is expensive. Justice demands that **access not be limited** to wealthy patients. Equity considerations include: post-trial access to the device, fair subject selection (avoiding exploitation of vulnerable populations), and global accessibility.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
The Belmont Report provides a **timeless ethical framework** for assistive device development. As technology advances into neurotechnology, the Belmont principles become even more critical to ensure that innovation does not outpace moral accountability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't confuse FDA classification with EU MDR classification** — many students write 3 EU classes. Correct: 4 (I, IIa, IIb, III).
> 2. **Don't write "FDA approves Class I devices"** — most Class I devices are **510(k)-exempt**, meaning they are marketed *without* a 510(k). They are still regulated, just minimally.
> 3. **Always mention "post-market surveillance"** in any regulatory answer. KTU examiners reward students who show awareness of the **lifecycle** approach.
> 4. **Distinguish "Belmont Report" (1979) from "Declaration of Helsinki" (1964, last revised 2013)** — these are NOT the same document. Declaration of Helsinki is by the **World Medical Association**.
> 5. **For 14-mark answers, include a diagram** — KTU board examiners typically award **1–2 extra marks** for a well-labeled flowchart or table.
> 6. **Spell-check the document names** — "Substantial Equivalence" is one word in usage; "510(k)" has a lowercase 'k' in parentheses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- **Regulatory Bodies to Remember:** US **FDA** (CDRH), EU (**Notified Bodies** + **European Commission**), India (**CDSCO**), UK (**MHRA**), Japan (**PMDA**), Australia (**TGA**).
- **FDA has 3 Classes (I, II, III); EU MDR has 4 Classes (I, IIa, IIb, III).**
- **510(k) → for Class II** (substantial equivalence to predicate).
- **PMA → for Class III** (independent safety/effectiveness proof).
- **CE Mark → required for EU market** (issued by Notified Body).
- **ISO 13485:2016** → Quality Management System for medical devices.
- **ISO 14971:2019** → Risk Management — 7-stage lifecycle process.
- **IEC 60601-1** → Electrical safety of medical equipment.
- **IEC 62304** → Medical device software lifecycle.
- **ISO 10993** → Biocompatibility evaluation.
- **Belmont Report (1979) → 3 principles:** Respect for Persons, Beneficence, Justice.
- **Beauchamp & Childress (1979) → 4 principles:** Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice.
- **Declaration of Helsinki (1964, revised 2013)** → Ethical principles for medical research involving human subjects.
- **Risk Formula:** $\text{Risk} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Probability}$.
- **Benefit-Risk Ratio:** $\text{BR} = \frac{\sum \text{Benefits}}{\sum \text{Risks}} > 1$ for approval.
- **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable.
- **TPLC** = Total Product Life Cycle (concept → design → verification → submission → manufacturing → post-market).
- **Risk Control Hierarchy:** (1) Inherent Safe Design → (2) Protective Measures → (3) Information for Safety.
- **India's MDR 2017** classifies devices as Class A, B, C, D — CDSCO is the apex body.
- **IRB/IEC** = Institutional Review Board / Independent Ethics Committee — mandatory for clinical studies.
- **MAUDE** = FDA's post-market adverse event database.
- **EUDAMED** = EU's central medical device database.
- **PSUR** = Periodic Safety Update Report (mandatory under EU MDR for Class IIa, IIb, III).
- **For 14-mark answers:** Always include a **diagram or matrix** + mention **Indian context** (MDR 2017) for KTU bonus marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Liner Final Exam Mantra:** *"Regulation tells you what is **legal**; Ethics tells you what is **right**; Standards tell you what is **safe**. A good biomedical engineer masters all three."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards and Guidelines for Accessibility and Usability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Standards and Guidelines for Accessibility and Usability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Standards and Guidelines for Accessibility and Usability

**Standards and Guidelines for Accessibility and Usability** in the context of Assistive Medical Devices refer to a structured set of internationally and nationally recognized technical documents, regulatory frameworks, and engineering protocols that define the minimum requirements, design philosophies, ergonomic principles, and performance benchmarks a medical device must satisfy so that it can be **accessed, perceived, understood, and operated** by users with the widest possible range of capabilities — including persons with disabilities, elderly users, and clinically vulnerable populations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Examiner's Quote Style):**
> "Standards and Guidelines for Accessibility and Usability are codified specifications — issued by bodies such as **ISO, IEC, WHO, FDA, BIS, and the Rehabilitation Council of India (RCI)** — that govern how assistive medical devices are designed, tested, validated, and labelled to ensure equitable access, safe use, and effective human–device interaction for users with diverse abilities."

The two pillars of this topic are:

| Pillar | Meaning | Engineering Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Accessibility** | The device can be *reached, reached into, operated, and understood* by users regardless of physical, sensory, or cognitive ability. | Universal Design, ADA, Section 508, WCAG, ISO 21542 |
| **Usability** | The device can be *used effectively, efficiently, and with satisfaction* within its intended use environment. | Human Factors Engineering, IEC 62366, ISO 9241, ISO 13407 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Elevator"**
> Imagine a hospital with three elevators. Elevator A has tiny buttons placed 2.2 m high with no Braille — fine for an average doctor, useless for a wheelchair user or a blind patient. Elevator B has buttons at wheelchair height with Braille and audio announcement, but the symbols are cryptic. Elevator C has buttons at wheelchair height, large high-contrast icons, Braille, audio cues, raised numerals, and a 2-second tactile confirmation buzz.
> *Elevator C embodies both **accessibility** (anyone can reach and find it) and **usability** (anyone can confidently operate it).*
> The international standards and guidelines we study today are the engineering "rulebook" that ensures every assistive medical device is designed and built like Elevator C — not A.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why examiners love this topic (Valuation Insight):**
> - It tests *regulatory literacy* — a key graduate attribute for biomedical engineers.
> - It maps directly to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Describe the engineering principles and design considerations of various assistive medical devices and rehabilitation aids."*
> - It blends **engineering, ethics, law, and human factors** — ideal for 14-mark analytical questions.
> - It is high-yield: a well-prepared answer can fetch 100% marks with structured presentation.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)

The following terms are **mandatory vocabulary** expected in KTU answer scripts:

- **Accessibility** — the degree to which a product, device, service, or environment is available to as many people as possible, including those with disabilities.
- **Usability** — the extent to which a specified user can achieve specified goals with **effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction** in a specified context of use (per ISO 9241-11).
- **Universal Design** — the design of products and environments to be usable by all people, to the greatest extent possible, without the need for adaptation or specialized design (Ronald Mace, 1997).
- **Assistive Technology (AT)** — any item, piece of equipment, or product system used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities (per the U.S. *Assistive Technology Act of 1998, 29 U.S.C. § 3002*).
- **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)** — the discipline of designing systems, devices, and processes to account for human capabilities, limitations, and characteristics.
- **User-Centered Design (UCD)** — a design philosophy that places the end-user at the center of the design and development process through iterative feedback.
- **Design for All (DfA)** — European equivalent term for Universal Design.
- **Section 508 (US)** — federal law requiring electronic and information technology to be accessible to people with disabilities.
- **WCAG** — Web Content Accessibility Guidelines, published by the W3C.

## 1.5 Key Standardization Bodies

| Body | Full Name | Role in Assistive Devices |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ISO** | International Organization for Standardization | Global voluntary standards (e.g., ISO 9999) |
| **IEC** | International Electrotechnical Commission | Electrical/electronic safety (e.g., IEC 60601) |
| **ISO/IEC Joint Technical Committee (JTC 1)** | Joint committee | Information technology accessibility (e.g., ISO 9241) |
| **WHO** | World Health Organization | Global policy, GATE initiative, ICF framework |
| **FDA** | U.S. Food and Drug Administration | Regulatory approval of medical devices in the USA |
| **European Commission (MDR 2017/745)** | European Medical Device Regulation | Regulatory framework in EU |
| **BIS** | Bureau of Indian Standards | Indian national standards (e.g., IS standards) |
| **RCI** | Rehabilitation Council of India | Rehabilitation professional and device regulation in India |
| **CEN** | European Committee for Standardization | European standards (e.g., EN 12182) |
| **ANSI** | American National Standards Institute | National standards in the USA |
| **RESNA** | Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America | Technical standards for AT |
| **W3C** | World Wide Web Consortium | WCAG, web accessibility |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Foundational Concepts — Accessibility and Usability

### 2.1.1 Accessibility (A)

Accessibility is often defined in the engineering literature as the **possibility of access**. It is a *binary precondition* — either the user *can* access the device or they *cannot*.

**Why and How — Layered Reasoning:**

1. **Why accessibility matters in medical devices:** A hearing aid is useless if a deaf person cannot feel the alert vibration. A wheelchair cushion is useless if a tetraplegic user cannot shift their weight with the supplied control.
2. **How it is achieved:** Through four engineering design properties:
   - **Perceivability** — information is presented so users can perceive it (large fonts, tactile cues, audio cues).
   - **Operability** — interface components and navigation are usable (sufficient time, no seizures, navigable).
   - **Understandability** — information and operation are comprehensible (readable, predictable, input assistance).
   - **Robustness** — content remains accessible as technology advances (compatible with assistive technologies).
3. **Governing principle:** **"Nothing About Us Without Us"** — slogan of the disability rights movement; users must be involved in design.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — POUR:** Accessibility under WCAG is built on the four principles **P**erceivable, **O**perable, **U**nderstandable, **R**obust.

### 2.1.2 Usability (U)

Usability is a *quality attribute* describing **how easy and pleasant** the device is to use. It is governed by the classic framework in **ISO 9241-11 (2018)**.

**Why and How — Layered Reasoning:**

1. **Why usability matters:** A device may be *accessible* (the user can reach it) but not *usable* (they cannot operate it correctly without frustration or error).
2. **How it is measured:** Through three measurable sub-attributes and one contextual anchor.
3. **Engineering implementation:** Through **Human Factors Engineering** and **User-Centered Design (UCD)** processes as mandated by **IEC 62366-1:2015**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This cheat sheet is the single most important study artifact for this topic. Examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions around these standards.**

### 2.2.1 The Core Usability Equation (ISO 9241-11)

The official ISO 9241-11 model defines usability as a function of three variables measured within a **Context of Use (C)**:

$$U = f(\text{Effectiveness},\ \text{Efficiency},\ \text{Satisfaction} \mid \text{Context of Use})$$

| Variable | Symbol | Definition | Typical Metric | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Effectiveness** | $E_{\text{eff}}$ | Accuracy and completeness with which users achieve specified goals. | Task completion rate | $\% \text{ (successes/total)}$ |
| **Efficiency** | $E_{\text{fic}}$ | Resources expended in relation to accuracy and completeness. | Time-on-task, errors, learning curve | seconds, error count |
| **Satisfaction** | $S$ | Freedom from discomfort and positive attitude towards use. | SUS score, NASA-TLX, Likert scales | score (0–100) |
| **Context of Use** | $C$ | Users, tasks, equipment (hardware, software, materials), and the physical/social environments in which the product is used. | Descriptive | — |

The **Subjective Usability Score (SUS)** — *Brooke, 1996* — is the most common questionnaire metric:

$$\text{SUS Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} \left( \text{contribution}_i \right) \times 2.5$$

where odd-item contributions are $\text{item score} - 1$ and even-item contributions are $5 - \text{item score}$. SUS $> 68$ is considered above average.

### 2.2.2 The Universal Design Principles — Ronald Mace (1997)

There are **7 Principles of Universal Design**. KTU examiners expect all seven to be memorised.

| # | Principle | Engineering Translation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Equitable Use** | Same means of use for all users; identical whenever possible, equivalent when not. |
| 2 | **Flexibility in Use** | Choice of input methods (voice, touch, switch). |
| 3 | **Simple and Intuitive Use** | Unnecessary complexity is eliminated. |
| 4 | **Perceptible Information** | Multi-modal feedback (visual + tactile + audio). |
| 5 | **Tolerance for Error** | Hazards minimised; fail-safe defaults. |
| 6 | **Low Physical Effort** | Operation without fatigue (relevant for crutches, wheelchairs). |
| 7 | **Size and Space for Approach and Use** | Adequate reach ranges and clearances. |

### 2.2.3 The ICF Framework (WHO, 2001)

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** replaces the old "medical model" of disability with a **biopsychosocial model**.

$$\text{Disability} = f(\text{Body Functions/Structures},\ \text{Activities},\ \text{Participation},\ \text{Environmental Factors},\ \text{Personal Factors})$$

This is the conceptual lens through which the WHO's **GATE Initiative** (Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology) and India's **RCI** approach accessibility and usability.

### 2.2.4 Mapping of Standards to the Design Lifecycle

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Table — Standards by Lifecycle Phase**

| Lifecycle Phase | Governing Standard | KTU Buzzword |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Concept & User Research | **ISO 9241-210** (UCD) | User-centered design process |
| Risk Management | **ISO 14971:2019** | Risk analysis for medical devices |
| Usability Engineering | **IEC 62366-1:2015** | Usability engineering file |
| Electrical Safety | **IEC 60601-1** | General safety and essential performance |
| Particular Device Safety | **IEC 60601-2-xx (series)** | E.g., IEC 60601-2-52 for beds |
| Quality Management | **ISO 13485:2016** | QMS for medical devices |
| Classification of AT | **ISO 9999:2022** | Assistive product taxonomy |
| Accessibility of Buildings | **ISO 21542:2011** | Building accessibility |
| Process for UCD | **ISO 13407** | (Superseded by 9241-210) |
| Web/Software Accessibility | **WCAG 2.2** (W3C) | POUR principles |
| Indian Rehabilitation | **RCI Act, 1992** | Regulation of rehabilitation professionals |
| European Regulatory | **EU MDR 2017/745** | Conformité Européenne (CE) Marking |
| US Regulatory | **21 CFR Part 820** (FDA QSR) | Quality system regulation |
| US Accessibility Law | **Section 508, ADA 1990** | Federal procurement law |

## 2.3 Why This System Matters in Engineering

- **Legal compliance** — non-compliant devices cannot be marketed (e.g., no CE mark under EU MDR).
- **Risk reduction** — usability-related use errors account for a large fraction of adverse medical device events; standards minimise this.
- **Market access** — harmonised standards (ISO/IEC) enable global market access.
- **Patient safety** — a usability-engineered infusion pump prevents overdoses.
- **Social equity** — assistive devices that fail on accessibility or usability deepen disability inequality.

## 2.4 The User-Centered Design (UCD) Process — ISO 9241-210

The UCD process is an **iterative 4-phase cycle**:

1. **Understand and specify the context of use** — who is the user, what is the task, what is the environment?
2. **Specify the user requirements** — translate user research into design inputs.
3. **Produce design solutions** — prototypes, mock-ups, simulations.
4. **Evaluate designs against requirements** — usability testing, heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthrough.

After step 4, the loop returns to step 1 with refinements. This is mandated by **IEC 62366-1** for medical devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to students:** This topic is standards-driven and qualitative, not calculation-driven. Therefore, in place of numerical derivations, the "derivations" in this section are **structured logical deconstructions** of the standards frameworks — the way a KTU examiner expects you to "derive" the structure of a standard in a 14-mark answer.

## 3.1 Analytical Deconstruction: ISO 9241-11 Usability Framework

The usability equation is *deconstructed* into measurable engineering sub-attributes as follows.

### Step 1 — Establish the Context of Use (C)

The context of use is **not a single number** — it is a five-tuple:

$$C = \langle U,\ T,\ E_{\text{hard}},\ E_{\text{soft}},\ P_{\text{phys}} \rangle$$

- $U$ = User characteristics (age, ability, prior training).
- $T$ = Tasks the user is trying to accomplish.
- $E_{\text{hard}}$ = Equipment (hardware).
- $E_{\text{soft}}$ = Equipment (software / materials).
- $P_{\text{phys}}$ = Physical and social environment (lighting, noise, support staff).

> **Logic of the step:** Without first defining $C$, any usability claim is meaningless. A device usable in a quiet clinic may not be usable on a noisy ambulance.

### Step 2 — Operationalise Effectiveness ($E_{\text{eff}}$)

Effectiveness is the **ratio of goals achieved to goals attempted**:

$$E_{\text{eff}} = \frac{G_{\text{achieved}}}{G_{\text{attempted}}} \times 100\%$$

> **Conversion logic:** Count completed tasks. Divide by total tasks assigned. Multiply by 100. KTU convention: a device with $E_{\text{eff}} < 80\%$ on critical tasks is considered to have a usability-driven safety risk.

### Step 3 — Operationalise Efficiency ($E_{\text{fic}}$)

Two common efficiency metrics:

**Time-on-task (ToT):**

$$E_{\text{fic, time}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$$

where $t_i$ is the time taken by user $i$ to complete one standard task.

**Errors per task (E):**

$$E_{\text{fic, error}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i$$

where $e_i$ is the number of errors (defined as *use errors*, not operator mistakes due to insufficient information) committed by user $i$.

> **Conversion logic:** Lower time and lower errors mean higher efficiency. Efficiency is *normalised* by effectiveness — a device on which the user is fast but fails is not efficient.

### Step 4 — Operationalise Satisfaction ($S$)

The most widely used instrument is the **System Usability Scale (SUS)** by John Brooke (1996). The full 10-item 5-point Likert questionnaire yields a score between **0 and 100**.

$$\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times \left[ \sum_{i\ \text{odd}} (x_i - 1) + \sum_{i\ \text{even}} (5 - x_i) \right]$$

- $x_i$ is the user's score for item $i$ (1 = strongly disagree, 5 = strongly agree).
- Odd-numbered items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9) are positively worded.
- Even-numbered items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10) are negatively worded.

> **Conversion logic:** The reversal of even items prevents response bias. Multiplying by 2.5 rescales the 0–40 raw score to 0–100. Industry benchmark: SUS $\geq 68$ is "above average", SUS $\geq 80$ is "excellent".

### Step 5 — Combine into the Usability Index

A common engineering composite is the weighted sum:

$$U_{\text{score}} = w_1 \cdot E_{\text{eff}} + w_2 \cdot \tilde{E}_{\text{fic}} + w_3 \cdot \tilde{S}$$

where $\tilde{E}_{\text{fic}}$ and $\tilde{S}$ are normalised 0–100 values of efficiency and satisfaction, and the weights $w_1, w_2, w_3$ sum to 1. KTU students should be ready to defend a chosen weighting in an exam (e.g., $w_1 = 0.5,\ w_2 = 0.3,\ w_3 = 0.2$ for safety-critical devices).

## 3.2 Analytical Deconstruction: IEC 62366-1 Usability Engineering File

For a 14-mark question on IEC 62366, the logical derivation is:

**Step 1:** Identify the **intended medical indication** and **user profile** (e.g., a home-use glucose meter for elderly patients with diabetes).

**Step 2:** Identify the **use environment** (clinic, home, ambulance, etc.).

**Step 3:** Identify the **frequently used functions** and **critical tasks** (e.g., lancing, blood application, reading result).

**Step 4:** Conduct **use-related risk analysis (URRA)** per ISO 14971 — link each critical task to a possible use error, harm, and severity.

**Step 5:** Derive the **Usability Engineering (UE) file** containing:
- Use specification
- Use-related risk analysis
- User research records
- Formative evaluation reports
- Summative evaluation (human factors validation) report

**Step 6:** Summative evaluation demonstrates that the **residual use-related risk is acceptable**.

## 3.3 Analytical Deconstruction: WCAG Conformance Levels

WCAG 2.2 defines four conformance levels:

| Level | Name | Meaning | Example |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Minimum | Essential accessibility — without it, content is inaccessible to entire user groups. | Alt-text for images. |
| **AA** | Standard | Removes significant barriers. Industry default target. | 4.5:1 colour contrast. |
| **AAA** | Enhanced | Highest accessibility — not always achievable. | Sign language for audio. |
| (Non-conformant) | Failure | Does not meet the standard. | — |

The KTU logician: *Conformance scales monotonically with the cost of implementation and the inclusivity of the user base reached.*

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Key Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparative Matrix — Memorise this for 14-mark "compare and contrast" questions.**

| Dimension | ISO 9241-11 | IEC 62366-1 | ISO 14971 | ISO 13485 | WCAG 2.2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Ergonomics of human–system interaction | Usability engineering for medical devices | Risk management for medical devices | QMS for medical devices | Web/software accessibility |
| **Mandatory?** | Voluntary (industry standard) | Mandatory under EU MDR, FDA | Mandatory under EU MDR, FDA | Mandatory for QMS | Voluntary globally, mandated by some laws (Section 508) |
| **Focus** | Measuring usability | Engineering usability into the device | Managing all risks | Process discipline | Accessibility of digital content |
| **Output Document** | Usability metrics report | Usability Engineering File | Risk Management File | Quality Manual | Accessibility Conformance Report |
| **Linkage** | Feeds into IEC 62366 | Uses ISO 14971 for risk | Linked to IEC 62366 | Covers IEC 62366 in QMS | Standalone but referenced by ICT standards |
| **Key Phrase** | Effectiveness, Efficiency, Satisfaction | Formative + Summative evaluation | Risk = Probability × Severity | Plan–Do–Check–Act | POUR principles |
| **KTU Module Tag** | Module 1 — Usability | Module 1 — Usability | Cross-module safety | Cross-module quality | Module 1 — Accessibility |

## 3.5 Logical Derivation of Universal Design vs. Accessible Design

**Step 1 — Accessible Design (Reactive):**
Design begins for the *typical* user. Adaptations are retrofitted for users with disabilities.

$$\text{Accessible Design} = \text{Standard Design} + \text{Adaptation Layer}$$

**Step 2 — Universal Design (Proactive):**
Design begins with the *widest possible range of users* in mind, so no retrofit is needed.

$$\text{Universal Design} = \max_{\text{user} \in \mathcal{U}} f(\text{user needs})$$

where $\mathcal{U}$ is the *spectrum* of user capabilities (sensory, motor, cognitive).

**Step 3 — Engineering Consequence:**
- Accessible design is **cheaper upfront** but costlier over the lifecycle (separate products).
- Universal design is **costlier upfront** but cheaper over the lifecycle (single product, larger market).

## 3.6 Worked Example: Applying UCD to a Hearing Aid

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style, 14 marks):** *Design a usability test plan for a behind-the-ear hearing aid for elderly users with presbycusis.*

| UCD Step | Application to Hearing Aid |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. Context of use | Elderly users (60–80 yrs), home environment, mild-to-moderate presbycusis, may have arthritis. |
| 2. User requirements | Large tactile volume wheel, low-battery warning (audio + visual), feedback suppression. |
| 3. Design solution | 3D-printed earhook with curved grip, LED low-battery indicator, telecoil. |
| 4. Evaluation | 10 elderly users, 5 tasks (insert, switch on, adjust volume, change program, remove). Measure $E_{\text{eff}}, E_{\text{fic}}, S$. |
| Iterate | Re-design if SUS $<$ 68 or task completion $<$ 90%. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — The Usability Framework (ISO 9241-11)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Context of Use C] --> B[Usability U]
    B --> C[Effectiveness]
    B --> D[Efficiency]
    B --> E[Satisfaction]
    C --> F[Goal Achievement %]
    D --> G[Time on Task]
    D --> H[Errors per Task]
    E --> I[SUS Score 0-100]
    A1[Users] --> A
    A2[Tasks] --> A
    A3[Equipment] --> A
    A4[Environment] --> A
```

**Caption:** The ISO 9241-11 framework decomposes usability into three measurable sub-attributes within a defined context of use.

## 4.2 Figure 2 — User-Centered Design (ISO 9241-210) Iterative Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step1 Understand Context of Use] --> P2[Step2 Specify User Requirements]
    P2 --> P3[Step3 Produce Design Solutions]
    P3 --> P4[Step4 Evaluate Against Requirements]
    P4 --> P5{Acceptable?}
    P5 -- No --> P2
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Released Product]
```

**Caption:** The four-phase iterative UCD cycle mandated by ISO 9241-210 and IEC 62366-1 for medical device design.

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Hierarchy of Standards Governing an Assistive Device

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[Universal Accessibility Goal] --> L1[International Standards]
    L1 --> L1a[ISO 9999 Taxonomy]
    L1 --> L1b[ISO 9241 Ergonomics]
    L1 --> L1c[IEC 60601 Safety]
    L1 --> L1d[IEC 62366 Usability]
    Top --> L2[Regional Regulations]
    L2 --> L2a[EU MDR 2017 745]
    L2 --> L2b[US FDA 21 CFR]
    L2 --> L2c[India RCI Act]
    Top --> L3[National Accessibility Laws]
    L3 --> L3a[US ADA 1990]
    L3 --> L3b[US Section 508]
    L3 --> L3c[RPwD Act 2016 India]
    Top --> L4[Domain Specific Guidelines]
    L4 --> L4a[WCAG 2.2 Web]
    L4 --> L4b[WHO GATE]
    L4 --> L4c[IS 15393 India]
```

**Caption:** The four-tier regulatory pyramid that governs the design, manufacture, and deployment of an assistive medical device.

## 4.4 Figure 4 — IEC 62366-1 Usability Engineering Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step1 Use Specification] --> S2[Step2 Identify Use Scenarios]
    S2 --> S3[Step3 Use Related Risk Analysis]
    S3 --> S4[Step4 Formative Evaluation]
    S4 --> S5{Residual Risk Acceptable?}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Redesign and Iterate]
    S6 --> S1
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Summative Evaluation]
    S7 --> S8[Usability Engineering File Complete]
```

**Caption:** IEC 62366-1:2015 mandates this usability engineering process for all medical devices subject to regulatory approval.

## 4.5 Figure 5 — Universal Design Principles (Ronald Mace) Mapped to Assistive Devices

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((Universal Design<br/>7 Principles))
        P1[Equitable Use]
            P1e[Same wheelchair model<br/>for all users]
        P2[Flexibility]
            P2e[Voice OR touch<br/>input on AAC device]
        P3[Simple Intuitive]
            P3e[Single button<br/>emergency call]
        P4[Perceptible Info]
            P4e[Audio PLUS visual<br/>PLUS tactile alerts]
        P5[Tolerance for Error]
            P5e[Fail safe insulin<br/>pump defaults]
        P6[Low Effort]
            P6e[Power assisted<br/>wheelchair]
        P7[Size and Space]
            P7e[Wide doorways<br/>and reach ranges]
```

**Caption:** The seven principles of Universal Design mapped to concrete assistive medical device examples.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instructions:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answer in 3–4 sentences. Define → State significance → Give an example.

### Q1. Define accessibility in the context of assistive medical devices. Mention any one standard that governs it. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**
Accessibility is the degree to which a device, product, or environment is available to people with the widest range of abilities, including those with disabilities. In assistive medical devices, accessibility ensures that users with sensory, motor, or cognitive impairments can perceive, reach, and operate the device. It is governed by standards such as **WCAG 2.2** (for software interfaces) and **Section 508** (for electronic and information technology procured by the U.S. federal government). India's **Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPwD) Act, 2016** also mandates accessibility for assistive technology.

### Q2. Differentiate between formative and summative usability evaluation as per IEC 62366-1. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**
**Formative evaluation** is conducted *during* the design and development cycle to identify and fix usability problems iteratively; it does not require a finalised device. **Summative evaluation** is the *final* validation performed on the finished device (or equivalent prototype) with intended users in the intended use environment to demonstrate that the residual use-related risk is acceptable. Formative evaluation refines the design; summative evaluation validates it for regulatory submission.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[A1] (a)** Explain the ISO 9241-11 framework for usability. Define the three measurable attributes and the context of use, with a labelled diagram. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **[A1] (b)** Discuss the User-Centered Design (UCD) process as specified in ISO 9241-210. Illustrate the four iterative phases with a suitable example of an assistive device. **[7 Marks]**
> `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

#### Model Answer to A1(a) — 7 Marks

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** ISO 9241-11 (2018) is the international standard that defines *usability* and provides a framework for measuring it within a defined context of use.

2. **Definition of Usability (1 mark):**
   > "The extent to which a specified user can achieve specified goals with **effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction** in a specified **context of use**."

3. **Three Attributes (3 marks):**
   - **Effectiveness** — accuracy and completeness of goal achievement. Metric: task completion rate ($\%$).
   - **Efficiency** — resources expended (time, errors) per goal. Metric: time-on-task, errors-per-task.
   - **Satisfaction** — freedom from discomfort, positive attitude. Metric: SUS score, NASA-TLX.

4. **Context of Use (1 mark):** Defined as the five-tuple $\langle \text{Users, Tasks, Equipment, Environment, Social} \rangle$. Without context, usability is meaningless.

5. **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to **Figure 1 (Section 4.1)** of these notes — the ISO 9241-11 framework.

#### Model Answer to A1(b) — 7 Marks

1. **Introduction to UCD (1 mark):** User-Centered Design (UCD), defined in ISO 9241-210, is an iterative design philosophy that places the user at the centre of the design process. It is mandated for medical devices under IEC 62366-1.

2. **The Four Phases (4 marks):**
   - **Phase 1 — Understand and specify the context of use:** Identify users (e.g., elderly diabetic patients), tasks (lancing, dosing), equipment, and environment.
   - **Phase 2 — Specify user requirements:** Translate research into design inputs (large display, tactile buttons, audio cues).
   - **Phase 3 — Produce design solutions:** Develop prototypes — 3D-printed, mock-ups, or digital twins.
   - **Phase 4 — Evaluate designs against requirements:** Conduct usability tests, heuristic evaluations, and cognitive walkthroughs.

3. **Iteration (1 mark):** If evaluation reveals problems, return to Phase 2. The loop continues until the design meets acceptance criteria (e.g., SUS $\geq 68$, $E_{\text{eff}} \geq 90\%$).

4. **Example (1 mark):** A behind-the-ear hearing aid for elderly users — context (home, presbycusis), requirements (large tactile wheel), solution (3D-printed earhook), evaluation (10-user summative test), iterate.

5. **Closing Line (carry 1 mark):** UCD ensures that usability is *engineered into* the device, reducing post-market use errors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]**

> **[B1] (a)** Describe the seven principles of Universal Design proposed by Ronald Mace. Apply at least three principles to the design of an electric wheelchair. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **[B1] (b)** Explain the WCAG 2.2 framework of web accessibility. Discuss the four POUR principles and the three conformance levels. **[7 Marks]**
> `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

#### Model Answer to B1(a) — 7 Marks

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Universal Design, coined by architect Ronald Mace (1997), is the design of products and environments to be usable by all people, to the greatest extent possible, without adaptation. It consists of **seven principles**.

2. **The Seven Principles (3 marks):** State all seven — *Equitable Use, Flexibility in Use, Simple and Intuitive Use, Perceptible Information, Tolerance for Error, Low Physical Effort, Size and Space for Approach and Use.*

3. **Application to Electric Wheelchair (3 marks):**
   - **Equitable Use:** Same wheelchair usable indoors and outdoors; no "hospital version" vs "home version".
   - **Flexibility in Use:** Joystick, sip-and-puff, and head-control input options.
   - **Tolerance for Error:** Automatic obstacle detection and gentle braking on slopes.
   - **Low Physical Effort:** Powered drive reduces user fatigue (vs manual wheelchair).
   - **Size and Space:** Compact turning radius for narrow doorways; adjustable armrests.
   - **Perceptible Information:** Audible + visual + haptic low-battery alerts.
   - **Simple and Intuitive:** Single-button folding mechanism with one-touch lock.

4. **Closing (carry 1 mark):** Universal Design expands the addressable market of the wheelchair and avoids the stigma of "specialised" designs.

#### Model Answer to B1(b) — 7 Marks

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** The **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2**, published by the W3C, is the international benchmark for digital accessibility. It is referenced by Section 508, EN 301 549, and India's RPwD Act 2016.

2. **The POUR Principles (4 marks):**
   - **Perceivable** — Information must be presentable in ways users can perceive (e.g., alt-text, captions, sufficient contrast 4.5:1).
   - **Operable** — UI components and navigation must be operable (keyboard access, no time traps, no seizure-inducing flashes).
   - **Understandable** — Information and operation must be understandable (consistent navigation, input assistance, readable text).
   - **Robust** — Content must be robust enough to be interpreted reliably by a wide variety of user agents, including current and future assistive technologies (e.g., screen readers).

3. **Conformance Levels (2 marks):**
   - **Level A** — Minimum; failure excludes entire groups.
   - **Level AA** — Industry standard; the typical legal target.
   - **Level AAA** — Highest; not always achievable.
   - **Non-conformant** — Fails to meet the standard.

4. **Closing (carry 1 mark):** For assistive medical devices with software components (e.g., cochlear implant programming apps, telemedicine interfaces), WCAG 2.2 Level AA conformance is now considered the minimum acceptable practice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — Examiner's Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ISO 9241-11 with ISO 9241-210.** ISO 9241-11 is the *usability measurement* standard; ISO 9241-210 is the *UCD process* standard. They are different documents.
> 2. **Listing standards without explaining their engineering function.** Examiners reward *application*, not memorisation. Always tie a standard to a design or verification step.
> 3. **Mixing up "Usability" and "Accessibility".** Accessibility is a *prerequisite*; usability is a *quality*. They overlap but are not synonyms.
> 4. **Omitting the context of use** in ISO 9241-11 answers. Context is the fifth element of the framework.
> 5. **Skipping the iterative loop** in UCD answers. The loop is worth marks.
> 6. **Ignoring Indian context.** The *RPwD Act 2016* and *RCI Act 1992* are India-specific and frequently examined.
> 7. **Wrong committee names.** ISO ≠ IEC. ISO = International Organization for Standardization; IEC = International Electrotechnical Commission. W3C ≠ ISO.
> 8. **Not using diagrams.** A single labelled block diagram can fetch 1–2 easy marks.
> 9. **Failing to quantify usability** in design questions. Use SUS, $E_{\text{eff}}$, or $E_{\text{fic}}$ metrics — qualitative answers only fetch partial marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 / Topic: Standards and Guidelines for Accessibility and Usability**

- **Accessibility** = possibility of access (perceivable, operable, understandable, robust).
- **Usability** = effectiveness + efficiency + satisfaction within a context (ISO 9241-11).
- **Context of Use** = Users + Tasks + Equipment + Environment + Social setting.
- **SUS** = System Usability Scale; score 0–100; benchmark 68.
- **Universal Design** has **7 principles** (Ronald Mace, 1997) — memorise all seven.
- **WCAG 2.2** has **4 principles** — **POUR** — and **3 conformance levels** (A, AA, AAA).
- **UCD** (ISO 9241-210) is a **4-phase iterative cycle** — Context, Requirements, Design, Evaluation.
- **IEC 62366-1** mandates a **Usability Engineering File** with **Formative + Summative** evaluation.
- **Formative evaluation** = during design; **Summative evaluation** = final validation.
- **ISO 14971** = risk management for medical devices (linked to IEC 62366).
- **ISO 13485** = Quality Management System for medical devices.
- **IEC 60601-1** = general electrical safety and essential performance of medical electrical equipment.
- **ISO 9999** = classification of assistive products (taxonomy).
- **Key global bodies:** ISO, IEC, WHO, FDA, EU (MDR 2017/745), W3C, RESNA.
- **Key Indian bodies/acts:** **RCI** (Rehabilitation Council of India, 1992), **RPwD Act 2016**, **BIS** standards.
- **WHO GATE Initiative** = Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology; promotes the 5P model (People, Policy, Products, Provision, Personnel).
- **Section 508** = U.S. federal law on ICT accessibility; **ADA 1990** = U.S. civil rights law on disability.
- **Design philosophies:** *Universal Design* (proactive, single product) vs *Accessible Design* (reactive, retrofitted).
- **Differential Mnemonic — UD vs AD:** *UD = Upfront Design*; *AD = Adapted Design*.
- **ICF Model (WHO 2001):** Disability is a function of body functions, activities, participation, and environmental factors — **biopsychosocial**, not medical.
- **Indian Context — Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act (RPwD), 2016** increased the list of recognised disabilities from 7 to 21 and mandates accessible assistive technology.
- **EU MDR 2017/745** replaced the MDD (93/42/EEC) and is the current European medical device regulation; it explicitly requires usability engineering per IEC 62366-1.
- **Slogan to remember:** *"Nothing About Us Without Us."* — disability rights movement, central to user-centered design.
- **Mnemonic for standard linkage:** **"URE"** — **U**CD → **R**isk (ISO 14971) → **E**valuation (IEC 62366). This is the workflow inside a Usability Engineering File.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Factors and Ergonomics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human Factors and Ergonomics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Factors (HF)** is the scientific discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among humans and other elements of a system. It applies theoretical principles, data, and methods to design in order to optimize human well-being and overall system performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ergonomics (or Human Engineering)** is the applied science of designing products, tasks, environments, and systems to fit the capabilities and limitations of the human body and mind. The terms *Human Factors* and *Ergonomics* are used interchangeably worldwide (HFES — Human Factors and Ergonomics Society).

The **International Ergonomics Association (IEA)** formally defines ergonomics as:

> *"Ergonomics (or human factors) is the scientific discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among humans and other elements of a system, and the profession that applies theory, principles, data, and methods to design in order to optimize human well-being and overall system performance."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **ready-made shirt vs. a tailor-made shirt**. A mass-produced shirt ignores the fact that you have a longer torso, broader shoulders, or a wider neck. It looks fine on a hanger but pinches, pulls, or hangs loosely on *you*. **Ergonomics is the "tailor" of engineering design** — it measures the human first, then designs the chair, the tool, the wheelchair, or the prosthetic socket around those real measurements rather than assuming a "one-size-fits-all" user.

For a **biomedical engineer** building an assistive medical device (a wheelchair, a hearing aid, an exoskeleton, a surgical tool), this means the device must "speak the language" of the human body — its strength, reach, sight, hearing, cognition, and even fatigue curve.

## 1.3 The Three Sub-Domains of Ergonomics (IEA Classification)

| Sub-domain | Focus Area | Assistive Medical Device Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Ergonomics** | Body posture, biomechanics, anthropometry, repetitive motion, safety | Wheelchair seat dimensions, prosthetic socket fit |
| **Cognitive Ergonomics** | Mental workload, perception, decision-making, human-computer interaction | Auditory display of a ventilator alarm, screen layout of an insulin pump |
| **Organizational Ergonomics** | Work schedules, communication, teamwork, shift design | Workflow in a tele-rehabilitation clinic |

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Reference Man mass:** **70 kg** (used in ISO 7250 and ISO 14738)
> - **Standard Reference Woman mass:** **62 kg**
> - **Design Temperature Range (Comfort):** **20–26 °C** (ISO 7730)
> - **Standard Lumbar Spine Lordosis Angle:** **30°–45°** (seated neutral posture)
> - **Hand Grip Strength (5th percentile female):** **~152 N** (used in tool design)
> - **Hand Grip Strength (95th percentile male):** **~339 N**
> - **Lifting Frequency limit (NIOSH):** 1 lift per 5 s (high frequency) → max recommended **23 kg** at ideal posture; drops to **0 kg** at frequency 1 lift / 1 s under poor posture.

These figures are used to ensure an assistive device does not over-stress the user, the caregiver, or the clinician who operates it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Human Factors Matter in Assistive Medical Devices (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 — The User is Not Average:** Human bodies vary widely. A device designed for the "average" user fits almost no one. Anthropometric data (body measurements) is the foundation of ergonomic design.
- **Step 2 — Mismatch Causes Injury:** A poor fit between user and device produces *Work-related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WMSDs)* such as carpal tunnel syndrome, lower back pain, and pressure sores — the single largest category of occupational injury.
- **Step 3 — The Mismatch is Quantifiable:** Through standardized tools (RULA, REBA, NIOSH Lifting Equation, Ovako Working Posture Analysis System — OWAS), engineers can numerically score a task or posture.
- **Step 4 — Design is Iterative:** The Human Factors cycle is **Analyze → Design → Test → Refine** (similar to the FDA Human Factors and Usability Engineering process — IEC 62366-1).
- **Step 5 — Accessibility & Universal Design:** A device that serves people with disabilities must address the *user*, the *task*, the *tool*, the *environment*, and the *organization* simultaneously.

## 2.2 Anthropometric Principles — Percentile Design

Designs are rarely built for the "average" person. Instead, they target a **5th–95th percentile range**:

- **5th percentile (small female, P5):** Smallest 5% of the population — used to define *minimum reach*, *minimum clearance*, and *minimum grip span*.
- **95th percentile (large male, P95):** Largest 5% of the population — used to define *maximum reach*, *eye height* for displays, and *largest body clearance*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accommodation Strategy:** If a product must serve 90% of users (P5 to P95), you design for the extremes. If it must serve 100%, you must add adjustability (e.g., height-adjustable wheelchair armrests, modular prosthetic sockets).

For a **normally distributed** anthropometric variable $X$ with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the percentile value is given by:

$$P_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma$$

where $z_k$ is the **z-score** corresponding to the $k^{th}$ percentile. For the 5th percentile $z_5 \approx -1.645$; for the 95th percentile $z_{95} \approx +1.645$. This means a 90% accommodation range spans approximately **3.29 standard deviations** of the population.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are extracted from the publicly available ANSI/AIHA, ISO 7250, NIOSH (1991, revised 2024), RULA, and REBA methodologies — the most-tested references in KTU Board Examinations for PEBMT744.

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard Metric | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Percentile value (normal distribution) | $P_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma$ | cm, kg, N | $z_{5} = -1.645$, $z_{95} = +1.645$ |
| 2 | 90% accommodation range | $\Delta = P_{95} - P_{5} \approx 3.29 \sigma$ | unit of measurement | Used to size adjustable ranges |
| 3 | NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) | $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | kg | $LC = 23$ kg load constant (original 1991) |
| 4 | Multipliers in NIOSH equation | Each $M \in [0, 1]$ dimensionless | — | $HM$ = Horizontal, $VM$ = Vertical, $DM$ = Distance, $AM$ = Asymmetry, $FM$ = Frequency, $CM$ = Coupling |
| 5 | Lifting Index (LI) | $LI = \dfrac{L}{RWL}$ | dimensionless | $\vert LI \vert > 1$ indicates elevated risk |
| 6 | RULA Grand Score | $A$ (arm/wrist) + $B$ (neck/trunk/leg) $\to$ Score 1–7 | — | Action Level 1 (Score 1–2) → Acceptable; Level 4 (Score 6–7) → Investigate & change |
| 7 | REBA Final Score | $A$ (neck, trunk, leg) + $B$ (arm, forearm, wrist) + $C$ (coupling) | — | Score 11+ → Necessary change |
| 8 | Body Surface Area (BSA — Mosteller) | $BSA = \sqrt{\dfrac{H \cdot W}{3600}}$ | m² | $H$ in cm, $W$ in kg |
| 9 | Pressure Sore Risk (capillary pressure) | $P_{capillary} \approx 32$ mmHg (4.27 kPa) | mmHg | Wheelchair cushion must keep interface pressure < 32 mmHg |
| 10 | Recommended wheelchair seat width | $W_{seat} = W_{hip,\,P95} + 2 \times 25$ mm clearance | mm | Standard ISO 7176-5 |
| 11 | Pushrim force capacity (manual wheelchair) | $F \approx 0.20 \times W_{user}$ | N | Empirical; for self-propulsion on level ground |
| 12 | Heart rate reserve (workload) | $HR_{target} = HR_{max} \cdot \%Intensity$ | bpm | $HR_{max} = 220 - Age$ |
| 13 | Energy expenditure (walking, basic) | $\approx 0.8$ kcal/kg/km | kcal | Reference for assistive walking devices |
| 14 | Snellen visual acuity | $VA = \dfrac{d_{\text{test}}}{d_{\text{normal}}}$ | — | 20/20 = 1.0; legally blind ≤ 20/200 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises. They are the **decision-making tools** that determine whether a device gets regulatory clearance (FDA, CE), whether a hospital buys it, and whether the user can actually live with it. For instance:

- A **powered wheelchair** manufacturer uses $P_{95}$ shoulder height to set the maximum joystick position.
- A **patient lift** manufacturer uses the NIOSH Lifting Equation to certify that a 50 kg patient lift protects a 25th-percentile female caregiver.
- A **prosthetic socket** uses pressure-mapping to ensure that the interface pressure stays below 32 mmHg to prevent skin breakdown.
- A **hearing aid** uses cognitive ergonomics to limit the number of alerts a user must interpret per minute, preventing alarm fatigue.

## 2.5 Universal Design & Accessibility Principles (Ronald Mace, 1997)

Seven principles that govern the design of every assistive medical device:

1. **Equitable Use** — Useful to people with diverse abilities.
2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a wide range of preferences.
3. **Simple and Intuitive** — Easy to understand regardless of experience.
4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates effectively to the user.
5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards from accidental actions.
6. **Low Physical Effort** — Can be used efficiently with minimum fatigue.
7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Provides adequate space.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Anthropometric Percentile Range for 90% Accommodation

**Problem (KTU-style):** The sitting knee height of a population is normally distributed with mean $\mu = 54$ cm and standard deviation $\sigma = 2.7$ cm. Determine the 5th and 95th percentile values, and the adjustment range required for an ergonomic wheelchair footrest to serve 90% of users.

**Step 1 — Identify the z-scores for the 5th and 95th percentiles from the standard normal table.**
$$z_{5} = -1.645, \quad z_{95} = +1.645$$

**Step 2 — Apply the percentile formula $P_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma$ to compute $P_5$ and $P_{95}$.**
$$P_5 = 54 + (-1.645)(2.7) = 54 - 4.4415 = 49.56 \text{ cm}$$

$$P_{95} = 54 + (+1.645)(2.7) = 54 + 4.4415 = 58.44 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the 90% accommodation range $\Delta$.**
$$\Delta = P_{95} - P_5 = 58.44 - 49.56 = 8.88 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Validate the relationship $\Delta \approx 3.29 \sigma$.**
$$3.29 \cdot \sigma = 3.29 \cdot 2.7 = 8.883 \text{ cm} \approx 8.88 \text{ cm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The footrest must adjust across at least **8.88 cm** to serve 90% of users, with absolute lower limit at **49.56 cm** and upper limit at **58.44 cm**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991 Revised) for a Caregiver

**Problem:** A female nurse (25th percentile strength) must transfer a 35 kg patient from a bed to a wheelchair. The task geometry is:
- Horizontal distance $H = 30$ cm
- Vertical lift origin $V = 25$ cm above floor
- Vertical travel $D = 35$ cm
- Asymmetry angle $A = 45°$ (twist required)
- Frequency $F = 1$ lift / 5 s (sustained, $F = 0.7$ multiplier from NIOSH table)
- Coupling $C$ = Poor (no handles) $\Rightarrow CM = 0.9$

**Step 1 — Recall the Load Constant.**
$$LC = 23 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each multiplier from the NIOSH table (1991, Waters et al.).**
$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{30} = 0.833$$

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 25 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 50 = 0.85$$

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{35} = 0.82 + 0.0514 = 0.8714$$

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 45 = 1 - 0.144 = 0.856$$

$$FM = 0.70 \text{ (frequency table for } \leq 1 \text{ hr, } F=12 \text{ lifts/min)}$$

$$CM = 0.90 \text{ (poor coupling)}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply all components.**
$$RWL = 23 \times 0.833 \times 0.85 \times 0.8714 \times 0.856 \times 0.70 \times 0.90$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate term by term.**
$$23 \times 0.833 = 19.159$$

$$19.159 \times 0.85 = 16.285$$

$$16.285 \times 0.8714 = 14.192$$

$$14.192 \times 0.856 = 12.148$$

$$12.148 \times 0.70 = 8.504$$

$$8.504 \times 0.90 = 7.654 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Lifting Index.**
$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{35}{7.654} = 4.57$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**
$$\vert LI \vert = 4.57 \gg 1 \Rightarrow \text{Unacceptable risk for the 25th-percentile female caregiver.}$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A mechanical patient lift is mandatory. The NIOSH equation provides a quantitative, defensible argument to the hospital administration for procurement.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Body Surface Area (Mosteller) for Pediatric Wheelchair Cushion Sizing

**Problem:** Compute the BSA of a 12-year-old child with $H = 145$ cm and $W = 38$ kg. Use it to size a custom-molded wheelchair cushion (typical cushion footprint = 0.6 × BSA).

**Step 1 — Apply the Mosteller formula.**
$$BSA = \sqrt{\frac{H \cdot W}{3600}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**
$$BSA = \sqrt{\frac{145 \cdot 38}{3600}} = \sqrt{\frac{5510}{3600}} = \sqrt{1.5306}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the square root.**
$$BSA = 1.237 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cushion footprint.**
$$A_{cushion} = 0.6 \cdot BSA = 0.6 \cdot 1.237 = 0.742 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Convert to cm² for the manufacturing drawing.**
$$A_{cushion} = 0.742 \times 10{,}000 = 7420 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Conclusion:** The cushion design footprint is approximately **7420 cm²** (e.g., a 60 cm × 124 cm molded foam block trimmed to fit the ischial tuberosities).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Ergonomic Tool

| Question being asked | Tool | Output | When to apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| What range of motion does the user need? | Anthropometry (P5–P95) | mm | Initial concept design |
| Is this posture safe? | RULA / REBA | 1–7 / 1–15 | Static posture analysis |
| Is this lift safe? | NIOSH Lifting Equation | LI (dimensionless) | Manual handling task |
| Is the cognitive load too high? | NASA-TLX, SWAT | Score 0–100 | Mental workload |
| Will the user injure their skin? | Interface pressure mapping | mmHg | Wheelchair cushion, prosthetic socket |
| Does the device meet universal design? | The 7 Principles of Ronald Mace | Pass/Fail | Final design review |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Human Factors & Ergonomics Conceptual Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Factors and Ergonomics] --> B[Physical Ergonomics]
    A --> C[Cognitive Ergonomics]

    B --> B1[Anthropometry]
    B --> B2[Biomechanics]
    B --> B3[Posture Analysis RULA REBA]

    C --> C1[Mental Workload]

    C --> C3[Situation Awareness]

    D --> D1[Shift Work Design]

    D --> D3[Tele rehabilitation Workflow]
    D --> D4[Staff Training]

    B1 --> E1[Assistive Device Sizing Wheelchair Prosthesis]
    B2 --> E2[Joint Torque Analysis Exoskeleton Lift Aids]
    B3 --> E3[Surgical Ergonomics]
    B4 --> E4[Cumulative Trauma Prevention]

    C1 --> F1[Ventilator Cognitive Load]
    C2 --> F2[Insulin Pump Interface]

    D1 --> G1[24 Hour Caregiver Schedule]

    E1 --> H[User Centred Assistive Medical Device]

    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    F4 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

## 4.2 Anthropometric Design Decision Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify User Population] --> S2[Step 2 Collect Anthropometric Data Mean and SD]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute P5 and P95 using Z scores]
    S3 --> S4{Step 4 Target 90 Percent or 100 Percent}
    S4 -->|90 Percent| S5[Design for P5 to P95 Fixed Range]
    S4 -->|100 Percent| S6[Add Adjustability Modular Components]
    S5 --> S7[Step 5 Prototype and Pressure Test]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Step 6 User Trial with Real Patients]
    S8 --> S9[Step 7 Iterate to Final Design]
```

## 4.3 NIOSH Lifting Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Define Lift Task] --> N2[Measure H V D A F C]
    N2 --> N3[Look up Multipliers from NIOSH Table]
    N3 --> N4[Compute RWL]
    N4 --> N5[Compute LI equals L divided by RWL]
    N5 --> Q{LI greater than 1}
    Q -->|No| N6[Task Acceptable]
    Q -->|Yes| N7[Task Hazardous Redesign Required]
    N7 --> N8[Options Reduce Load Lower Frequency Better Coupling Mechanical Lift]
```
