# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEBMT744, Model Paper]`
**Define Ergonomics. List the three sub-domains of ergonomics as classified by the International Ergonomics Association (IEA), giving one assistive medical device example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Ergonomics is the scientific discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among humans and other elements of a system, and the profession that applies theory, principles, data, and methods to design in order to optimize human well-being and overall system performance.
- **Sub-domains (1 mark each, total 1.5 marks):**
  - **Physical Ergonomics** — Example: Wheelchair seat geometry (seat width, depth, backrest angle).
  - **Cognitive Ergonomics** — Example: Auditory alarm design for an ICU ventilator to minimize alarm fatigue.
  - **Organizational Ergonomics** — Example: Shift scheduling for caregivers in a long-term rehabilitation centre.
- **Closing statement (0.5 marks):** All three sub-domains interact; a complete ergonomic analysis of any assistive device must address all three.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEBMT744]`
**What is meant by 5th–95th percentile design in anthropometry? Why is designing for the "average" user considered an ergonomic error?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **5th–95th percentile definition (1.5 marks):** A population's body measurements follow a normal distribution. The 5th percentile ($P_5$, $z = -1.645$) represents the smallest 5% (typically small females); the 95th percentile ($P_{95}$, $z = +1.645$) represents the largest 5% (typically large males). Designing to the $P_5$–$P_{95}$ band accommodates approximately 90% of the population.
- **Why "average" fails (1.5 marks):** Almost no human is exactly average. A device built for the mean fits no one well — it is too large for the small user (causing reach, grip, and clearance failures) and too small for the large user (causing contact pressure, weight-bearing failures, and discomfort). In assistive medical devices, this directly translates to pressure sores, joint pain, abandonment of the device, and secondary injuries.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. Writing the definition of ergonomics *without* relating it to an assistive device (you lose the "application" marks).
> 2. Confusing RULA and REBA (RULA is for upper limbs in seated/standing work; REBA is for whole-body and is healthcare-preferred).
> 3. Forgetting units in the NIOSH equation. The 23 kg *LC* and the multipliers are dimensionless; you must state this clearly.
> 4. Stating z-scores incorrectly ($z_{5} = -1.645$, **not** $+1.645$).
> 5. Skipping the engineering *interpretation* of $LI > 1$ (the Board awards 2 marks for the recommendation).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, PEBMT744, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the seven principles of Universal Design as formulated by Ronald Mace. For each principle, give **one specific application** to an assistive medical device. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 35-year-old female nurse must manually transfer a patient weighing 40 kg from a bed (origin 30 cm above floor) to a wheelchair 40 cm away horizontally. The lift asymmetry is 30°, frequency is 1 lift per minute for 1 hour, and the coupling is good. Compute the **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** and the **Lifting Index (LI)** using the NIOSH 1991 equation. Comment on the safety of the task. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Seven Principles of Universal Design (1 mark each = 7 marks):**

1. **Equitable Use** — Useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities.
   *Application:* A height-adjustable examination table that serves both a wheelchair user and an ambulatory patient equally well.

2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities.
   *Application:* A prosthetic hand with interchangeable terminal devices (hook, gripper, tool holder) that can be swapped based on the task.

3. **Simple and Intuitive** — Easy to understand, regardless of the user's experience, knowledge, language skills, or concentration level.
   *Application:* An insulin pump with a single large confirmation button and a colour-coded screen (green = safe, amber = warning, red = alert).

4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates necessary information effectively to the user, regardless of ambient conditions or the user's sensory abilities.
   *Application:* A multi-modal alarm (auditory beep + flashing light + tactile vibration) on a home dialysis machine to alert deaf or blind users.

5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards and the adverse consequences of accidental or unintended actions.
   *Application:* A powered wheelchair with a soft-start motor controller that prevents tipping if the joystick is bumped.

6. **Low Physical Effort** — Can be used efficiently and comfortably with a minimum of fatigue.
   *Application:* A ceiling-mounted patient lift that transfers a 100 kg patient with a single caregiver (zero manual lift).

7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Provides appropriate size and space for approach, reach, manipulation, and use regardless of the user's body size, posture, or mobility.
   *Application:* A wheelchair-accessible MRI suite with 1.5 m turning radius, compliant with ISO 7176-5.

**(b) NIOSH Lifting Equation Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Given data (0.5 marks):**
$$L = 40 \text{ kg}, \quad H = 40 \text{ cm}, \quad V = 30 \text{ cm}, \quad D = 40 \text{ cm}, \quad A = 30°, \quad F = 1 \text{ lift/min for 1 hr}, \quad C = \text{good} \Rightarrow CM = 1.00$$

**Step 2 — Load Constant (0.5 marks):**
$$LC = 23 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each multiplier (3.5 marks total):**

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 30 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 45 = 0.865$$

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{40} = 0.82 + 0.045 = 0.865$$

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904$$

$$FM = 0.94 \text{ (from NIOSH table, } F = 1 \text{ lift/min, duration } \leq 1 \text{ hr)}$$

$$CM = 1.00 \text{ (good coupling)}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply all components (1 mark):**
$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.865 \times 0.865 \times 0.904 \times 0.94 \times 1.00$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 = 14.375$$

$$14.375 \times 0.865 = 12.434$$

$$12.434 \times 0.865 = 10.756$$

$$10.756 \times 0.904 = 9.723$$

$$9.723 \times 0.94 = 9.140 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{RWL = 9.14 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 5 — Lifting Index (0.5 marks):**
$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{40}{9.14} = 4.38$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
$$LI = 4.38 \gg 1.0$$
The task is **highly hazardous** for the nurse. The NIOSH guidance is unambiguous: a single lift is **4.38 times** the recommended limit, putting the caregiver at severe risk of acute lumbar injury and long-term Work-related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WMSDs).

**Engineering Recommendations:** (i) Substitute manual lift with a mechanical patient lift (ceiling- or floor-based); (ii) use a sliding transfer board to reduce the horizontal multiplier $HM$; (iii) split the load across two caregivers; (iv) reposition the bed and wheelchair to reduce $H$ to ≤ 25 cm and eliminate the asymmetry.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEBMT744, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Define **Anthropometry**. With the help of a labelled sketch (or block diagram), explain the importance of the 5th–95th percentile design approach in sizing a manual wheelchair for a generic adult population. Compute the seat width for a population where the mean hip breadth is 36 cm and the standard deviation is 2.5 cm. Assume the standard clearance is 25 mm on each side. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Work-related Musculoskeletal Disorder (WMSD)**? List **four common WMSDs** seen in caregivers and rehabilitation professionals. Describe in detail the **RULA (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment)** posture scoring methodology and state its four Action Levels. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Anthropometry & Seat Width (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Anthropometry is the scientific study of the measurements and proportions of the human body. It provides the quantitative data on body dimensions (lengths, breadths, depths, circumferences) required to design products that fit the human user.
- **Labelled sketch / block diagram (1.5 marks):** A frontal view of a seated human showing (i) hip breadth, (ii) thigh clearance, (iii) seat depth, (iv) backrest height, (v) armrest height, (vi) footrest position — each annotated as derived from a specific anthropometric percentile.
- **Importance of 5th–95th percentile design (2 marks):**
  - Ensures the product fits a defined percentage (typically 90%) of the target population.
  - Provides quantitative design limits (minimum reach, maximum clearance) rather than vague "average" assumptions.
  - Allows modular / adjustable design where 100% accommodation is needed.
  - Reduces product abandonment, injury, and secondary complications such as pressure sores.
- **Seat width calculation (2.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the percentile formula:**
$$P_5 = \mu + z_5 \cdot \sigma = 36 + (-1.645)(2.5) = 36 - 4.1125 = 31.89 \text{ cm}$$

$$P_{95} = \mu + z_{95} \cdot \sigma = 36 + (+1.645)(2.5) = 36 + 4.1125 = 40.11 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Choose the 95th percentile for seat width (largest hip breadth determines minimum width) (0.5 marks):**
$$W_{hip, P95} = 40.11 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Add bilateral clearance (25 mm = 2.5 cm each side):**
$$W_{seat} = W_{hip, P95} + 2 \times 2.5 = 40.11 + 5.0 = 45.11 \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{W_{seat} \approx 45.1 \text{ cm} \approx 451 \text{ mm}}$$

**Conclusion:** The standard ISO 7176-5 wheelchair seat width for this population is **451 mm**, with a recommended manufacturing tolerance of ± 5 mm.

**(b) WMSD & RULA (7 marks):**

- **WMSD definition (1 mark):** A Work-related Musculoskeletal Disorder is an injury or disorder of the muscles, tendons, nerves, ligaments, joints, cartilage, or spinal discs caused or aggravated by workplace risk factors (force, repetition, awkward posture, vibration, sustained pressure).
- **Four common WMSDs in caregivers and rehab professionals (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Lower Back Pain (LBP)** — from manual patient handling and transfers.
  2. **Carpal Tunnel Syndrome (CTS)** — from repetitive gripping of assistive devices, wheelchairs, and tools.
  3. **Rotator Cuff Tendinitis** — from overhead reaching during patient transfers and from propelling manual wheelchairs.
  4. **Lateral Epicondylitis (Tennis Elbow)** — from repetitive lifting, gripping crutches, or pushing heavy equipment.
- **RULA methodology (3 marks):**
  - RULA (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment, McAtamney & Corlett, 1993) is a single-page observational tool that scores **postural loading** on the upper limb, neck, and trunk.
  - **Step A — Score the upper arm (1–6), lower arm (1–2), wrist (1–3), and wrist twist (1–2).** Combine into **Posture A Score** (1–9) using the RULA Table A.
  - **Step B — Score the neck (1–6), trunk (1–6), and legs (1–2).** Combine into **Posture B Score** (1–9) using RULA Table B.
  - **Step C — Add a muscle-use score (+1) and a force/load score (+0 to +3)** to both A and B scores to obtain the **Grand Score C** (1–7).
- **Four Action Levels (1 mark):**
  - **Action Level 1 (Score 1–2):** Acceptable posture.
  - **Action Level 2 (Score 3–4):** Investigate, possible change.
  - **Action Level 3 (Score 5–6):** Investigate and change soon.
  - **Action Level 4 (Score 7):** Investigate and change **immediately**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Ergonomics = Human Factors** — the science of fitting the *system* (device, task, environment) to the *human* (anatomy, physiology, cognition).
- **Three sub-domains:** Physical, Cognitive, Organizational (IEA classification).
- **Anthropometry** is the *quantitative foundation* of ergonomic design — measure before you design.
- **5th–95th percentile range** accommodates ≈ 90% of users; $z_5 = -1.645$, $z_{95} = +1.645$.
- **90% accommodation range** $\Delta = P_{95} - P_5 \approx 3.29 \sigma$.
- **Capillary closing pressure** $\approx$ **32 mmHg** — never exceed this on a wheelchair cushion or prosthetic socket.
- **NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991):** $RWL = 23 \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$; $LI = L / RWL$; **LI > 1 → redesign**.
- **RULA** is for upper-limb posture (1–7 scale; 4 Action Levels).
- **REBA** is for whole-body healthcare tasks (1–15 scale; higher = worse).
- **Universal Design** has **7 principles** (Mace, 1997) — know all seven; one principle per minute, one example per principle.
- **Standard Reference Man** = 70 kg, Reference Woman = 62 kg (ISO 7250/14738).
- **Hand grip strength:** ~152 N (P5 female) to ~339 N (P95 male) — used in tool and handle design.
- **BSA (Mosteller):** $BSA = \sqrt{H \cdot W / 3600}$ — used for cushion, paediatric, and drug-dose design.
- **Pushrim force on a manual wheelchair:** $F \approx 0.20 \times W_{user}$ — used in self-propulsion analysis.
- **Standard luminal comfort temperature:** 20–26 °C (ISO 7730).
- **Snellen visual acuity:** 20/20 = 1.0 normal; ≤ 20/200 legally blind.
- Always **relate the formula to an assistive medical device** in the KTU answer to score full marks.
- The KTU Board favours **NIOSH Lifting Equation** and **RULA/REBA** as high-yield topics — practise at least one numerical problem for each.
- Always state **units** (kg, cm, N, mmHg) and the **engineering interpretation** at the end of every numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials and Manufacturing for Assistive Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Materials and Manufacturing for Assistive Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Materials for Assistive Devices** refer to the carefully engineered biocompatible substances—metals, polymers, ceramics, composites, and smart materials—that are selected to interact safely and effectively with the human body for the purpose of restoring, augmenting, or substituting lost physiological function.

**Manufacturing for Assistive Devices** encompasses the complete suite of fabrication, forming, machining, joining, surface modification, and sterilization processes used to convert raw biomaterials into finished, clinically deployable assistive technologies such as prosthetics, orthotics, implants, exoskeletons, wheelchairs, and sensory aids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEBMT744 – Module 2):**
> The selection of materials is **not** a generic engineering decision. The material must satisfy the **"Biocompatibility Triad"** — (1) *Host Response Compatibility* (no toxicity, no inflammation), (2) *Material Response Compatibility* (no degradation, no corrosion in physiological environment), and (3) *Functional Compatibility* (adequate mechanical, electrical, or optical performance for the intended application).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing materials to build a **kitchen utensil for a baby**. You would never select:
- **Lead** (toxic → analogous to a non-biocompatible metal like pure nickel)
- **Brittle glass** (breaks on impact → analogous to a ceramic with low fracture toughness)
- **Rusted iron** (corrodes in saliva → analogous to a non-passivated stainless steel in body fluid)

You would choose **food-grade stainless steel or silicone rubber** — safe, durable, easy to clean, and non-reactive.

In the same way, when an engineer designs a **knee implant**, a **prosthetic socket**, or a **wheelchair frame**, the material must be:
- **Safe** inside the human body (biocompatible)
- **Strong enough** to bear body weight and movement loads
- **Light** so the user is not fatigued
- **Resistant** to body fluids (corrosion-resistant)
- **Long-lasting** (fatigue and wear resistant)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Body is a Hostile Environment for Materials:**
> The human body contains **37 °C temperature, ~7.4 pH, ~0.9 % NaCl saline** with constant mechanical loading and enzymatic attack. A material must survive this for **10–30 years** in implants. This is far more aggressive than most industrial environments.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Body temperature | **36.5 – 37.5 °C (≈ 310 K)** | Operating temperature of implants |
| Blood pH | **7.35 – 7.45 (≈ 7.4)** | Affects corrosion rate |
| Physiological saline concentration | **0.9 % NaCl (≈ 0.154 M)** | Chloride ions initiate pitting corrosion |
| Cortical bone Young's modulus | **7 – 30 GPa** | Reference for stress shielding consideration |
| Titanium Young's modulus | **≈ 110 GPa** | Closer to bone than Co-Cr (≈ 210 GPa) |
| Walking load on hip joint | **≈ 3 – 5× body weight** | Peak cyclic load on lower-limb implants |
| Typical implant service life | **10⁷ – 10⁸ cycles** (10–20 yrs) | Fatigue design requirement |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Biomaterials for Assistive Devices

#### 2.1.1 Metallic Biomaterials

**Why metals?** Load-bearing applications (orthopedic implants, bone plates, dental roots, exoskeleton frames) require **high strength, ductility, and fatigue resistance** that polymers cannot offer.

| Metal | Composition | Key Properties | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **316L Stainless Steel** | Fe-18Cr-14Ni-2Mo | Low cost, moderate corrosion, magnetic | Temporary implants, bone plates, surgical tools |
| **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)** | Ti-6Al-4V | Excellent biocompatibility, low density (4.5 g/cm³), high strength, non-magnetic (MRI safe) | Hip stems, dental implants, spinal cages |
| **Commercially Pure Ti (CP-Ti)** | > 99 % Ti | Best biocompatibility, lower strength | Dental implants, porous coatings |
| **Co-Cr-Mo Alloy** | Co-28Cr-6Mo | Highest wear resistance, very high strength | Joint replacement (femoral head), dental |
| **Nitinol (Ni-Ti)** | Ni-Ti (≈ 55:45) | **Shape memory effect**, superelasticity | Stents, orthodontic wires, smart actuators |
| **Magnesium alloys (Mg-Zn-Ca)** | Biodegradable Mg | Resorbable, low modulus | Temporary bone screws, cardiovascular stents |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biocompatibility ranking (general):** Ti > Co-Cr > 316L SS > Ni alloys
> **Density ranking (lightest first):** Mg (1.74) < Ti (4.5) < Co-Cr (8.3) < SS (7.9) g/cm³

#### 2.1.2 Polymeric Biomaterials

**Why polymers?** Flexibility, low density, ease of fabrication, and tuneable mechanical properties make them indispensable for soft-tissue contact devices.

| Polymer | Key Properties | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **UHMWPE (Ultra-High Molecular Weight Polyethylene)** | Extremely low wear, high impact resistance | Acetabular cup in hip joint, meniscus replacement |
| **PMMA (Polymethyl methacrylate)** | Transparent, rigid, easy to mold | Intraocular lens (IOL), bone cement |
| **Silicone rubber (PDMS)** | Flexible, chemically stable, hydrophobic | Breast implants, catheters, prosthetic liners |
| **PEEK (Polyether ether ketone)** | High strength, radiolucent, bone-like modulus (≈ 3–4 GPa) | Spinal cages, cranial implants, prosthetic sockets |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | Excellent flexibility, abrasion resistance | Artificial heart, vascular grafts, wheelchair cushion |
| **Hydrogels (PHEMA, PVA)** | High water content, soft, permeable | Contact lenses, drug delivery, tissue scaffolds |
| **PLA / PLGA** | Biodegradable, resorbable | Sutures, scaffolds, 3D-printed bone grafts |

#### 2.1.3 Ceramic Biomaterials

**Why ceramics?** Bioinertness, high compressive strength, and wear resistance. **Disadvantage: brittleness.**

| Ceramic | Type | Key Properties | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alumina (Al₂O₃)** | Bioinert | High hardness, low friction | Femoral head, dental crowns |
| **Zirconia (ZrO₂)** | Bioinert | Transformation toughening, high strength | Dental bridges, femoral heads |
| **Hydroxyapatite (HA, Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂)** | Bioactive | Chemically similar to bone mineral | Bone graft substitute, coatings on implants |
| **Bioactive glass (Bioglass®)** | Bioactive | Forms bone-bonding HA layer | Bone repair, ear ossicles |
| **Calcium phosphate cements** | Bioresorbable | Injectable, sets in situ | Vertebroplasty, dental fillings |

#### 2.1.4 Composite Biomaterials

Combining two or more materials to get **superior combined properties**.
- **Carbon-fiber-reinforced PEEK (CFR-PEEK):** Used in spinal implants and prosthetic limbs (lightweight + strong).
- **Fiber-reinforced bone cement:** PMMA + PMMA fibers or Ti wires to reduce fracture risk.
- **Natural composites:** Collagen + HA mimics natural bone matrix.

#### 2.1.5 Smart / Advanced Materials

- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA):** Nitinol — recovers original shape on heating (one-way effect) or cooling (two-way effect).
- **Piezoelectric materials:** PZT, PVDF — convert mechanical strain into voltage and vice versa. Used in energy-harvesting prosthetics, sensors, ultrasonic transducers.
- **Electroactive polymers (EAPs):** Change shape on electrical stimulus — artificial muscles.
- **pH / temperature-responsive hydrogels:** Used in drug-delivery and smart valves.

### 2.2 Key Engineering Properties for Selection

#### 2.2.1 Mechanical Properties

**Young's Modulus (E):** Ratio of stress to strain in elastic region.
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F/A}{\Delta L / L_0}$$

**Tensile Strength (σ_UTS):** Maximum stress a material can withstand while being stretched.

**Yield Strength (σ_y):** Stress at which plastic deformation begins (0.2 % offset rule).

**Fatigue Strength (σ_f or S-N curve):** Stress level below which material can survive ≥ 10⁷ cycles.

**Wear Rate (Archard's equation):** Volume of material lost per unit sliding distance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stress Shielding:** When implant modulus (E) >> bone modulus, bone is "shielded" from load, weakens, and resorbs. **Solution:** use low-modulus materials (Ti, PEEK) or porous structures to reduce effective E.

#### 2.2.2 Surface Properties

- **Surface energy / wettability** affects protein adsorption and cell adhesion.
- **Roughness (Ra):** 1–10 μm promotes osteoblast attachment; < 0.5 μm promotes fibroblast growth.
- **Surface treatments:** Plasma treatment, anodization, sandblasting, HA coating, drug loading.

#### 2.2.3 Biocompatibility Testing (ISO 10993)

| Test | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Cytotoxicity (ISO 10993-5) | Tests if material leaches toxic substances |
| Sensitization (ISO 10993-10) | Tests allergic reaction |
| Irritation / Intracutaneous reactivity | Tests local tissue reaction |
| Systemic toxicity | Tests whole-body reaction |
| Genotoxicity | Tests DNA damage |
| Implantation test (ISO 10993-6) | Long-term tissue response |
| Hemocompatibility | Tests blood-material interaction |

#### 2.2.4 Sterilization Compatibility

Different materials demand different sterilization methods:

| Method | Temp/Agent | Suitable for |
|---|---|---|
| **Autoclave (steam, 121 °C / 134 °C)** | High T, moisture | Metals, ceramics, some polymers (silicone, PP) |
| **Gamma irradiation (25–40 kGy)** | Ionizing radiation | Polymers, tissue grafts |
| **Ethylene Oxide (EtO)** | Low T, toxic gas | Heat-sensitive polymers, electronics |
| **Plasma sterilization (H₂O₂)** | Low T | Sensitive devices, polymer implants |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** UHMWPE cannot be autoclaved repeatedly — it oxidizes. Gamma-irradiated UHMWPE must be stored in inert atmosphere (Ar/N₂) to prevent oxidative degradation.

### 2.3 Manufacturing Processes for Assistive Devices

#### 2.3.1 Conventional (Subtractive) Manufacturing

- **CNC Machining:** Computer-controlled cutting of metals and polymers from solid blocks. Used for custom prosthetic sockets, surgical instruments.
- **Casting (Investment casting for Ti):** Used for complex implant geometries.
- **Forging:** For high-strength orthopedic stems.
- **Grinding / Polishing:** Surface finishing of femoral heads to sub-micron Ra.

#### 2.3.2 Polymer Processing

- **Injection molding:** Mass production of polymer parts (e.g., prosthetic feet, orthotic shells).
- **Compression molding:** Used for UHMWPE acetabular cups.
- **Extrusion:** Tubing for catheters, vascular grafts.
- **Thermoforming:** Custom orthotic shells from thermoplastic sheets (polypropylene, PETG).

#### 2.3.3 Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing) — The KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic

Additive manufacturing (AM) has **revolutionized** assistive devices by enabling:
- **Patient-specific customization** (anatomically exact from CT/MRI scans)
- **Complex internal lattices** (porous scaffolds, weight reduction)
- **Rapid prototyping** (days instead of weeks)
- **On-demand production** (no inventory)

| 3D Printing Technology | Material | Resolution | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** | PLA, ABS, TPU, PEEK | 100–200 μm | Prosthetic sockets, orthotic shells |
| **SLA (Stereolithography)** | Photopolymer resin | 25–50 μm | Dental models, surgical guides |
| **SLS (Selective Laser Sintering)** | Nylon, TPU, PEEK powder | 80–150 μm | Custom orthotics, lattice implants |
| **DMLS / SLM (Direct Metal Laser Sintering / Selective Laser Melting)** | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr, 316L SS | 30–60 μm | Patient-specific implants, exoskeleton parts |
| **PolyJet** | Photopolymer + rubber-like | 16–28 μm | Soft-tissue anatomical models |
| **Bioprinting (Extrusion / Inkjet)** | Cell-laden hydrogels | ~100 μm | Tissue scaffolds, organoids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Bioprinting is classified into (1) **Scaffold-based** (3D printed polymer scaffold seeded with cells), (2) **Scaffold-free** (spheroids or cell sheets assembled), and (3) **In-situ bioprinting** (direct printing at the defect site during surgery).

#### 2.3.4 Joining and Assembly

- **Welding:** TIG, laser, electron beam for Ti implants.
- **Brazing / Soldering:** For dental prostheses.
- **Adhesive bonding:** Epoxy, cyanoacrylate, silicone for polymer assemblies.
- **Mechanical fastening:** Snap-fit, screws (Ti), rivets.

#### 2.3.5 Surface Modification

- **Anodization (Type II / Type III for Ti):** Forms TiO₂ layer for corrosion resistance and dye coloring.
- **Plasma spraying:** HA coating on Ti implants for bioactivity.
- **Electropolishing:** Smoothens metal surface (reduces Ra, removes contaminants).
- **Dip coating / Spin coating:** Polymer coatings (drug-eluting).
- **Sandblasting + acid etching (SLA):** Creates micro-roughness for bone in-growth.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation | Symbol / Form | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Stiffness of material | Pa (GPa) |
| Engineering Stress | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Load per original area | Pa |
| Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Deformation per original length | dimensionless |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ | Lateral strain response | dimensionless |
| Shear Stress | $\tau = V / A$ | Parallel load on area | Pa |
| Shear Modulus | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | Resistance to shear | Pa |
| Bulk Modulus | $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$ | Resistance to compression | Pa |
| Bending Stress | $\sigma = M y / I$ | Stress in beam under moment | Pa |
| Fatigue Life (Basquin) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | Stress vs cycles to failure | Pa, cycles |
| Archard's Wear | $V = k \cdot W \cdot s / H$ | Volume loss in sliding wear | m³ |
| Stress Intensity Factor | $K = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture driving force | Pa·√m |
| Hertzian Contact Stress | $p_{\max} \propto \sqrt{W / (R \cdot E^*)}$ | Contact peak stress | Pa |
| Corrosion Rate (Faday) | $CR = (K \cdot I \cdot EW) / (\rho \cdot A)$ | Mass loss per time | mm/yr |
| Stress Shielding Ratio | $R_{ss} = (E_{\text{implant}} - E_{\text{bone}}) / E_{\text{implant}}$ | Impedance mismatch | dimensionless |
| Fatigue Strength Ratio | $FSR = \sigma_e / \sigma_{UTS}$ | Endurance / tensile ratio | dimensionless |
| Bioresorption Rate | $dM/dt = -k \cdot M$ | First-order mass loss | g/day |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions / validity:**
> - Basquin's law is valid for **10³ < N_f < 10⁶** (high-cycle fatigue).
> - Archard's equation assumes **mild wear, sliding contact, no third-body abrasion**.
> - Poisson's ratio for metals ≈ 0.3, for polymers 0.35–0.45, for cork ≈ 0 (auxetic).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of material-manufacturing combination drives:
1. **Cost-to-patient ratio** (3D-printed Ti spinal cages vs custom-machined).
2. **Time-to-deployment** (3D printing = 1 day; traditional casting = 4–6 weeks).
3. **Regulatory pathway** (FDA 510(k) for substantially equivalent devices; PMA for novel materials).
4. **Sustainability** (biodegradable Mg implants eliminate revision surgery).
5. **Accessibility in low-resource settings** (e.g., Project 3D-printed Jaipur foot, e-NABLE prosthetic hands).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Young's Modulus from First Principles (Hooke's Law)

We derive the relationship between stress, strain, and modulus — the foundation of mechanical property analysis for assistive devices.

**Given:**
- A cylindrical specimen of original length $L_0$ and cross-sectional area $A_0$.
- An axial tensile force $F$ applied along its length.
- The specimen elongates by $\Delta L$ in the elastic region.

**Step 1 — Define Engineering Stress**
The internal force per unit original cross-sectional area is defined as engineering stress. This is the force the material must internally resist to maintain equilibrium.
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 2 — Define Engineering Strain**
The fractional change in length (deformation per unit original length) is defined as engineering strain. This is dimensionless and represents the deformability of the material.
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law**
In the linear elastic region, stress is directly proportional to strain (Robert Hooke, 1660). The proportionality constant is the Young's Modulus.
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**
Replace stress and strain with their definitions to obtain the operational form.
$$\frac{F}{A_0} = E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for E**
Rearranging, the Young's modulus — a fundamental material property — is obtained as the ratio of stress to strain.
$$E = \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$$

**Step 6 — Units Check**
$[\text{Pa}] = [\text{N} \cdot \text{m}] / [\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{m}] = [\text{N}/\text{m}^2] = [\text{Pa}]$ ✓

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU style)**

A Ti-6Al-4V hip stem test specimen has:
- $F = 50{,}000$ N
- $L_0 = 50$ mm = 0.050 m
- $d_0 = 10$ mm → $A_0 = \pi d_0^2 / 4 = \pi (0.010)^2 / 4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5}$ m²
- $\Delta L = 0.025$ mm = $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m

Substituting:
$$E = \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.050}{7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$E = \frac{2500}{1.9635 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.273 \times 10^{12} \text{ Pa} = 1273 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — that's incorrect.** Recalculation:
> Numerator = 2500; Denominator = $1.9635 \times 10^{-9}$ ⇒ $E = 1.273 \times 10^{12}$ Pa = **1273 GPa**, which is ~10× too high for Ti-6Al-4V (actual ≈ 110 GPa). This indicates the strain was underestimated. In reality, a 50 kN load on a 10 mm Ti rod would produce a strain of ~0.6 %, i.e., $\Delta L \approx 0.3$ mm (not 0.025 mm). **Engineering takeaway:** always check sanity of result against published E values (Ti ≈ 110, Co-Cr ≈ 210, 316L SS ≈ 200, PEEK ≈ 3–4, Cortical bone ≈ 7–30 GPa).

### 3.2 Derivation: Archard's Wear Equation

This is the **fundamental equation** for predicting wear in articulating joints (hip, knee) and socket-residual-limb interfaces in prosthetics.

**Given:**
- Two surfaces in sliding contact under normal load $W$.
- Sliding distance $s$.
- Hardness $H$ of the softer material.
- Dimensionless wear coefficient $k$ (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ for polymers, $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ for metals).

**Step 1 — Assume wear volume is proportional to load and sliding distance.**
$$V \propto W \cdot s$$

**Step 2 — Assume the real area of contact is inversely proportional to hardness.**
Real contact area $A_r = W / H$ (this is the classical Bowden-Tabor relation).

**Step 3 — Volume of material removed equals real contact area multiplied by sliding distance.**
$$V = A_r \cdot s = \frac{W \cdot s}{H}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce wear coefficient $k$ to account for non-ideal conditions (third-body particles, surface films, micro-fracture).**
$$V = \frac{k \cdot W \cdot s}{H}$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric wear rate**
$$\frac{dV}{ds} = \frac{k \cdot W}{H}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (Co-Cr femoral head on UHMWPE cup)**

- $W = 2000$ N (body weight load on hip)
- $s$ = walking 1 km → sliding distance per day ≈ 5000 m
- $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ (typical Co-Cr/UHMWPE pair)
- $H_{\text{UHMWPE}} = 2.5 \times 10^{7}$ Pa

$$V = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000 \times 5000}{2.5 \times 10^{7}} = \frac{15}{2.5 \times 10^{7}} = 6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

Convert to mm³: $6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 = 600 \text{ mm}^3$ **per km walked**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** A typical patient walks 1–2 million steps per year. Total UHMWPE wear over 15 years ≈ 60–150 mm³ — within the clinically acceptable limit, but at the threshold of osteolysis (bone resorption due to wear particles). This is why **highly cross-linked UHMWPE (HXLPE)** was developed.

### 3.3 Derivation: Fatigue Life using Basquin's Equation

**Given:**
- A Ti-6Al-4V bone plate subjected to cyclic stress.
- Fatigue strength coefficient $\sigma_f'$ and fatigue strength exponent $b$.

**Step 1 — Basquin's equation relates stress amplitude to number of cycles to failure.**
$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$$

**Step 2 — Take logarithm of both sides to obtain a linear S-N plot.**
$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma_f' + b \log (2N_f)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for cycles to failure.**
$$N_f = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'} \right)^{1/b}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

For Ti-6Al-4V at $R = -1$ (fully reversed loading):
- $\sigma_f' = 1200$ MPa
- $b = -0.085$
- Applied stress amplitude $\sigma_a = 400$ MPa

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{400}{1200} \right)^{1/(-0.085)} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{3} \right)^{-11.76}$$

$$\frac{1}{3} = 0.3333; \quad \log_{10}(0.3333) = -0.4771$$
$$-0.4771 \times (-11.76) = 5.612$$
$$10^{5.612} = 4.09 \times 10^5$$
$$N_f = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.09 \times 10^5 \approx 2.05 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}$$

For a person walking 1 million cycles/year, this is **~ 0.2 years** — clearly insufficient. To achieve 10 years of life, the stress amplitude must be reduced to the **endurance limit** (typically ≈ 0.4 × σ_UTS for Ti).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Material Selection for Common Assistive Devices

| Device | Load / Function | Key Material Demands | Best Material Choice | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hip femoral stem | High cyclic load, body fluid | High fatigue strength, biocompatible, low modulus | Ti-6Al-4V (forged or 3D-printed lattice) | Modulus closer to bone (reduces stress shielding), excellent fatigue life |
| Acetabular cup | Articulating surface | Low wear, low friction | UHMWPE / HXLPE | Lowest wear against Co-Cr or ceramic head |
| Dental implant root | Osseointegration, cyclic bite force | Bioactive, fatigue-resistant | CP-Ti (Grade 4) with SLA surface | Direct bone bonding, proven clinical history |
| Bone plate (fracture fixation) | Static + cyclic load | High strength, formable | 316L SS (temporary) or Ti (long-term) | Ti avoids MRI artifact; SS is cheaper for temporary use |
| Prosthetic socket (below-knee) | Body weight, sweat, skin contact | Light, breathable, custom-shaped | Laminated carbon fiber + epoxy or 3D-printed PP/PA12 | Patient-specific via 3D scan, lightweight |
| Artificial heart | Cyclic pump, blood contact | Fatigue, hemocompatible | Segmented polyurethane (Biomer®) or Ti pump housing | PU has 10⁹ cycle fatigue life |
| Intraocular lens (IOL) | Optical clarity, foldable | Transparent, biocompatible, flexible | Foldable hydrophobic acrylic or silicone | Allows 2 mm incision insertion |
| Cochlear implant electrode | Electrical stimulation, tissue | Flexible, conductive, biostable | Pt-Ir wires in silicone carrier | Pt-Ir inert + silicone flexible |
| Wheelchair frame | Cyclic load, weight | Light, corrosion-resistant | 6061-T6 Al or 3D-printed Ti (premium) | Light + durable |
| Exoskeleton structural link | High cyclic load | High specific strength, fatigue | 7075 Al or CFR-PEEK | Best strength-to-weight |
| Orthotic insole | Compressive, sweat | Cushioning, durable | EVA foam or 3D-printed TPU lattice | Energy return, custom fit |
| Sutures | Tensile, then degrade | Strong then absorbable | Polyglycolide (PGA) or polylactide (PLA) | Degrades in 2–6 months |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Manufacturing Process Selection

| Process | Material | Tolerance | Surface Finish | Lot Size | Typical Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CNC Machining | Metals, PEEK | ± 0.01 mm | Ra 0.4 μm | Low–Med | Custom implants, surgical tools |
| Injection Molding | Thermoplastics | ± 0.05 mm | Ra 0.8 μm | High (10⁵+) | Standardized prosthetic components |
| Compression Molding | UHMWPE, silicone | ± 0.1 mm | Ra 1.6 μm | Med | Acetabular cups, breast implants |
| Investment Casting | Ti, Co-Cr | ± 0.1 mm | Ra 1.6 μm | Med | Femoral components |
| FDM 3D Printing | PLA, ABS, TPU | ± 0.2 mm | Ra 10 μm | Low | Prosthetic sockets, orthotics |
| SLA 3D Printing | Photopolymer resin | ± 0.05 mm | Ra 0.8 μm | Low | Dental models, surgical guides |
| SLS 3D Printing | Nylon, PEEK | ± 0.1 mm | Ra 6 μm | Low–Med | Lattice implants, custom orthotics |
| DMLS 3D Printing | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr | ± 0.05 mm | Ra 8 μm | Low | Patient-specific implants |
| Electrospinning | PCL, PLA, collagen | Sub-micron fibers | Nano-fibrous mat | Low | Tissue scaffolds, vascular grafts |
| Plasma Spray | HA on Ti | ± 0.05 mm coating | Ra 5–10 μm | Med | Bioactive implant coatings |
| Anodization | Ti (Type II/III) | Coating | Colored TiO₂ | Med | Color-coded implants, corrosion resistance |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define biocompatibility. List the three tests in ISO 10993 used to evaluate biocompatibility of an implant material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Biocompatibility is the ability of a material to perform its desired function within the human body **without eliciting any undesirable local or systemic host response** such as toxicity, inflammation, allergic reaction, or carcinogenicity. It is governed by ISO 10993.

**Three ISO 10993 tests (1.5 marks):**
1. **ISO 10993-5: Cytotoxicity test** – Determines if leachates from the material kill or damage cultured cells.
2. **ISO 10993-10: Sensitization and irritation test** – Evaluates potential for allergic contact dermatitis.
3. **ISO 10993-6: Implantation test** – Examines local tissue response after subcutaneous, intramuscular, or bone implantation in an animal model.

(Other acceptable tests: ISO 10993-3 genotoxicity, ISO 10993-4 hemocompatibility.)

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain with examples why Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) is preferred over 316L stainless steel for long-term orthopedic implants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Biocompatibility:** Ti-6Al-4V forms a stable, inert passive TiO₂ oxide layer that does not release toxic ions. 316L SS releases Ni and Cr ions over time, causing allergic and carcinogenic responses. **(1 mark)**
2. **Corrosion resistance:** Ti is far more corrosion-resistant in physiological saline (pitting resistance equivalent number, PREN > 40) than 316L SS, critical for > 10-year service. **(0.75 mark)**
3. **Lower modulus:** Ti (110 GPa) is closer to cortical bone (7–30 GPa) than 316L SS (200 GPa), reducing **stress shielding** and bone resorption. **(0.75 mark)**
4. **Non-magnetic:** Ti is MRI-compatible, while 316L SS causes imaging artifacts and may be contraindicated. **(0.5 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only "Ti is biocompatible" without quantifying or mentioning the **TiO₂ passive layer** or the **stress shielding concept**. You lose 1.5 marks for vague answers. Always mention the **passive oxide layer** explicitly.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]**

**(a)** Classify the biomaterials used in assistive devices with suitable examples. Discuss the advantages and limitations of polymers as biomaterials. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** process for fabricating patient-specific orthopedic implants. List any four advantages of SLS over conventional manufacturing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks**

**Classification of biomaterials (4 marks):**

| Class | Examples | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Metals** | Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Co-Cr-Mo, Nitinol (Ni-Ti), Mg alloys | High strength, ductile, used for load-bearing |
| **Polymers** | UHMWPE, PMMA, PEEK, silicone, PU, PLA, hydrogels | Tunable flexibility, easy fabrication |
| **Ceramics** | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, hydroxyapatite, bioactive glass | Bioinert / bioactive, brittle, high wear resistance |
| **Composites** | CFR-PEEK, HA/collagen, fiber-reinforced bone cement | Synergistic properties |
| **Smart materials** | Shape memory alloys, piezoelectric (PZT, PVDF), electroactive polymers | Respond to stimulus (T, E, pH, stress) |

**Advantages of polymers as biomaterials (2 marks):**
1. Lightweight (density 0.9–1.5 g/cm³ vs 4.5–8 g/cm³ for metals).
2. Easily fabricated into complex shapes via molding, extrusion, 3D printing.
3. Mechanical properties (E, strength) can be tuned via molecular weight, copolymerization, blending.
4. Can be made biodegradable (PLA, PLGA, PGA) for temporary implants.
5. Low cost in mass production.
6. Can be optically transparent (PMMA, hydrophilic hydrogels).

**Limitations of polymers (1 mark):**
1. Lower strength and wear resistance than metals.
2. Creep under sustained load.
3. May undergo oxidative degradation (e.g., UHMWPE shelf-life issue).
4. Some polymers release acidic degradation products (PLA → lactic acid).
5. Cannot be sterilized by autoclave if glass transition is low.

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks**

**SLS Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CAD Model STL File] --> B[Slicing Software - Layer Thickness 0.1 mm]
    B --> C[Build Chamber Filled with Nylon 12 or PEEK Powder]
    C --> D[Laser CO2 Scans Selected Cross Section]
    D --> E[Powder Bed Lowered by One Layer]
    E --> F{More Layers?}
    F -- Yes --> D
    F -- No --> G[Cooling and Part Removal]
    G --> H[Post Processing: Bead Blasting, Infiltration, Sterilization]
    H --> I[Patient Specific Orthopedic Implant]
```

**Process Steps (2 marks):**
1. **CAD model preparation** from CT/MRI scan of patient anatomy.
2. **Slicing** into 2D layers (typically 0.1 mm thick).
3. **Powder deposition:** A roller spreads a thin layer of polymer or metal powder across the build platform.
4. **Laser scanning:** A CO₂ laser (for polymers) or fiber laser (for metals) selectively sinters/fuses the powder following the slice geometry.
5. **Layer-by-layer build:** Build platform descends by one layer thickness; new powder is spread and sintered.
6. **Cooling, depowdering, post-processing** (bead blasting, dyeing, infiltration, sterilization).

**Four advantages of SLS over conventional manufacturing (2 marks):**
1. **No support structures** needed — unsintered powder itself supports the part → enables complex internal lattices and overhangs.
2. **Complex geometry** (lattice, porous, topology-optimized) achievable in one step.
3. **Material efficiency** — unsintered powder is reusable.
4. **Patient-specific customization** directly from CT/MRI data.
5. **No tooling cost** — economical for low-volume, custom implants.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks lost in this question:**
> - Student writes "SLS uses UV light" — **WRONG**. SLS uses a **CO₂ laser** for polymers, **fiber laser** for metals. SLA uses UV.
> - Student does not mention that the **unsintered powder acts as support**, which is the key advantage over SLA/FDM.
> - Student does not list examples of materials (Nylon 12, PA11, TPU, PEEK for polymer SLS; Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr for metal SLS — the latter is DMLS, not strictly SLS).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Module Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **stress shielding** in orthopedic implants. How is it mitigated by material choice and design? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **surface modification techniques** used to improve biocompatibility of metallic implants, with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks**

**Concept of stress shielding (3 marks):**
Stress shielding is a phenomenon in which an orthopedic implant (with Young's modulus significantly higher than that of bone) bears a disproportionately large share of the physiological load, while the surrounding bone is "shielded" from normal mechanical stimulus. According to **Wolff's law** (1892), bone adapts to the loads it experiences — if load decreases, bone resorbs. The result is **bone loss, implant loosening, and eventual failure**. The mismatch ratio is quantified as:
$$R_{ss} = \frac{E_{\text{implant}} - E_{\text{bone}}}{E_{\text{implant}}}$$

For Co-Cr (210 GPa) vs cortical bone (20 GPa): $R_{ss} = (210-20)/210 = 0.905$ (90.5 % load bypass).
For Ti-6Al-4V (110 GPa) vs bone (20 GPa): $R_{ss} = 0.818$ (still high).
For PEEK (3.5 GPa) vs bone: $R_{ss} = -0.83$ (modulus too low — opposite problem: implant may fail).

**Mitigation by material choice (2 marks):**
1. Use **Ti-6Al-4V** instead of Co-Cr (closer modulus to bone).
2. Use **PEEK or CFR-PEEK** (modulus 3–18 GPa, close to bone) for non-articulating components.
3. Use **biodegradable Mg alloys** that gradually transfer load to healing bone and then resorb.

**Mitigation by design (2 marks):**
1. **Porous / lattice structures** (via DMLS / EBM) reduce the effective modulus by introducing controlled porosity (target 50–80 %).
2. **Functionally graded materials (FGM):** dense core + porous outer to match bone modulus at the interface.
3. **Stem geometry optimization** (e.g., shorter, tapered stems).
4. **Hydroxyapatite coating** to enhance bone on-growth, restoring physiological loading.

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks**

**Surface modification techniques (7 marks — 1.5 each for 4 techniques + 1 mark for example):**

**1. Plasma Spray Coating (HA on Ti implants):**
- A plasma jet at > 10,000 °C melts HA powder and propels it onto the Ti substrate, forming a 50–200 μm thick bioactive layer.
- Promotes bone on-growth and early osseointegration.
- Risk: high temperature may degrade HA crystallinity; amorphous HA dissolves faster than crystalline.

**2. Anodization (Type II and Type III for Ti):**
- Electrochemical oxidation of Ti in acidic electrolyte (H₂SO₄ or H₃PO₄) creates a controlled TiO₂ layer (Type II: < 25 nm; Type III hardcoat: > 25 nm).
- Improves corrosion resistance, allows **color-coding** (gold, blue, pink) for surgical identification.
- Type III is used for dental abutments and aerospace-class wear surfaces.

**3. Sandblasting + Acid Etching (SLA):**
- Ti surface is first sandblasted with Al₂O₃ or TiO₂ particles (creating macro-roughness, Ra 1–2 μm) and then acid-etched (HCl/H₂SO₄) for micro-roughness (Ra 0.5–1 μm).
- Dual-scale roughness enhances osteoblast attachment, proliferation, and bone in-growth.
- Used in dental implants (Straumann SLA).

**4. Electropolishing (316L SS, Co-Cr):**
- Electrochemical dissolution of surface asperities in acidic electrolyte yields an ultra-smooth, passive surface (Ra < 0.1 μm).
- Reduces corrosion, removes embedded contaminants, improves fatigue life.
- Critical for articulating surfaces (hip stems).

**5. Bioactive / Drug-Eluting Coatings (mention for completeness):**
- Dip-coating, spin-coating, or sol-gel deposition of biopolymers (chitosan, gelatin) loaded with antibiotics (gentamicin) or growth factors (BMP-2).
- Enables localized drug delivery and reduced post-op infection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "surface treatment improves biocompatibility" without naming the **technique, mechanism, and quantitative result (e.g., Ra, coating thickness)**. You must specify the technique (e.g., anodization), the substrate (Ti), the resulting layer (TiO₂), the thickness (nm to μm), and the effect (corrosion resistance, bioactivity, color coding).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Yield Points for KTU PEBMT744 Module 2**

**1. Biocompatibility**
- Biocompatibility is governed by **ISO 10993** series.
- The **biocompatibility triad** is: host response, material response, functional compatibility.
- A material is "biocompatible" **only for a specific application** (e.g., Ti may be biocompatible as an implant but toxic as a wear particle).

**2. Metals for Assistive Devices**
- **Ti-6Al-4V:** Best balance of biocompatibility, strength, and low modulus. Forms passive TiO₂ layer. MRI-compatible.
- **316L SS:** Cheap, magnetic, contains Ni → limited to temporary implants.
- **Co-Cr-Mo:** Hardest, best for articulating surfaces; releases Cr ions.
- **Nitinol:** Shape memory + superelasticity → stents, orthodontic wires, smart actuators.
- **Mg alloys:** Biodegradable → temporary bone screws, stents.

**3. Polymers for Assistive Devices**
- **UHMWPE:** Acetabular cup; HXLPE (cross-linked) reduces wear further.
- **PMMA:** Bone cement, IOL.
- **PEEK:** Bone-like modulus, radiolucent, used in spinal cages and sockets.
- **Silicone (PDMS):** Catheters, breast implants, prosthetic liners.
- **PLA / PLGA / PGA:** Biodegradable sutures, scaffolds.

**4. Ceramics for Assistive Devices**
- **Alumina & Zirconia:** Bioinert, wear-resistant articulating surfaces.
- **Hydroxyapatite & Bioactive glass:** Bioactive, form bone-bonding layer.

**5. Smart / Advanced Materials**
- **Shape Memory Alloys (Nitinol):** Recover shape on heating.
- **Piezoelectric (PZT, PVDF, BaTiO₃):** Convert strain ↔ voltage.
- **Electroactive polymers (EAPs):** Artificial muscles.
- **Hydrogels (pHEMA, PVA, PEG):** Soft contact lenses, drug delivery.

**6. Manufacturing Processes — Know the Distinction**
- **FDM:** thermoplastic filament melted and extruded — cheap, anisotropic.
- **SLA:** liquid photopolymer cured by UV laser — high resolution, brittle.
- **SLS:** polymer powder fused by CO₂ laser — no support needed, porous.
- **DMLS / SLM:** metal powder fully melted by fiber laser — patient-specific Ti implants.
- **Bioprinting:** extrusion / inkjet / laser-assisted of cell-laden bioink → tissue scaffolds.

**7. Surface Modification**
- **Anodization** (Ti): forms TiO₂, color-codes implants.
- **Plasma spray HA** (Ti): bioactive bone-bonding coating.
- **SLA surface** (Ti dental): sandblasting + acid etching.
- **Electropolishing** (316L SS, Co-Cr): ultra-smooth articulating surface.

**8. Sterilization**
- **Autoclave** (121 °C steam) → metals, ceramics, heat-resistant polymers.
- **Gamma irradiation** (25–40 kGy) → polymers, but UHMWPE needs inert storage.
- **EtO gas** → heat-sensitive polymers and electronics.
- **H₂O₂ plasma** → modern low-temperature sterilization.

**9. Key Equations to Memorize**
- $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$
- $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$
- $V = k W s / H$ (Archard wear)
- $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2 N_f)^b$ (Basquin fatigue)
- $R_{ss} = (E_{\text{implant}} - E_{\text{bone}}) / E_{\text{implant}}$
- $CR = (K \cdot I \cdot EW) / (\rho \cdot A)$ (Faraday corrosion)

**10. Standards and Regulations**
- **ISO 13485:** Quality management for medical devices.
- **ISO 10993:** Biocompatibility evaluation.
- **FDA 510(k):** Substantially equivalent devices.
- **FDA PMA:** Pre-market approval for high-risk novel devices.
- **CE Mark (EU):** Conformité Européenne for European market.

**11. Common Exam Traps**
- Confusing **SLS (powder + CO₂ laser)** with **SLA (liquid resin + UV laser)**.
- Confusing **biocompatible** with **bioactive** (bioactive = forms bond with bone; biocompatible = tolerated by body).
- Confusing **biodegradable** (e.g., Mg, PLA) with **bioinert** (e.g., Ti, Al₂O₃).
- Forgetting units in numerical problems (GPa, MPa, mm³).
- Forgetting the **passive oxide layer (TiO₂)** when explaining Ti's biocompatibility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Processes: Traditional vs. Advanced Methods (e.g., 3D Printing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Processes: Traditional vs. Advanced Methods (e.g., 3D Printing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **manufacturing process** is a sequence of technical operations applied to raw materials (metals, polymers, ceramics, or biological substrates) to transform them into finished assistive medical devices such as orthoses, prostheses, implants, surgical guides, and rehabilitation aids. In the context of **PEBMT744 (Assistive Medical Devices)**, manufacturing processes are broadly classified into:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Manufacturing Processes for assistive medical devices refer to the systematic fabrication methodologies — both conventional (subtractive/ formative) and modern (additive) — used to convert biocompatible raw materials into patient-specific functional devices, ensuring mechanical integrity, anatomical conformity, and regulatory compliance.*

| Category | Strategy | Material Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Traditional / Conventional** | Subtractive & Formative | Material REMOVED or reshaped from a bulk block |
| **Advanced / Modern** | Additive (3D Printing) | Material ADDED layer-by-layer |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Sculptor vs. The Stonemason"**
>
> Imagine you want to create a customized artificial hand for a patient.
>
> - **Traditional Method (Subtractive)** is like a **sculptor carving a statue from a marble block** — you start with extra material and chisel/cut/mill away the unwanted portions. Wasteful, time-consuming, but produces very strong parts.
>
> - **Advanced Method (Additive / 3D Printing)** is like a **chef building a cake one icing layer at a time** — the material is deposited only where needed, allowing complex internal geometries (lattice structures, hollow channels) that a sculptor could never carve.
>
> Both produce the same final object, but **the layer-by-layer chef can create a heart-shaped cake with intricate roses on top** — geometries impossible for the sculptor.

### 1.3 Classification Flow

Manufacturing processes for medical devices fall under three paradigms:

1. **Subtractive Manufacturing (SM)** — Material is removed. Examples: CNC milling, turning, EDM, grinding.
2. **Formative / Deformation Manufacturing (FM)** — Material is shaped without removal. Examples: Injection molding, compression molding, casting, forging, extrusion.
3. **Additive Manufacturing (AM / 3D Printing)** — Material is added. Examples: FDM, SLA, SLS, DMLS, Bioprinting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Insight:** For assistive medical devices, the global shift is towards **Hybrid Manufacturing** — combining traditional strength with additive customization. ISO/ASTM 52900:2021 is the international standard governing AM terminology.

### 1.4 Biocompatibility & Regulatory Context

Standard biocompatible materials used (must be **ISO 10993** tested):
- **Metals**: Ti-6Al-4V (Titanium alloy), 316L Stainless Steel, Co-Cr alloys.
- **Polymers**: PEEK, PLA, ABS, PMMA, Polycarbonate.
- **Bio-inks**: Hydrogels (Alginate, GelMA), decellularized ECM.
- **Ceramics**: Hydroxyapatite (HA), Zirconia, Tricalcium Phosphate (TCP).

> [!WARNING]
> **Material Selection Pitfall:** Not all 3D-printable polymers are biocompatible. PLA is bio-inert and FDA-cleared for temporary implants; ABS releases styrene and is restricted to device housings only.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Traditional Manufacturing Processes — Theory

#### 2.1.1 CNC Machining (Subtractive)
- Uses rotary cutters controlled by G-code to remove material from a solid blank.
- **Tolerance achievable**: $\pm\,0.025$ mm to $\pm\,0.05$ mm.
- **Surface roughness**: $R_a$ = 0.4 – 3.2 μm.
- **Best for**: Metallic orthopedic implants (titanium hip stems, bone plates).

#### 2.1.2 Injection Molding (Formative)
- Molten polymer is injected into a steel mold under high pressure.
- **Cycle time**: 15 – 90 seconds per part.
- **Best for**: Mass production of standardized prosthetic sockets, hand grips, wheelchair components.
- **Limitation**: High initial tooling cost (~$10,000–$100,000) makes it uneconomical for one-off patient-specific devices.

#### 2.1.3 Casting (Formative)
- Liquid metal poured into a mold, allowed to solidify.
- **Used for**: Co-Cr knee implants, dental crowns.
- **Tolerances**: $\pm\,0.1$ to $\pm\,0.5$ mm.

#### 2.1.4 Forging (Formative — Deformation)
- Compressive force shapes metal in solid state.
- **Used for**: High-strength load-bearing implants (titanium femoral stems).

### 2.2 Advanced / Additive Manufacturing Processes — Theory

#### 2.2.1 Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)
- A thermoplastic filament is extruded through a heated nozzle.
- **Layer height (h)**: 0.05 – 0.4 mm.
- **Materials**: PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU (flexible).
- **Use case**: Low-cost prosthetic hands, orthotic braces, anatomical teaching models.

#### 2.2.2 Stereolithography (SLA)
- A UV laser cures liquid photopolymer resin point-by-point.
- **Resolution**: 25 – 100 μm.
- **Best for**: High-detail surgical guides, dental aligners, hearing aid shells.

#### 2.2.3 Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)
- A laser fuses powdered polymer (Nylon PA12) without melting it fully.
- **Advantage**: No support structures needed → complex internal lattices possible.

#### 2.2.4 Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) / SLM
- Laser fuses metal powder layer-by-layer.
- **Materials**: Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr, 316L SS.
- **Use case**: Patient-specific cranial implants, spinal cages, custom knee jigs.

#### 2.2.5 Bioprinting
- Living cells + bio-ink extruded into scaffolds.
- **Use case (emerging)**: Skin grafts for burn patients, cartilage patches, organ-on-chip models.

### 2.3 Operational Logic Flow — Layer-by-Layer Build

The **'Why' and 'How'** of additive layer deposition:

1. **Digital Slice (How)**: A 3D CAD model (STL file) is virtually sliced into N horizontal cross-sections of uniform thickness *h*.
2. **Layer Preparation (Why)**: Each slice is a 2D instruction map for the printer head.
3. **Material Deposition (How)**: The printer head deposits / cures / sinters one cross-section.
4. **Z-Axis Increment (Why)**: The build platform descends by exactly *h*, and the next layer is bonded.
5. **Repeat & Cool**: Process repeats for N layers; final part undergoes post-curing (SLA) or HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing for DMLS).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-examination essential**. Memorize variables and units.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{part}}}{R \cdot h}$ | Total build time of a 3D-printed part | $V$ = volume (mm³); $R$ = deposition rate (mm³/s); $h$ = layer height (mm) |
| 2 | $\text{MRR} = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material Removal Rate (Traditional CNC) | $v$ = cutting speed (mm/min); $f$ = feed (mm/rev); $d$ = depth of cut (mm) |
| 3 | $R_a \approx k \cdot h$ | Surface roughness ≈ proportional to layer height | $k$ ≈ 0.3–0.5 (process constant) |
| 4 | $N_{\text{layers}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{part}}}{h}$ | Number of layers printed | $H$ = part height (mm) |
| 5 | $\text{Part Cost} = C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{machine}} \cdot T_{\text{build}} + C_{\text{labor}}$ | Total cost of a manufactured device | $C$ = cost components ($) |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{AM}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{block}}}$ | Material utilization efficiency (0 < η < 1) | $V_{\text{block}}$ = bounding block volume |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{SM}} \approx 0.05 - 0.30$ | Material utilization of subtractive (typical) | — |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{bond}} = \sigma_{\text{bulk}} \cdot \left[1 - \alpha \cdot \left(\frac{h}{h_0}\right)\right]$ | Layer-bonding strength (empirical) | $\alpha$ = defect coefficient; $h_0$ = reference height |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{\text{cool}} = T_{\text{melt}} - T_{\text{ambient}}$ | Thermal gradient causing residual stress | $T$ in °C or K |
| 10 | $\text{Porosity } \Phi = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{printed}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}$ | Relative density / defect fraction | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip:** $1 \text{ in} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$; $1 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^3$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prosthetics**: 3D-printed transtibial sockets reduce fabrication time from 5 days (traditional lamination) to 8 hours (FDM/Polyjet).
- **Cranial Implants**: DMLS titanium implants are pre-shaped to match CT-scan defect topology — impossible with forging.
- **Surgical Guides**: SLA-printed drill guides improve pedicle screw placement accuracy by **38%** (clinical literature).
- **Wheelchair Customization**: SLS Nylon lattice seat cushions provide patient-specific pressure distribution.
- **Hearing Aids**: Since 2015, **99%** of in-ear hearing aids globally are SLA-printed (mass customization milestone).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Build Time for an FDM 3D Printer

**Problem Statement:** A patient-specific prosthetic socket has total bounding volume $V_{\text{part}} = 48{,}000\ \text{mm}^3$, the printer extrudes material at a volumetric rate $R = 4\ \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$, and layer height $h = 0.2\ \text{mm}$. Derive and calculate the total build time, $T_{\text{build}}$.

**Step 1 — Identify Governing Equation.**
From the formula sheet:

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{R \cdot h}
$$

**Step 2 — Explain the Logic.**
The factor $R \cdot h$ represents the **effective volumetric deposition rate per unit layer height**. Dividing total volume by this rate yields the cumulative time spent depositing all layers.

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values.**

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \frac{48{,}000\ \text{mm}^3}{4\ \text{mm}^3/\text{s} \cdot 0.2\ \text{mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Denominator First.**

$$
R \cdot h = 4 \cdot 0.2 = 0.8\ \text{mm}^4/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Division.**

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \frac{48{,}000}{0.8} = 60{,}000\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Practical Units.**

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \frac{60{,}000}{3600} \approx 16.67\ \text{hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Real FDM time is ~30% longer due to **travel moves** (non-printing head motion) and **first-layer adhesion delay**. Add a multiplicative correction factor $\xi \approx 1.3$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Roughness from Layer Height

**Problem Statement:** For an SLA-printed hearing-aid shell, the printer uses layer height $h = 0.05\ \text{mm}$. The process constant $k = 0.4$. Derive the expected arithmetic surface roughness $R_a$.

**Step 1 — State Empirical Relation.**

$$
R_a \approx k \cdot h
$$

**Step 2 — Plug In Values.**

$$
R_a = 0.4 \cdot 0.05\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
R_a = 0.020\ \text{mm} = 20\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to Traditional CNC.**
A CNC-machined surface achieves $R_a = 0.4$ to $3.2\ \mu\text{m}$, i.e., **6×–50× smoother** than this SLA print. This is why SLA parts often receive post-processing (polishing, vapor smoothing, or clear-coat lacquering).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Utilization Comparison

**Problem Statement:** A titanium hip stem bounding block is $60\ \text{mm} \times 20\ \text{mm} \times 20\ \text{mm}$. The final part volume is $12{,}000\ \text{mm}^3$. (a) Compute AM efficiency $\eta_{\text{AM}}$ assuming DMLS deposits only part volume. (b) Compare with subtractive efficiency if $V_{\text{binding block}}$ of raw titanium is the same.

**Step 1 — Compute Bounding Block Volume.**

$$
V_{\text{block}} = 60 \cdot 20 \cdot 20 = 24{,}000\ \text{mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — AM Efficiency (DMLS deposits only what's needed).**

$$
\eta_{\text{AM}} = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{block}}} = \frac{12{,}000}{24{,}000} = 0.50
$$

**Step 3 — Subtractive Efficiency (CNC from block).**

$$
\eta_{\text{SM}} = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{block}}} = \frac{12{,}000}{24{,}000} = 0.50 \quad \text{(same theoretical)}
$$

**Step 4 — Realistic Adjustment.**
In practice, DMLS includes unfused surrounding powder that can be **recycled** (≥95% reuse), pushing effective $\eta_{\text{AM,real}} \to 0.95+$. CNC swarf cannot be economically reused for medical-grade Ti, giving $\eta_{\text{SM,real}} \approx 0.15$.

$$
\eta_{\text{AM,real}} \approx 0.95 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \eta_{\text{SM,real}} \approx 0.15
$$

**Step 5 — Cost Implication.**
Material savings per implant ≈ $(0.95 - 0.15) \cdot V_{\text{part}} \cdot \rho_{\text{Ti}} \cdot c_{\text{Ti}}$

For titanium: $\rho \approx 4{,}430\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c \approx \$150/\text{kg}$.

$$
\text{Savings per part} = 0.80 \cdot 0.000012\ \text{m}^3 \cdot 4430\ \text{kg/m}^3 \cdot 150\ \$/\text{kg} \approx \$6.38
$$

Modest per part, but **critical at scale** (millions of implants/year).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Traditional vs. Additive

| Parameter | Traditional (CNC / Molding) | Advanced (3D Printing) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Setup Cost** | High (tooling $10k+) | Very Low (no tooling) | AM |
| **Per-Unit Cost (low volume)** | High | Moderate | AM |
| **Per-Unit Cost (high volume)** | Very Low | Moderate | Traditional |
| **Geometric Complexity** | Limited (5-axis max) | Unlimited (lattice, hollow) | AM |
| **Patient-Specific Customization** | Difficult | Trivial (from CT/MRI scan) | AM |
| **Material Waste** | High (70–95%) | Low (5–10%) | AM |
| **Tolerance (metallic parts)** | $\pm\,25\ \mu\text{m}$ | $\pm\,50\ \mu\text{m}$ (post-machining needed) | Traditional |
| **Surface Finish (as-printed)** | $R_a$ 0.4 – 3.2 μm | $R_a$ 5 – 50 μm | Traditional |
| **Build Speed (single unit)** | Faster for simple parts | Slow (hours) | Traditional |
| **Mechanical Strength (Z-axis)** | Isotropic | Anisotropic (layered) | Traditional |
| **Mass-Personalization Scale** | Difficult | Inherently digital | AM |
| **Regulatory Pathway (FDA)** | Established (510k legacy) | Evolving (technical considerations 2017+) | Traditional |
| **Sustainability** | Low | High (recyclable powder) | AM |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Manufacturing Process Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MANUFACTURING PROCESSES FOR ASSISTIVE DEVICES] --> B[TRADITIONAL]
    A --> C[ADVANCED ADDITIVE]
    A --> D[HYBRID]
    B --> B1[SUBTRACTIVE]
    B --> B2[FORMATIVE]
    C --> C1[POLYMER AM]
    C --> C2[METAL AM]
    C --> C3[BIO AM]
    B1 --> B1a[CNC Milling]
    B1 --> B1b[Turning]
    B1 --> B1c[EDM Grinding]
    B2 --> B2a[Injection Molding]
    B2 --> B2b[Compression Molding]
    B2 --> B2c[Casting]
    B2 --> B2d[Forging]
    C1 --> C1a[FDM]
    C1 --> C1b[SLA]
    C1 --> C1c[SLS]
    C1 --> C1d[Polyjet]
    C2 --> C2a[DMLS SLM]
    C2 --> C2b[EBM]
    C3 --> C3a[Extrusion Bioprinting]
    C3 --> C3b[Droplet Inkjet]
    C3 --> C3c[Laser Assisted]
    D --> D1[Subtractive plus Additive Combo]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DMLS Workflow for a Cranial Implant

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[CT SCAN OF PATIENT] --> stepB[SEGMENTATION IN MIMICS]
    stepB --> stepC[STL MODEL GENERATION]
    stepC --> stepD[MAGICS SOFTWARE FOR SUPPORT DESIGN]
    stepD --> stepE[SLICING INTO LAYERS]
    stepE --> stepF[DMLS MACHINE POWDER BED]
    stepF --> stepG[LASER FUSION LAYER BY LAYER]
    stepG --> stepH[BUILD PLATFORM LOWERED BY H]
    stepH --> stepI{ALL LAYERS COMPLETE}
    stepI -->|NO| stepF
    stepI -->|YES| stepJ[STRESS RELIEF HEAT TREATMENT]
    stepJ --> stepK[HIP HOT ISOSTATIC PRESSING]
    stepK --> stepL[CNC POST MACHINING FOR TOLERANCE]
    stepL --> stepM[SURFACE TREATMENT ANODIZING]
    stepM --> stepN[STERILIZATION AUTOCLAVE GAMMA]
    stepN --> stepO[STERILE IMPLANT PACKAGED]
    stepO --> stepP[SURGICAL IMPLANTATION]
```

### 4.3 Process Selection Decision Flow for Assistive Medical Devices

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1[START] --> q2{PRODUCTION VOLUME}
    q2 -->|ONE OFF PATIENT SPECIFIC| q3[3D PRINTING AM]
    q2 -->|MASS PRODUCTION OVER 1000| q4[INJECTION MOLDING OR CNC]
    q2 -->|PROTOTYPE 5 TO 50 UNITS| q5[FDM OR SLA]
    q3 --> q6{MATERIAL}
    q6 -->|METAL TITANIUM COCR| q7[DMLS SLM]
    q6 -->|POLYMER PA12 ABS| q8[SLS FDM]
    q6 -->|LIVING TISSUE| q9[BIOPRINTING]
    q4 --> q10{GEOMETRY}
    q10 -->|SIMPLE PRISMATIC| q11[CNC MILLING]
    q10 -->|COMPLEX UNDERCUTS| q12[MOLDING REQUIRED]
    q7 --> qZ[POST PROCESS HIP CNC POLISH]
    q8 --> qZ
    q11 --> qY[QC INSPECTION]
    q12 --> qY
    q9 --> qZ
```

### 4.4 FDM Nozzle Mechanics — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
graph TB
    filA[FILAMENT SPOOL] --> extA[DRIVE GEAR EXTRUDER]
    extA --> heatA[HEATED NOZZLE 200 TO 250 C]
    heatA --> depA[MOLTEN FILAMENT DEPOSITION]
    depA --> bedA[HEATED BUILD PLATE 60 C]
    bedA --> solidA[SOLIDIFIED LAYER]
    solidA --> movA[Z AXIS INCREMENT BY H]
    movA --> depA
    coolA[FAN COOLING] --> solidA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** In exam questions on FDM, examiners expect you to mention (1) heated nozzle temperature above glass-transition $T_g$, (2) heated bed to prevent warping, (3) layer-by-layer Z increment governed by stepper motor resolution, and (4) active cooling fan.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Additive Manufacturing. List any four medical-device applications of 3D printing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Additive Manufacturing (AM), also called 3D printing, is a process of fabricating three-dimensional solid objects by adding material layer-by-layer from a digital CAD model, as per ISO/ASTM 52900:2021.

Four medical-device applications:
1. Patient-specific cranial implants (DMLS Titanium).
2. Custom prosthetic sockets and artificial limbs (FDM/Polyjet).
3. Surgical drill guides and anatomical models (SLA).
4. Bioprinted skin patches for burn wound management.
5. (Bonus) Custom orthodontic aligners and hearing-aid shells.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare CNC machining and Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) in terms of material utilization efficiency and part complexity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabulated):**

| Parameter | CNC Machining | FDM |
|---|---|---|
| Material Utilization | 5–30% (subtractive) | 90–98% (additive) |
| Part Complexity | Limited by tool access (5-axis typical) | Theoretically unlimited (lattice, internal channels) |
| Setup Cost | Moderate | Low |
| Post-processing | Minimal | Often required (support removal) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the various **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** technologies used in the fabrication of assistive medical devices. Discuss the working principle, materials, advantages, and limitations of **FDM**, **SLA**, **SLS**, and **DMLS**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A custom orthotic insole is to be 3D-printed using FDM. The insole bounding volume is $V = 72{,}000\ \text{mm}^3$, the extrusion rate is $R = 6\ \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$, and layer height $h = 0.15\ \text{mm}$. Calculate:
   (i) Build time $T_{\text{build}}$ in hours.
   (ii) Number of layers if part height is $H = 30\ \text{mm}$.
   (iii) Expected surface roughness $R_a$ assuming $k = 0.4$.
   *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Technology | Working Principle | Materials | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FDM** | Thermoplastic filament melted and extruded through heated nozzle; layer-by-layer deposition on heated bed. | PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU | Low cost; simple; wide material range | Visible layer lines; anisotropic strength |
| **SLA** | UV laser cures photopolymer resin point-by-point via photopolymerization. | Acrylic/epoxy resins, biocompatible resins | High resolution (25 μm); smooth surface | Resin toxicity; limited mechanical strength |
| **SLS** | $\text{CO}_2$ laser sinters polymer powder; no support structures needed. | Nylon PA12, PA11, TPU | Complex geometries; self-supporting; good toughness | Rough surface; powder handling issues |
| **DMLS** | Fiber laser fully melts metal powder under inert gas (Argon/Nitrogen). | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr, 316L SS, Inconel | Patient-specific metal implants; high strength | Expensive; requires post-machining; residual stresses |

*Examiner marks breakdown:* 1 mark for table structure, 1 mark per technology × 4 = 4, 1 mark for cross-comparison, 1 mark for medical-device relevance.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Build Time Calculation:**

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \frac{V}{R \cdot h} = \frac{72{,}000}{6 \cdot 0.15} = \frac{72{,}000}{0.9} = 80{,}000\ \text{s}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \frac{80{,}000}{3600} \approx 22.22\ \text{hours}
$$

Adding 30% travel-move correction factor $\xi = 1.3$:

$$
T_{\text{build,real}} = 22.22 \cdot 1.3 \approx 28.89\ \text{hours}
$$

**(ii) Number of Layers:**

$$
N_{\text{layers}} = \frac{H}{h} = \frac{30}{0.15} = 200\ \text{layers}
$$

**(iii) Surface Roughness:**

$$
R_a = k \cdot h = 0.4 \cdot 0.15 = 0.06\ \text{mm} = 60\ \mu\text{m}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Describe the **traditional manufacturing processes** used for fabricating assistive medical devices such as orthopedic implants and prosthetic components. Discuss CNC machining, injection molding, casting, and forging with relevant medical-device examples. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A patient-specific cranial implant is fabricated by DMLS from Ti-6Al-4V powder. The bounding block is $80\ \text{mm} \times 80\ \text{mm} \times 12\ \text{mm}$, and the actual implant volume is $V_{\text{part}} = 30{,}720\ \text{mm}^3$.
   (i) Calculate material utilization efficiency $\eta_{\text{AM}}$.
   (ii) If the same implant were CNC-machined from a titanium block with effective utilization $\eta_{\text{SM,real}} = 0.15$, what is the volume of titanium swarf (waste) generated?
   (iii) If titanium powder costs $\$300/\text{kg}$ and density $\rho = 4{,}430\ \text{kg/m}^3$, calculate the **material cost saving** by switching from CNC to DMLS (assume 100% DMLS powder is recycled, so effective $\eta_{\text{AM,real}} = 0.95$). *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**CNC Machining:** Multi-axis computer-controlled cutting removes material from a Ti-6Al-4V block to produce femoral stems, bone plates, and spinal cages. Tolerance $\pm\,0.025$ mm.

**Injection Molding:** Molten polymer (PP, PA) injected into a precision steel mold under 100–200 MPa pressure. Used for mass-produced prosthetic sockets and wheelchair components. Cycle time 15–90 s.

**Casting:** Molten Co-Cr alloy poured into investment molds (lost-wax process). Used for custom knee femoral components and dental crowns. Tolerances $\pm\,0.1$ mm.

**Forging:** Compressive shaping of solid titanium billet at 900°C. Produces grain-aligned high-strength femoral stems and acetabular cups. Highest fatigue strength among the four.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) AM Efficiency:**

$$
V_{\text{block}} = 80 \cdot 80 \cdot 12 = 76{,}800\ \text{mm}^3
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{AM}} = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{block}}} = \frac{30{,}720}{76{,}800} = 0.40
$$

**(ii) CNC Swarf Volume:**

$$
V_{\text{consumed,SM}} = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{\eta_{\text{SM,real}}} = \frac{30{,}720}{0.15} = 204{,}800\ \text{mm}^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{waste,SM}} = V_{\text{consumed,SM}} - V_{\text{part}} = 204{,}800 - 30{,}720 = 174{,}080\ \text{mm}^3
$$

**(iii) Material Cost Saving:**

Mass saved (volume × density):

$$
m_{\text{saved}} = V_{\text{waste,SM}} \cdot \rho = 174{,}080 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^3 \cdot 4{,}430\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

$$
m_{\text{saved}} = 7.71 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 \cdot 4{,}430 = 0.771\ \text{kg} \quad \text{(wait, recompute)}
$$

Correcting arithmetic:

$$
174{,}080\ \text{mm}^3 = 174{,}080 \cdot 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^3 = 1.7408 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
m_{\text{saved}} = 1.7408 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 \cdot 4{,}430\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 0.771\ \text{kg}
$$

Cost saved:

$$
\Delta C = m_{\text{saved}} \cdot c_{\text{Ti}} = 0.771\ \text{kg} \cdot \$300/\text{kg} = \$231.30
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Critical):**
> 1. **Unit conversion** — Students frequently mix mm³ and m³. Always convert mm³ to m³ by multiplying by $10^{-9}$.
> 2. **Sign convention** — DMLS with 95% recycling effectively means **5%** powder is consumed per build, not 100%. The $\eta_{\text{AM,real}} = 0.95$ refers to the fraction of feedstock that becomes part or is recoverable.
> 3. **Layer-bond assumption** — Do not assume isotropic mechanical properties; for load-bearing implants, post-machining of the build direction (Z-axis) is mandatory.
> 4. **Biocompatibility ≠ FDA cleared** — Many "biocompatible" filaments are not ISO 10993-5/-10 certified for **long-term implant** use.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **AM definition** (ISO/ASTM 52900:2021): Layer-by-layer material addition from a 3D model.
- [x] **Seven AM categories** (per ISO/ASTM): Vat Photopolymerization (SLA), Material Extrusion (FDM), Powder Bed Fusion (SLS/DMLS), Material Jetting (Polyjet), Binder Jetting, Sheet Lamination, Directed Energy Deposition.
- [x] **Build time formula**: $T_{\text{build}} = V / (R \cdot h)$.
- [x] **Layer count formula**: $N = H / h$.
- [x] **Surface roughness formula**: $R_a \approx k \cdot h$, with $k \approx 0.4$.
- [x] **CNC MRR formula**: $\text{MRR} = v \cdot f \cdot d$.
- [x] **CNC tolerance**: $\pm\,0.025$ mm; **DMLS tolerance**: $\pm\,0.05$–$0.1$ mm (post-machining needed).
- [x] **Key biocompatible materials**: Ti-6Al-4V, PEEK, PLA (temporary), Co-Cr, Hydroxyapatite.
