F(v) = F_{0}\,\frac{v_{\text{max}} - v}{v_{\text{max}} + k_{v}\,v}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify boundary conditions.**

- At $v = 0$: $F = F_{0}\,\dfrac{v_{\text{max}}}{v_{\text{max}}} = F_{0}$ ✓
- At $v = v_{\text{max}}$: $F = F_{0}\,\dfrac{0}{v_{\text{max}}(1+k_{v})} = 0$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering relevance:** This is the equation that OpenSim uses to compute muscle forces in musculoskeletal simulations — a critical tool when designing assistive devices that must coexist with (or replace) natural muscle function.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Gait Phase vs. Measurement Technique

| Gait Phase | % Cycle | Key Joint Event | Best Measurement Technique | Typical GRF Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Contact | 0 % | Heel-strike, knee flexes | Force plate + high-speed video | Sharp vertical peak onset |
| Loading Response | 0–12 % | Knee flexes to 15–20° | Force plate, EMG (quads) | Vertical GRF rises to 1.2 mg |
| Mid-Stance | 12–31 % | Trunk over stance foot | IMU, motion capture | Vertical GRF = body weight |
| Terminal Stance | 31–50 % | Heel-off, ankle plantarflex | Pressure insole, MoCap | Vertical GRF begins descent |
| Pre-Swing | 50–62 % | Toe-off | Force plate (last contact) | Vertical GRF = 0 at toe-off |
| Initial Swing | 62–75 % | Hip/knee flex, foot clear | IMU, electrogoniometer | No ground contact |
| Mid-Swing | 75–87 % | Swing leg passes stance | IMU | No ground contact |
| Terminal Swing | 87–100 % | Knee extends, leg decelerates | EMG (hamstrings) | No ground contact |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete Gait Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subject Walks Across Capture Volume] --> B[Sensor Suite]
    B --> B1[Optical Motion Capture - 8 to 12 IR Cameras]
    B --> B2[Force Plates - Embedded in Floor]
    B --> B3[IMU Array - 7 to 17 Body Segments]
    B --> B4[Surface EMG - 8 to 16 Channels]
    B --> B5[Pressure Insoles - Plantar Mapping]

    B1 --> C[Synchronised Data Acquisition Unit]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C

    C --> D[Pre-Processing Pipeline]
    D --> D1[Marker Labelling and Gap Filling]
    D --> D2[GRF Filtering - 4th Order Butterworth]
    D --> D3[IMU Sensor Fusion - Madgwick or Kalman]
    D --> D4[EMG Rectification and Envelope]

    D1 --> E[Biomechanical Model Engine]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> E1[Inverse Dynamics - Joint Moments]
    E --> E2[Kinematic Solver - Joint Angles]
    E --> E3[Musculoskeletal Model - OpenSim]
    E --> E4[Temporal-Spatial Parameter Extractor]

    E1 --> F[Visualisation and Reporting Module]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F

    F --> F1[Stick Figure Animation]
    F --> F2[GRF Vector Plot]
    F --> F3[Joint Angle Curves vs Gait Cycle]
    F --> F4[Clinical Report PDF]

    F1 --> G[Clinical Decision Support]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G

    G --> G1[Prescription of Orthosis]
    G --> G2[Design Input for Prosthesis]
    G --> G3[Exoskeleton Control Tuning]
    G --> G4[Rehabilitation Progress Tracking]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style D fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b
    style F fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c
    style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

### 4.2 Movement-Model Hierarchy (Conceptual Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Movement Models for Gait] --> B[Level 1 - Inverted Pendulum]
    A --> C[Level 2 - SLIP - Spring Loaded Inverted Pendulum]
    A --> D[Level 3 - 2D Multi-Link - 5 or 7 Segments]
    A --> E[Level 4 - 3D Full-Body - 12 to 17 Segments]
    A --> F[Level 5 - Musculoskeletal - Bones plus Muscles plus Tendons]

    B --> B1[Use Case - Real Time Exoskeleton Control]
    C --> C1[Use Case - Running Prosthesis Tuning]
    D --> D1[Use Case - Clinical Gait Lab Reports]
    E --> E1[Use Case - Sports Biomechanics Research]
    F --> F1[Use Case - Surgical Planning and Device Design]

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style F fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** As you move **left to right**, model complexity and biofidelity increase, but real-time computability decreases. An assistive device designer **chooses the level based on the application** — not the most complex model is always the best.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define gait cycle. List the eight subdivisions of the gait cycle with their percentage durations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Gait Cycle Definition (1 mark):** The gait cycle is the time interval between two successive occurrences of the same event in the same foot — conventionally measured from one *initial contact* (heel-strike) to the next *initial contact* of the same foot. It is the fundamental temporal unit used to describe human locomotion and is normalized to 100 % for cross-subject comparison.

**Eight Subdivisions (2 marks):**
1. Initial Contact — **0 %**
2. Loading Response — **0–12 %**
3. Mid-Stance — **12–31 %**
4. Terminal Stance — **31–50 %**
5. Pre-Swing — **50–62 %**
6. Initial Swing — **62–75 %**
7. Mid-Swing — **75–87 %**
8. Terminal Swing — **87–100 %**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid:** *"IL MT PS IS MS TS"* — read the first letters down: *I Love My Tea, Please Inject More Sugar Tonight.*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between kinematics and kinetics in the context of gait analysis. Give one instrument used to measure each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kinematics | Kinetics |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The study of motion *without* considering the forces that cause it (1 mark) | The study of the *forces and moments* that cause or result from motion (1 mark) |
| Quantities measured | Joint angles, angular velocities, linear accelerations, segment positions | Joint moments, joint powers, ground reaction forces |
| Typical instrument | Optical motion capture (Vicon) / IMU (1/2 mark) | Force plate / pressure insole (1/2 mark) |

**Key exam sentence:** *"Kinematics describes how the body moves; kinetics explains why it moves that way."*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Gait Analysis Techniques

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **different techniques used for gait analysis**, classifying them into observational, instrumented, and model-based methods. For each category, give at least **two specific examples** with their working principles.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **components of a typical instrumented 3D gait analysis laboratory**. Briefly explain the role of **force plates** and **inverse dynamics** in computing joint moments.

---

**Model Answer — (a) [7 marks]:**

Gait analysis techniques are classified into three broad categories:

**1. Observational Techniques (1 mark)**
- **Visual inspection** by a trained clinician, often using a standardized form (e.g., the Rancho Los Amigos Observational Gait Analysis form).
- **Slow-motion video review**, which can be replayed frame-by-frame to identify asymmetries or abnormal movements.
- *Limitation:* highly subjective, inter-rater variability is high.

**2. Instrumented Techniques (3 marks)**
- **Optical motion capture:** Multiple infra-red cameras detect retro-reflective markers placed on anatomical landmarks; triangulation yields 3D marker positions with sub-millimeter accuracy. (e.g., Vicon, Qualisys)
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs):** Each IMU contains a 3-axis gyroscope, accelerometer, and magnetometer. Sensor-fusion algorithms (Madgwick / Kalman) yield 3D orientation in real time. Wearable, ambulatory, low-cost.
- **Force plates:** A rigid plate instrumented with piezoelectric or strain-gauge load cells. Measures the three components of the Ground Reaction Force (GRF) — $F_{x}$, $F_{y}$, $F_{z}$ — and the Centre of Pressure (CoP).
- **Surface electromyography (sEMG):** Surface electrodes detect the electrical activity of superficial muscles. Used to assess activation timing, useful for diagnosing spasticity, dystonia, or co-contraction patterns.
- **Pressure insoles / pedography:** Capacitive sensor arrays inside the shoe; map plantar pressure distribution — critical for diabetic foot care and prosthetic socket design.

**3. Model-Based / Computational Techniques (2 marks)**
- **Inverse dynamics:** Given measured kinematics and external forces (GRF), computes the internal joint moments and powers using Newton-Euler equations applied segment-by-segment from distal to proximal.
- **Forward dynamics / simulation:** Given known muscle forces (from EMG-driven models), predicts the resulting motion. Used in OpenSim and AnyBody musculoskeletal simulations.
- **Hill-type muscle models:** Each muscle is represented by a contractile element in parallel with a passive elastic element and in series with a tendon spring.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern clinical gait analysis combines **all three** layers — observational for screening, instrumented for objective data, and model-based for clinical interpretation and device design.

---

**Model Answer — (b) [7 marks]:**

**Block Diagram of an Instrumented 3D Gait Lab (4 marks):**

The system architecture (already illustrated in Section 4.1) consists of:

1. **Capture volume** with embedded force plates flush with the floor.
2. **Camera array** (typically 8–12 IR cameras) surrounding the volume.
3. **Body-mounted IMUs and EMG electrodes** for additional data streams.
4. **Data acquisition (DAQ) unit** that synchronizes all sensors using a common hardware trigger (sub-millisecond jitter).
5. **Pre-processing pipeline** (marker labelling, Butterworth filtering at ~6 Hz for kinematics, ~20 Hz cut-off for force data, EMG rectification and envelope extraction).
6. **Biomechanical model engine** running inverse dynamics.
7. **Visualization and reporting module** (stick figure, GRF vector plot, joint-angle curves, clinical report PDF).

**Role of Force Plates in Inverse Dynamics (2 marks):**
Force plates supply the **external loads** ($F_{x}, F_{y}, F_{z}, M_{x}, M_{y}, M_{z}$) applied to the body. These are the only external forces acting on the lower limbs during stance (the upper body is free). In Newton's second law written for each segment, these external loads are the **known inputs** that allow the unknown internal joint moments to be solved.

**Why Inverse Dynamics is Necessary (1 mark):**
Internal joint moments cannot be measured directly by any non-invasive sensor. They are *inferred* by combining kinematic data (from cameras/IMUs), anthropometric data (segment masses and inertias), and force-plate data through the Newton-Euler equations. The output — joint moments, powers, and work — is the gold-standard basis for designing prostheses, orthoses, and exoskeleton controllers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Movement Modelling (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **inverted pendulum model** of human walking during the stance phase. Derive the equation for the **vertical ground reaction force** as a function of the leg angle $\theta$ and its derivatives.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **Spring-Loaded Inverted Pendulum (SLIP) model** and explain its relevance in the design of **running prostheses and lower-limb exoskeletons**.

---

**Model Answer — (a) [7 marks]:**

**The Inverted Pendulum Model (3 marks):**
The inverted pendulum model treats the stance leg as a **rigid, massless rod** of length $L$, pivoting about the foot, with the **body's centre of mass (CoM)** as a **point mass** $m$ at the top. The model applies during the **stance phase** of walking (one foot in contact with the ground). It explains the **vaulting motion** of the body over the support foot and is the simplest model that captures the double-hump ("M-shape") in the vertical GRF.

**Geometry (1 mark):**
The height of the CoM above the ground is:

$$
h(t) = L \cos\theta(t)
$$

**First and second derivatives (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{h} = -L \sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}
$$

$$
\ddot{h} = -L \cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}^{2} - L \sin\theta \cdot \ddot{\theta}
$$

**Newton's Second Law (1 mark):**
With $F_{v}$ as the vertical GRF:

$$
F_{v} - mg = m \ddot{h}
$$

**Resulting GRF equation (1 mark):**

$$
F_{v}(t) = m g - m L \cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}^{2} - m L \sin\theta \cdot \ddot{\theta}
$$

The first term is the static body weight; the second is the centripetal contribution (which **reduces** $F_{v}$ at mid-stance); the third is the tangential contribution.

---

**Model Answer — (b) [7 marks]:**

**SLIP Model Description (3 marks):**
The Spring-Loaded Inverted Pendulum (SLIP) model extends the inverted pendulum by replacing the **rigid leg** with a **massless, compliant spring** of stiffness $k$ and natural length $L_{0}$. The model has **three state variables** (CoM position $(x,y)$ and leg angle $\theta$) and **two control parameters** (spring stiffness $k$ and leg angle at touch-down $\theta_{0}$). It is the **canonical model of running gaits** in humans, birds, and robots.

The radial force produced by the spring is:

$$
F_{r} = -k\,(L - L_{0}) \quad \text{where} \quad L = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}
$$

**Why SLIP is Relevant to Running Prostheses (2 marks):**
- The human Achilles tendon + gastrocnemius–soleus complex behaves like a SLIP spring, storing and releasing elastic energy during the stance phase of running.
- A **running-specific prosthetic foot** (e.g., the Össur Cheetah, Ottobock Runner) is engineered to mimic the SLIP spring — typically using a carbon-fiber leaf-spring that deflects at heel-strike and recoils at toe-off, **returning 80–90 % of the stored elastic energy**.
- The required spring stiffness is computed from the runner's body mass and leg length:

$$
k \approx \frac{2.5\,mg}{0.08\,L}
$$

For a 70 kg runner with $L = 0.95$ m, $k \approx 22.6$ kN/m.

**Why SLIP is Relevant to Lower-Limb Exoskeletons (2 marks):**
- Exoskeletons must inject assistive torque **at the right phase of the gait cycle** — typically at push-off (terminal stance).
- The SLIP model predicts the optimal **push-off instant** (when the leg is at maximum compression) and the **required force magnitude** to sustain steady running.
- Real-time controllers in devices like **HULC, BLEEX, and ReWalk** use SLIP-based reduced-order models because the full multi-body dynamics is too expensive to run on embedded processors at 1 kHz.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the recursion order** in inverse dynamics — it is always **distal → proximal**, not proximal → distal. Wrong order = 0 marks for that step.
> 2. **Confusing kinematics with kinetics** — describing a *force* as a kinematic quantity or vice-versa is a common and costly error.
> 3. **Skipping the "why"** — KTU 2024 Scheme demands *Outcome-Based Education* answers. Always state *why* a technique is used, not just *what* it measures.
> 4. **Sign errors in GRF equation** — the centripetal term is **subtractive** during mid-stance; students often add it.
> 5. **Writing "inverted pendulum has stable equilibrium"** — it is inherently **unstable**; that's the entire point. Stabilizing it requires active control.
> 6. **Not normalizing the gait cycle to 100 %** — always present temporal events as percentages, not absolute times.
> 7. **Omitting units** in numerical answers (e.g., $k$ in N/m, not just "k").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gait cycle** = one full stride = 100 %; **stance 62 %**, **swing 38 %**, **double support 25 %**.
- **Cadence** ≈ 100–120 steps/min; **stride length** ≈ 1.2–1.6 m; **walking speed** ≈ 1.2–1.4 m/s.
- Gait analysis has **three layers**: observational → instrumented → model-based.
- **Motion capture** = gold standard for kinematics; **force plates** = gold standard for kinetics; both are required for inverse dynamics.
- **IMUs** are wearable and ambulatory — preferred for *out-of-lab* and *long-duration* monitoring.
- **sEMG** gives activation *timing*; it does **not** give muscle *force* directly.
- **Inverted pendulum** = simplest gait model; **SLIP** = extends it to running.
- The **GRF equation** $F_{v} = mg - mL\cos\theta\,\dot\theta^{2} - mL\sin\theta\,\ddot\theta$ is the most-tested equation from this topic — memorize it.
- **Hill's force-velocity** curve: $F = F_{0}\,\dfrac{v_{\text{max}} - v}{v_{\text{max}} + k_{v}\,v}$; $k_{v} \approx 0.18$.
- **Inverse dynamics** is solved **distal → proximal** using the Newton-Euler equations.
- **Froude number** $Fr = v^{2}/(gL)$ is dimensionless and used to scale gait across species and speeds.
- **Duty factor** $\beta = T_{\text{stance}}/T_{\text{cycle}}$; $\beta > 0.5$ ⇒ **walk**, $\beta < 0.5$ ⇒ **run**.
- **Spring stiffness benchmark** for human running: $k \approx 20\text{–}30$ kN/m.
- For KTU 2024: always tie your answer back to **assistive device design** (prosthesis, orthosis, exoskeleton, rehab robot) — that's the course context.
- **Exam tip:** Draw the block diagram of a gait lab; it is worth 4–5 marks almost every semester.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of Biomechanical Data in Device Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Integration of Biomechanical Data in Device Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Integration of Biomechanical Data in Device Design** is the systematic, iterative engineering process of acquiring, processing, analyzing, and translating quantitative human-movement and tissue-loading information (kinematic, kinetic, electrophysiological, and anthropometric data) into actionable design specifications, geometric constraints, material selection criteria, and performance benchmarks for assistive medical devices such as prostheses, orthoses, exoskeletons, wheelchairs, and rehabilitation aids. In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the process is governed by the convergence of three engineering streams: 1. **Biomechanics** (the science of biological systems under mechanical load) 2. **Instrumentation** (sensors, data acquisition, signal conditioning) 3. **Patient-Centric Design** (anthropometry, ergonomics, ISO 13485 design control) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The integration is *not* merely "using data" — it implies a closed-loop workflow where biomechanical measurements directly drive **design inputs**, **verification protocols**, and **validation outcomes** in compliance with regulatory standards (e.g., **ISO 22675** for prosthetic devices, **ISO 16840** for wheelchair seating). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bespoke Tailor" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — A Master Bespoke Tailor:** > Imagine a master tailor commissioned to craft a bespoke suit for an unusual client. The tailor does **not** start by cutting fabric. He first **measures** every curve (biomechanical data acquisition), studies how the client **moves and bends** (kinematic analysis), records the **pressure points** under daily activities (force/pressure mapping), and only then **drafts a pattern unique to that body** (device design specifications). > > In the same way, an assistive device engineer must measure the *patient's body and movement* *before* designing a prosthetic socket, an exoskeleton linkage, or a wheelchair cushion. A "one-size-fits-all" design without biomechanical data is equivalent to a tailor guessing measurements — it might *look* correct, but it will fail under real loading. ## 1.3 Why This Integration Matters | Without Biomechanical Data | With Biomechanical Data Integration | |---|---| | Empirical trial-and-error prototyping | Evidence-based design with reduced iterations | | Generic, off-the-shelf fit | Patient-specific customization | | Risk of tissue damage, pressure ulcers, joint stress | Load distribution optimized for safety | | Subjective performance evaluation | Quantitative verification & validation (V&V) | ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Values (Healthy Adult, 70 kg):** > - **Body Segment Inertial Parameters** are typically reported as fractions of total body mass and height (Dempster's coefficients). > - **Gait cadence:** ~**100–120 steps/min** for natural walking. > - **Vertical ground reaction force (GRF):** ~**1.0–1.2 × BW** during normal walking, peaking at **~1.5–2.0 × BW** during running. > - **Skin pressure tolerance threshold** for prolonged seating: **< 32 mmHg** (capillary occlusion pressure ≈ **4.27 kPa**). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Biomechanical Data Biomechanical data feeding assistive-device design is classified into **four primary families**: ### A. Kinematic Data (Motion, independent of force) - **Displacement** (m) - **Velocity** (m/s) - **Acceleration** (m/s²) - **Joint angles** (rad or °) - **Range of Motion (ROM)** — measured via goniometer or motion capture ### B. Kinetic Data (Forces and Moments) - **Ground Reaction Forces (GRF)** — measured by force plates - **Joint Reaction Forces (JRF)** - **External moments** (Nm) - **Internal muscle-tendon forces** (computed via inverse dynamics) - **Contact pressure** (Pa or mmHg) — measured by pressure mats ### C. Electrophysiological Data - **Surface Electromyography (sEMG)** — muscle activation timing & amplitude - **Electroencephalography (EEG)** — for brain–computer interface (BCI) controlled devices ### D. Anthropometric Data - **Static dimensions** (stature, segment lengths, circumferences) - **Dynamic dimensions** (functional reach, clearance envelopes during gait) - **Soft-tissue thickness** (relevant for socket/lining design) ## 2.2 The Biomechanical Data Integration Workflow > [!NOTE] > **Step-by-Step Logic** (the "Why" and "How"): 1. **Data Acquisition** — *Why?* You cannot design what you have not measured. *How?* Optical motion capture, IMU sensors, force plates, EMG electrodes, 3D body scanners. 2. **Signal Conditioning** — *Why?* Raw signals are noisy. *How?* Amplification, filtering (e.g., **4th-order Butterworth low-pass filter** with cut-off at **6–20 Hz** for gait kinematics). 3. **Data Processing & Normalization** — *Why?* To compare across patients and tasks. *How?* Time normalization (0–100% gait cycle), amplitude normalization (% Maximum Voluntary Contraction for EMG). 4. **Inverse Dynamics / Model Construction** — *Why?* To compute internal joint loads from external measurements. *How?* Newton–Euler equations using measured kinematics + GRF. 5. **Design Translation** — *Why?* To convert numbers into engineering drawings. *How?* Map joint ROM → device range of motion, GRF → structural load cases, pressure map → cushion geometry. 6. **Prototyping & Iterative Validation** — *Why?* First prototypes rarely fit perfectly. *How?* 3D printing, CAD-CAM, finite element re-analysis, and re-testing. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | Velocity from displacement | m/s | Kinematic analysis | | 2 | $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{d^{2}s}{dt^{2}}$ | Acceleration | m/s² | Impact loading, gait | | 3 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's Second Law | N | External force estimation | | 4 | $M = I \cdot \alpha$ | Rotational analog (moment of inertia × angular accel.) | N·m | Joint moment | | 5 | $\tau = I \cdot \alpha + \omega \times (I \cdot \omega)$ | General rotational equation (Euler) | N·m | Inverse dynamics | | 6 | $\text{GRF}_{vertical} = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.2 \cdot BW$ | Normal walking peak GRF | N (×BW) | Prosthesis loading | | 7 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Contact Area | Pa (N/m²) | Cushion/socket design | | 8 | $P_{capillary} \approx 32 \text{ mmHg} \approx 4.27 \text{ kPa}$ | Capillary occlusion pressure | mmHg, kPa | Pressure ulcer threshold | | 9 | $f_{sampling} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem | Hz | DAQ system design | | 10 | $BW_{subject} = m_{subject} \cdot g$ | Body Weight calculation | N | Load normalization | | 11 | $X_{segment} = c_{i} \cdot m_{body}$ | Dempster's inertial parameters (segment mass as fraction of body mass) | kg | Inverse dynamics input | | 12 | $\%MVC = \dfrac{EMG_{task}}{EMG_{max}} \times 100$ | EMG normalization | % | Prosthesis control | | 13 | $L_{scaled} = L_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{h_{subject}}{h_{ref}}\right)$ | Anthropometric scaling by stature | m | Population design | | 14 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending stress in a structural member | Pa | Frame design (e.g., wheelchair) | | 15 | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Tip deflection of cantilever | m | Deformation check | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Table Constraint Note:** The vertical bar symbol `|` was intentionally replaced with `BW` (Body Weight) in Row 6 to prevent breaking table parsing. In LaTeX-rendered answers, always write $\vert BW \vert$ or $1.0\text{--}1.2 \cdot BW$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Prosthetic Sockets:** Inverse-dynamics-derived residual-limb loading profiles dictate socket wall thickness and trim-line geometry. - **Powered Exoskeletons:** sEMG signals drive real-time actuator torque commands. - **Wheelchair Cushions:** Pressure-mapping data reveal high-risk ischial regions → drives contoured foam/cell geometry. - **Orthotic Braces:** Kinematic gait data identify pathological joint patterns → guides corrective joint-angle constraints. - **Rehabilitation Robotics:** Force-feedback data personalize therapy protocols (impedance/admittance control gains). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Joint Moment from Inverse Dynamics (Planar 3-Link Sagittal Model) This derivation is the **core analytical engine** that converts externally measured GRF + kinematics into internal joint moments — the *single most important* calculation in biomechanical device design. ### Setting Consider a sagittal-plane model of the lower limb during stance: **Foot → Shank → Thigh**, with the foot in contact with a force plate. The ankle, knee, and hip joints are frictionless pin joints. ### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of the Foot Segment The foot experiences: - **Ground Reaction Force** $\vec{F}_{GRF} = (F_{x}, F_{y})$ applied at the center of pressure (CoP). - **Ankle joint reaction force** $\vec{F}_{A} = (A_{x}, A_{y})$. - **Ankle joint moment** $M_A$. - **Gravitational force** $m_{foot} \cdot g$ acting at the foot's center of mass. - **Inertial force** $-m_{foot} \cdot \vec{a}_{CoM,foot}$ (Newton's second law, D'Alembert form). ### Step 2 — Newton–Euler Equations (Translation) Sum of forces in the sagittal plane equals mass × acceleration of the foot's center of mass: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum F_{x} &= F_{x,GRF} + A_{x} - m_{foot} \cdot a_{CoM,foot,x} = 0 \\ \sum F_{y} &= F_{y,GRF} + A_{y} - m_{foot} \cdot g - m_{foot} \cdot a_{CoM,foot,y} = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Textual interpretation:** The horizontal GRF and ankle shear must balance the foot's horizontal acceleration. The vertical GRF and ankle vertical reaction balance the foot's weight plus its vertical acceleration. ### Step 3 — Newton–Euler Equation (Rotation about the Ankle) Summing moments about the ankle joint (this is the **strategic choice** — it eliminates the unknown ankle joint force terms): $$ \begin{aligned} \sum M_{A} &= (F_{x,GRF} \cdot d_{y}) - (F_{y,GRF} \cdot d_{x}) + M_A - (m_{foot} \cdot g \cdot p_{x}) \\ &\quad - (m_{foot} \cdot a_{CoM,foot,x} \cdot p_{y}) + (m_{foot} \cdot a_{CoM,foot,y} \cdot p_{x}) - I_{foot} \cdot \alpha_{foot} = 0 \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $d_x, d_y$ = position of CoP relative to ankle - $p_x, p_y$ = position of foot's CoM relative to ankle - $I_{foot}$ = moment of inertia of the foot about its CoM - $\alpha_{foot}$ = angular acceleration of the foot segment ### Step 4 — Solve for the Ankle Moment Rearranging for $M_A$: $$ \begin{aligned} M_A = \; &(F_{y,GRF} \cdot d_{x}) - (F_{x,GRF} \cdot d_{y}) + (m_{foot} \cdot g \cdot p_{x}) \\ &+ (m_{foot} \cdot a_{CoM,foot,x} \cdot p_{y}) - (m_{foot} \cdot a_{CoM,foot,y} \cdot p_{x}) + I_{foot} \cdot \alpha_{foot} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The ankle moment depends on the GRF lever arm, the foot's weight, inertial terms, and its angular acceleration. This moment is what the **prosthetic foot-ankle assembly must resist** in normal use. ### Step 5 — Propagate Upward (Proximal-to-Distal Recursion) Once the foot's reaction at the ankle is known, the **shank** is analyzed using the ankle force/moment as the *known* input, the knee force/moment as the *unknown* output. This recursion is the foundation of software packages like **OpenSim** and **AnyBody**. $$ M_{K} = M_{A,\text{on shank}} + \text{(shank inertial + gravitational terms)} + \text{(knee GRF lever terms)} $$ ### Step 6 — Design Translation The computed joint moment time-histories are the **loading spectrum** for the assistive device: - Peak $M_{hip}$ during stair ascent → sets the **actuator torque rating** of a hip exoskeleton. - Peak $M_{knee}$ during squatting → sets the **structural strength** of a knee-ankle-foot orthosis (KAFO). - Cumulative moment impulse → informs **fatigue life** prediction. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Anthropometric Scaling (Stature-Based) When designing for a population, the device dimensions must scale across body sizes. The simplest geometric scaling is **stature-based linear scaling**: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{i,\text{predicted}} &= L_{i,\text{ref}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{h_{\text{subject}}}{h_{\text{ref}}}\right) \\ \text{or, more accurately (mass + stature):} \quad L_{i,\text{predicted}} &= L_{i,\text{ref}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{m_{\text{subject}}}{m_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \left(\dfrac{h_{\text{subject}}}{h_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{1/3} \end{aligned} $$ The second form (geometric similarity, exponent **1/3** for length from mass and stature) is more physiologically grounded, as it accounts for **3D geometric similarity** (volume $\propto$ mass, length $\propto$ volume$^{1/3}$). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility:** This derivation is the basis of **size grading** in mass-produced wheelchairs, crutches, and reach-extenders. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Distribution and Cushion Design A seated patient on a cushion experiences pressure $P(x, y, t)$ that must be **bounded** by the capillary occlusion pressure to prevent pressure ulcers: $$ P(x, y, t) = \dfrac{F_{vertical}(t)}{A_{contact}(x, y, t)} $$ To minimize peak pressure, the **contact area** must be maximized, i.e., the cushion should deform to **conformal contact**: $$ A_{contact} \geq \dfrac{F_{vertical,max}}{P_{tolerance}} = \dfrac{BW}{4.27 \text{ kPa}} $$ **Example numeric check** (for a **70 kg** subject, $BW = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7$ N): $$ \begin{aligned} A_{contact,min} &= \dfrac{686.7 \text{ N}}{4270 \text{ Pa}} \\ &= 0.1609 \text{ m}^{2} \\ &\approx 1609 \text{ cm}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ A cushion/contact patch smaller than ~**1609 cm²** will exceed the pressure threshold for a 70 kg patient — a critical design constraint. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Biomechanical Data Sources vs. Design Outputs | Biomechanical Data Source | Measurement Tool | Typical Range | Design Output Driven | |---|---|---|---| | Joint kinematics | Motion capture / IMU / goniometer | 0°–120° knee flexion | Device ROM, joint locks, alignment | | Ground reaction force | Force plate / instrumented treadmill | 0–2 × BW | Structural load cases, fatigue spectra | | Surface EMG | sEMG electrodes | 0–100 %MVC | Controller gains, actuator bandwidth | | Pressure mapping | Tekscan / Xsensor mat | 0–200 mmHg | Cushion geometry, socket trim lines | | 3D body scan | Structured-light scanner | mm-level precision | Custom-molded interfaces (sockets, seats) | | Spatiotemporal gait | Instrumented walkway | 0.5–2.0 m/s | Crutch/walker ergonomics, gait trainer timing | | Energy expenditure | Cosmed K5, indirect calorimetry | 2–10 METs | Battery sizing, actuator efficiency targets | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Workflow Diagram — Biomechanical Data → Assistive Device ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient and Task Definition] --> B[Biomechanical Data Acquisition] B --> B1[Kinematic Sensors
Motion Capture, IMU, Goniometer] B --> B2[Kinetic Sensors
Force Plate, Pressure Mat] B --> B3[Electrophysiology
sEMG, EEG] B --> B4[Anthropometry
3D Scan, Tape Measure] B1 --> C[Signal Conditioning and Filtering] B2 --> C B3 --> C B4 --> C C --> D[Data Normalization
Time, Amplitude, Body Weight] D --> E[Biomechanical Modelling
Inverse Dynamics, FEA, Musculoskeletal Simulation] E --> F[Design Specification Extraction
Loads, ROM, Pressure Maps, Geometry] F --> G[CAD and Prototyping
3D Print, CNC, CAD CAM] G --> H[Device Fabrication] H --> I[In Vivo Verification and Validation] I --> J{Meets Design Inputs?} J -->|No| K[Iterate Design Loop] K --> E J -->|Yes| L[Final Validation per ISO Standards
ISO 22675, ISO 16840, ISO 13485] L --> M[Clinical Deployment] M --> N[Post Market Surveillance
Feedback to Acquisition Stage] N --> B ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Fusion in a Smart Assistive Device ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENS[Sensor Layer] S1[IMU Module] S2[Force or Load Cell] S3[EMG Electrodes] S4[Pressure Sensor Array] end subgraph PROC[Signal Processing Layer] P1[Analog Front End
Amplifier and Filter] P2[ADC Conversion] P3[Digital Filtering
Butterworth, Kalman] P4[Feature Extraction
RMS, Mean, Frequency] end subgraph CTRL[Control and Decision Layer] C1[Microcontroller or DSP] C2[Intention Recognition
Classifier, Neural Net] C3[Actuator Command Generator] end subgraph ACT[Actuator Layer] A1[Motor Driver] A2[Electric Motor or Pneumatic Actuator] A3[Mechanical Linkage to Limb] end S1 --> P1 S2 --> P1 S3 --> P1 S4 --> P1 P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> C1 C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 A3 --> FEED[Feedback to Patient] FEED -.-> S1 FEED -.-> S2 ``` ## 4.3 Inverse-Dynamics Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD IN[Inputs: Kinematics, GRF, Anthropometry] --> MD[Model Definition
Segments, Joints, Mass Inertia] MD --> ID[Inverse Dynamics Engine
Newton Euler Recursion] ID --> OUT1[Internal Joint Moments] ID --> OUT2[Internal Joint Forces] ID --> OUT3[Muscle Tendon Forces via Static Optimization] OUT1 --> LD[Load Spectrum for Device Design] OUT2 --> LD OUT3 --> CTRL[Control Inputs for Powered Device] LD --> DV[Design Verification] CTRL --> RT[Real Time Control] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test direct recall and concise explanation. Answers should fit within **60–80 words**. ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** *Define the term "Integration of Biomechanical Data in Device Design." List any four categories of biomechanical data used in the design of assistive medical devices.* **Model Answer:** Integration of biomechanical data in device design is the systematic process of acquiring, processing, and translating human-motion and tissue-loading measurements into actionable design specifications, performance benchmarks, and validation criteria for assistive devices such as prostheses, orthoses, exoskeletons, and wheelchairs. The four primary categories of biomechanical data are: 1. **Kinematic data** (displacement, velocity, joint angles) 2. **Kinetic data** (GRF, joint forces and moments, pressure) 3. **Electrophysiological data** (sEMG, EEG) 4. **Anthropometric data** (stature, segment lengths, circumferences, soft-tissue thickness) *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing categories with brief examples)* --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** *What is the significance of the Nyquist sampling theorem in biomechanical data acquisition for assistive devices? State the minimum sampling frequency required to capture gait kinetics up to 20 Hz.* **Model Answer:** The **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem** states that to faithfully reconstruct a signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency component $f_{max}$ present in the signal. In biomechanical data acquisition, aliasing of gait signals (e.g., GRF, EMG) leads to incorrect amplitude and phase, producing erroneous joint moments and unsafe device designs. For $f_{max} = 20$ Hz: $$ f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \cdot 20 = 40 \text{ Hz} $$ In practice, gait labs sample at **100–1000 Hz** to allow margin and capture impulsive events. *(3 marks: 1.5 for theorem statement + 1 for calculation + 0.5 for practical note)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step workflow** for integrating biomechanical data into the design of an assistive device. Include in your answer the four major data-acquisition modalities and the role of signal conditioning. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **60 kg** subject walks across a force plate. The vertical GRF is measured as a function of time and the maximum value recorded is **720 N** at a specific instant. Compute (i) the peak GRF in **body weights (BW)**, and (ii) the minimum contact area required with a seating surface such that the interface pressure does not exceed the capillary occlusion pressure of **4.27 kPa**. Comment on the design implication. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A (a) The integration workflow is a **closed-loop, evidence-based** process: 1. **Patient & Task Definition:** Define the user's impairment (e.g., transfemoral amputee), intended activity (level walking, stair ascent), and the regulatory design class. 2. **Biomechanical Data Acquisition** using four modalities: - **Kinematic:** Optical motion capture (Vicon, OptiTrack) or IMU-based wearable sensors to capture joint angles, segmental velocities, and accelerations. - **Kinetic:** Force plate under the foot or instrumented treadmill to record 3D GRF and center of pressure. - **Electrophysiological:** sEMG on residual-limb muscles for above-knee prostheses; EEG for BCI-controlled devices. - **Anthropometric:** 3D laser/structured-light body scanning plus manual tape measurements. 3. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, anti-aliasing low-pass filtering (e.g., 4th-order Butterworth at 6–20 Hz for kinematics, 500 Hz for EMG), and analog-to-digital conversion at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. 4. **Data Processing:** Time normalization (0–100% gait cycle), amplitude normalization (BW, %MVC), outlier removal, and synchronization across sensors. 5. **Inverse Dynamics / Modelling:** Newton–Euler recursion or musculoskeletal simulation (OpenSim, AnyBody) to compute internal joint moments and forces. 6. **Design Translation:** Convert computed loads, ranges of motion, and pressure maps into CAD geometry, material selection, and structural sizing. 7. **Prototyping, V&V, and Iteration:** 3D print, instrument the prototype, re-test on the patient, iterate until the device meets ISO standards (e.g., ISO 22675). *(7 marks: 1 per major step + integrating statement)* #### Model Solution to Question A (b) **Given:** - Subject mass: $m = 60$ kg - Peak vertical GRF: $F_{GRF,peak} = 720$ N - Capillary occlusion pressure: $P_{tolerance} = 4.27$ kPa $= 4270$ Pa **Step 1 — Body Weight Calculation:** $$ BW = m \cdot g = 60 \times 9.81 = 588.6 \text{ N} $$ **Step 2 — Peak GRF in Body Weights:** $$ GRF_{peak} = \dfrac{720}{588.6} = 1.223 \; BW $$ This is consistent with **normal walking peak GRF** in the range 1.0–1.2 BW. **Step 3 — Minimum Contact Area:** $$ A_{contact,min} = \dfrac{F_{GRF,peak}}{P_{tolerance}} = \dfrac{720}{4270} = 0.1686 \text{ m}^{2} $$ Converting to cm²: $$ A_{contact,min} = 0.1686 \times 10^{4} = 1686 \text{ cm}^{2} $$ **Step 4 — Design Implication:** Any seating, prosthetic socket, or orthotic interface with a contact patch smaller than **1686 cm²** for this 60 kg subject will produce interface pressures exceeding the capillary occlusion threshold, risking **tissue ischemia and pressure ulcers**. The cushion/socket must therefore be **conformal** (maximizing contact area) and may require **load redistribution** through contoured foam, gel inserts, or air-cell technology. *(7 marks: 2 for BW + 1 for GRF/BW + 2 for area + 2 for design commentary)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the closed-loop workflow of biomechanical data integration in assistive device design. Highlight the role of **inverse dynamics** and **finite element analysis (FEA)** in this loop. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A lower-limb exoskeleton is to be designed for a 75 kg user. The hip joint moment during stair ascent is measured (via inverse dynamics) to be **85 Nm peak**. The exoskeleton actuator must share at least **40%** of this load. Compute the required **actuator torque rating** and discuss two design strategies to deliver this torque at the hip joint. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B (a) **Block Diagram Description (refer to Section 4.1 above for the full Mermaid schematic):** 1. **Patient + Task** → defines design intent. 2. **Biomechanical Data Acquisition** → kinematic, kinetic, EMG, anthropometric. 3. **Signal Conditioning + Normalization** → removes noise, aligns time, scales amplitude. 4. **Inverse Dynamics** → uses measured kinematics + GRF to compute internal joint moments and forces via Newton–Euler recursion. This is the **primary bridge** between *measurement* and *internal loading*. 5. **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** → takes the inverse-dynamics-computed loads as boundary conditions to compute **stress, strain, and deformation** in candidate device geometries and materials. 6. **Design Specification Extraction** → ROM, peak loads, fatigue spectra, pressure maps. 7. **CAD + Prototyping** → translates specifications into a physical device. 8. **Verification & Validation (V&V)** → instrumented bench test + in-vivo trial; results **loop back** to step 3 if the design fails. **Role of Inverse Dynamics:** It is the analytical engine that converts *external* measurements into *internal* loads that the device must withstand or assist. **Role of FEA:** It predicts the structural response of the device under those internal loads, enabling virtual prototyping and material optimization before physical fabrication. *(7 marks: 3 for block diagram + 2 for inverse dynamics role + 2 for FEA role)* #### Model Solution to Question B (b) **Given:** - User mass: $m = 75$ kg - Peak hip moment during stair ascent: $M_{hip,peak} = 85$ Nm - Actuator share required: 40% **Step 1 — Required Actuator Torque:** $$ \tau_{actuator} = 0.40 \times M_{hip,peak} = 0.40 \times 85 = 34 \text{ Nm} $$ **Step 2 — Apply a Safety Factor (typical for assistive devices: SF = 1.5–2.0):** $$ \tau_{rated} = SF \times \tau_{actuator} = 1.5 \times 34 = 51 \text{ Nm} $$ Thus, the actuator must be **rated for at least 51 Nm** continuous torque (and higher for peak/transient). **Step 3 — Design Strategies to Deliver the Torque:** 1. **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) with Harmonic Drive Gear:** A brushless DC motor coupled to a high-ratio harmonic-drive reducer (e.g., 100:1) plus a series spring. The spring provides **compliance and force control**, critical for safe human–robot interaction, while the gearbox delivers high torque in a compact package. This is the approach used in the BLEEX and many modern lower-limb exoskeletons. 2. **Pneumatic Muscle Actuator (PMA) / McKibben Artificial Muscle:** A contractile pneumatic bladder produces a pulling force analogous to a biological muscle. By selecting an appropriate bladder diameter and operating pressure (typically 4–8 bar), torques exceeding 50 Nm can be generated with inherent **back-drivability and high power-to-weight ratio**. Two PMAs arranged in **antagonistic pair** (flexor–extensor) at the hip enable biomimetic control. *(7 marks: 2 for calculation + 1 for safety factor + 2 + 2 for the two strategies with technical detail)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Biomechanical-Data-Integration Questions:** > 1. **Skipping the units in inverse-dynamics derivations** — Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing N·m, rad/s², or kg·m². > 2. **Confusing "kinetic" with "kinematic"** — *Kinematic* = motion-only; *Kinetic* = force/moment. This single error costs 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting the safety factor in actuator sizing** — Real devices are *never* sized at exactly the computed load; failure to add SF (typically 1.5–2.0) loses 1 mark. > 4. **Citing wrong pressure threshold for pressure ulcers** — Capillary occlusion is **~32 mmHg (4.27 kPa)**, *not* 100 mmHg or 200 mmHg. > 5. **Not drawing a block diagram when asked** — Even a simple hand-drawn flowchart scores full marks for "neat diagram" sub-parts. > 6. **Mere listing without "why"** — The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *understanding* under NEP 2020. Lists without justification earn partial credit only. > 7. **Mixing up Dempster's coefficients or anthropometric exponents** — Length scales as **mass$^{1/3}$ · stature$^{1/3}$** under geometric similarity, *not* mass or stature alone. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Integration of Biomechanical Data in Device Design:** - **Definition:** Closed-loop engineering process converting human movement/loading measurements into assistive-device specifications. - **Four Data Families:** Kinematic · Kinetic · Electrophysiological (sEMG/EEG) · Anthropometric. - **Key Sensors:** Motion capture, IMU, force plate, pressure mat, sEMG, 3D body scanner. - **Signal Processing Essentials:** Anti-aliasing filter, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist), 4th-order Butterworth, time & amplitude normalization. - **Core Equation — Inverse Dynamics:** Newton–Euler recursion from distal segment to proximal, summing moments about the joint of interest. - **Capillary Occlusion Pressure:** $\approx 4.27$ kPa $= 32$ mmHg (pressure-ulcer threshold). - **Normal Walking Peak GRF:** $\approx 1.0$–$1.2 \times BW$ (running peaks 1.5–2.0 × BW). - **Anthropometric Scaling:** Geometric similarity ⇒ length $\propto (\text{mass} \cdot \text{stature})^{1/3}$. - **EMG Normalization:** $\%MVC = EMG_{task} / EMG_{max} \times 100$. - **Pressure–Area Design Rule:** $A_{contact,min} = F_{peak} / P_{tolerance}$. - **Actuator Sizing Rule:** $\tau_{rated} = SF \times \tau_{required}$ with $SF = 1.5$–$2.0$. - **Workflow Loop:** Acquire → Condition → Normalize → Model (Inverse Dynamics + FEA) → Specify → Prototype → V&V → Iterate. - **Key Standards:** ISO 22675 (prosthetics), ISO 16840 (wheelchair seating), ISO 13485 (medical device QMS). - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Treating biomechanical data as *descriptive* rather than *prescriptive* for design inputs. - **One-Line Memory Hook:** *"Measure the body, model the load, then design the device — in that order, every time."* ---

topics.title: Integration of Sensors into Assistive Devices for Data Acquisition and Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integration of Sensors into Assistive Devices for Data Acquisition and Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensor Integration** in the context of Assistive Medical Devices (AMDs) is the systematic process of embedding one or more **physiological, biomechanical, or environmental transducers** within a rehabilitation or supportive apparatus to enable **real-time data acquisition (DAQ)**, **signal conditioning**, and **closed-loop biofeedback** to the end user or the caregiver.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PEBMT744*, the integration chain consists of four tightly coupled stages:

1. **Sensing Element** — Converts a biological variable (e.g., EMG, ECG, joint angle, grip force) into a measurable electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — Amplification, filtering, and isolation to make the raw signal MCU/DAQ-compatible.
3. **Data Acquisition Unit (DAU/DAQ)** — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC), sampling, storage, and digital processing.
4. **Feedback / Actuation Path** — Haptic, visual, auditory, or functional-electrical-stimulation (FES) output that closes the loop with the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *“An assistive device is said to be ‘integrated with sensors’ when the transducer output, after suitable signal conditioning, drives a data acquisition subsystem that processes the signal and delivers a meaningful physiological or biomechanical feedback to the user, completing the human-machine interface loop.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Nervous System

Imagine a person touching a hot kettle. The skin (the **sensor**) detects temperature; the nerve fibre (the **signal conditioner + DAQ**) carries an electrical impulse up the spinal cord; the brain (the **processor**) interprets it; and the hand muscle (the **actuator/feedback**) jerks away.

An assistive medical device mimics this exact reflex arc:

| Human Reflex Arc | Assistive Device Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Skin receptors | Biopotential / physical sensor |
| Nerve axon | Amplifier + cable / wireless link |
| Spinal cord / brain | Microcontroller / DSP |
| Muscle | Motor, buzzer, FES pad, or display |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a damaged nerve causes the brain to lose touch with reality, a poorly conditioned or improperly sampled sensor signal causes the assistive device to "misbehave" — producing tremor, false-trigger alarms, or unstable actuation. This is why sensor integration is the **most graded topic** in KTU university exams for this module.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following physical constants and standards are repeatedly cited in KTU question papers:

- **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$
- **ADC Resolution:** $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** $SNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ for an ideal N-bit ADC
- **EMG bandwidth:** **20 Hz – 500 Hz**
- **ECG bandwidth:** **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz**
- **EEG bandwidth:** **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz**
- **Standard supply rail for biomedical signal acquisition:** **$\pm 5\,\text{V}$** (battery-isolated, IEC 60601-1 compliant)
- **Leakage current limit (Type BF applied part):** **< 100 µA (DC)** and **< 100 µA (AC rms)** as per IEC 60601-1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Stage Integration Pipeline

**Stage 1 — Transduction Layer (The Sensor)**
- **Biopotential electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Capture ionic currents from the body and convert them to electron flow.
- **Strain gauges / FSR (Force-Sensitive Resistor):** Convert mechanical deformation into resistance change; used in prosthetic grip-pressure sensing.
- **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit — accelerometer + gyroscope):** Captures tilt, acceleration, and angular velocity for posture-correction exoskeletons.
- **Flex sensors / encoders:** Measure joint angle in upper-limb orthoses.
- **Pulse oximeter (SpO₂):** Optical PPG sensor using red (660 nm) and infrared (940 nm) LEDs.

**Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning**
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** Provides high CMRR (> 80 dB) and differential gain to reject mains 50 Hz hum.
- **Band-Pass Filter:** Restricts bandwidth to the biosignal of interest and rejects motion artefact and 1/f noise.
- **Isolation Amplifier (ADuM3151 / ISO124):** Mandatory barrier for patient safety; provides galvanic isolation > 5 kV.
- **Right-Leg Driver (RLD):** Actively drives the patient’s body potential to the common-mode voltage to improve CMRR.

**Stage 3 — Data Acquisition (DAQ)**
- **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Freezes the analog voltage for the ADC conversion window.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Successive-Approximation (SAR) for ECGs, Sigma-Delta for high-precision weight scales.
- **Microcontroller / DSP:** Performs feature extraction (RMS for EMG, R-peak detection for ECG).
- **Communication:** UART/BLE/Wi-Fi/LoRa for telemetry to the clinician’s dashboard.

**Stage 4 — Feedback / Actuation**
- **Visual:** LCD / OLED display, smartphone app.
- **Auditory:** Buzzer, beeps, voice prompts.
- **Haptic:** Vibration motor (ERM / LRA) in a cane for visually impaired users.
- **Functional:** FES pads stimulating muscle groups to restore grasp in a stroke survivor.

### 2.2 Why Each Stage is Non-Negotiable

- *Why an Instrumentation Amplifier?* — A standard op-amp has a low CMRR (~40 dB) and is unsuitable when 50 Hz mains interference dominates. An INA achieves **> 100 dB CMRR** and is the de-facto front-end for any biopotential device.
- *Why anti-aliasing?* — Real biomedical signals (e.g., EMG up to 500 Hz) contain harmonics; without a low-pass filter *before* the ADC, frequencies above $f_s/2$ fold back into the baseband as **aliasing artefacts** that mimic physiological events.
- *Why galvanic isolation?* — A defective amplifier could otherwise expose the patient to lethal mains voltage; isolation breaks the DC path.
- *Why feedback?* — Without feedback, an assistive device is **open-loop** (e.g., a static cane). Adding sensors + feedback makes it **closed-loop adaptive** (e.g., a smart cane that vibrates when an obstacle is detected via ultrasonic sensor).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use in AMDs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | $f_s$ = sample freq, $f_{max}$ = highest sig freq | EMG ($f_s \geq 1$ kHz), ECG ($f_s \geq 360$ Hz) |
| 2 | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | $V_{ref}$ ADC reference, $n$ = resolution | 12-bit ADC with 3.3 V ref ⇒ $V_{LSB} = 806\,\mu V$ |
| 3 | $SNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | $n$ = ADC bits (ideal) | Quality check of DAQ front-end |
| 4 | $G_{INA} = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$ | $R_G$ = gain resistor | 3-op-amp INA gain setting |
| 5 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | RC low-pass cutoff | Anti-aliasing / EMG high-cut filter |
| 6 | $e_{quant,max} = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$ | Max quantization error | DAQ resolution analysis |
| 7 | $\tau = RC$ | Time constant of filter | Step-response analysis |
| 8 | $H(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | Feedback-controlled prosthetic |
| 9 | $\varepsilon = e_{ref} - e_{fb}$ | Error signal in feedback loop | FES intensity control |
| 10 | $P_{out} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$ | Actuator power | FES / vibration motor sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In any 14-mark Part B question, examiners expect you to (a) state the formula, (b) state the assumption, (c) substitute, and (d) interpret the numerical result. A bare formula without numerical interpretation fetches only 2/7 marks per sub-part.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real Devices

- **Smart Prosthetic Hand (e.g., DEKA LUKE):** Uses surface EMG (sEMG) sensors on the residual limb; closed-loop force feedback via pressure sensors on the fingertips prevents crushing a held object.
- **Cochlear Implant:** Microphone → DSP → electrode array; essentially a sensor-DAQ-actuator chain for the auditory nerve.
- **Smart Cane (ultrasonic + IR):** Sensor detects obstacle → DAQ computes distance → haptic vibration motor alerts visually impaired user.
- **Wheelchair Fall-Prevention:** IMU on the seat → tilt-angle DAQ → automatic brake engagement when $\theta > 20^\circ$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem (Algebraic Form)

We start from the trigonometric form of a band-limited signal:

$$x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_{max} t)$$

Multiplying by an impulse train $p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT_s)$ produces a sampled sequence $x_s(t)$.

By Fourier theory, the spectrum of the sampled signal is:

$$X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - n f_s)$$

This means the original spectrum $X(f)$ is replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s$.

**Step 1 — Condition to avoid spectral overlap:**

To prevent replicas from overlapping, the separation between adjacent replicas must satisfy:

$$f_s - f_{max} \geq f_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the inequality:**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Define the minimum sampling rate (Nyquist rate):**

$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering implication for an EMG signal ($f_{max} = 500\,\text{Hz}$):**

$$f_s = 2 \times 500 = 1000\,\text{Hz}$$

In practice, KTU-recommended oversampling factor is **2.5× to 5×**, so $f_s = 2.5\,\text{kHz}$ is commonly used.

> [!NOTE]
> A common student error is to *confuse* $f_{Nyquist}$ (twice the signal frequency) with $f_s$ (the actual sampling frequency). $f_s$ is what your ADC *actually* runs at; $f_{Nyquist}$ is the *minimum acceptable* value of $f_s$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantization Error of an Ideal N-bit ADC

Let the ADC input range be $0$ to $V_{ref}$ and resolution be $n$ bits.

**Step 1 — Total number of discrete output codes:**

$$N_{codes} = 2^n$$

**Step 2 — Width of one Least Significant Bit (LSB):**

$$V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Quantization error is uniformly distributed in the range $\left[ -\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2},\,+\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} \right]$:**

$$e_{quant} \in \left[ -\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2},\,+\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} \right]$$

**Step 4 — RMS value of uniform quantisation noise:**

$$e_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}$$

**Step 5 — SNR for a full-scale sinusoid (peak = $V_{ref}/2$):**

The signal RMS is:

$$x_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

Substituting:

$$SNR = \dfrac{x_{rms}^2}{e_{rms}^2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_{ref}^2}{8}}{\dfrac{V_{LSB}^2}{12}}$$

Replace $V_{LSB}$:

$$SNR = \dfrac{12 \times 2^{2n}}{8} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels:**

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 n \log_{10}(2)$$

Using $\log_{10}(1.5) = 0.1761$ and $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02$:

$$SNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n$$

> This matches the canonical formula used in KTU derivations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Sensor-Driven FES Prosthesis

Consider a functional-electrical-stimulation (FES) loop with forward gain $G(s)$ (controller + muscle) and feedback $H(s)$ (force sensor).

**Step 1 — Define the error signal:**

$$\varepsilon(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$$

**Step 2 — Output in terms of error and forward path:**

$$Y(s) = G(s)\,\varepsilon(s)$$

**Step 3 — Feedback from sensor:**

$$Y_{fb}(s) = H(s)\,Y(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\varepsilon = R - H Y$ into the output equation:**

$$Y(s) = G(s)\bigl[R(s) - H(s) Y(s)\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect $Y(s)$ terms:**

$$Y(s) = G(s)R(s) - G(s)H(s)Y(s)$$

$$Y(s)\bigl[1 + G(s)H(s)\bigr] = G(s)R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$T(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Step 7 — Special case — Unity feedback ($H(s) = 1$):**

$$T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

This is the most commonly tested scenario for FES intensity control in KTU exams.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Biosignal Acquisition Parameters

| Signal | Amplitude (typical) | Bandwidth | Recommended ADC | Min $f_s$ | Sensor Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECG | 0.5 – 5 mV | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 12-bit SAR | 360 Hz | Ag/AgCl electrodes |
| EMG | 50 µV – 5 mV | 20 – 500 Hz | 12/16-bit SAR | 1 kHz | Surface / needle electrode |
| EEG | 5 – 300 µV | 0.5 – 100 Hz | 16/24-bit Σ-Δ | 250 Hz | Ag/AgCl scalp electrode |
| Gait IMU | $\pm 16\,g$ accel | 0 – 50 Hz | 14-bit MEMS | 100 Hz | MPU-6050 / MPU-9250 |
| Grip force | 0 – 200 N | DC – 20 Hz | 10/12-bit | 50 Hz | FSR / load cell |
| SpO₂ | 0 – 100 % | 0.5 – 5 Hz (pulse) | 16-bit | 100 Hz | Photodiode + LED |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End Sensor Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BODY[Patient Body] -->|Ionic potential| ELEC[Electrodes and Physical Sensor]
    ELEC -->|Microvolt level| SC[Signal Conditioning INA and Filter]
    SC -->|Anti-aliased analog| ADC[ADC and Sample Hold]
    ADC -->|Digital samples| MCU[Microcontroller and DSP]
    MCU -->|Feature extraction| FB1[Visual Feedback Display]
    MCU -->|Threshold logic| FB2[Auditory Buzzer]
    MCU -->|Control command| ACT[FES or Vibration Motor Actuator]
    ACT -->|Restored function| BODY
    MCU -.->|BLE or Wi-Fi| CLD[Clinician Cloud Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram follows the classical "sense → condition → acquire → act" reflex arc, with the optional telemetry path drawn as a dotted line to indicate non-critical redundancy.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Detailed Signal Conditioning Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Biopotential Input Differential] --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100 to 1000]
    IA --> HPF[High Pass Filter 0.05 Hz to 20 Hz]
    HPF --> LPF[Low Pass Filter 150 Hz to 500 Hz]
    LPF --> ISO[Isolation Amplifier 5 kV Barrier]
    ISO --> RLD[Right Leg Driver Feedback]
    IA -.->|Common mode signal| RLD
    ISO --> ADCIN[ADC Input Channel]
    ADCIN --> SAMPLE[S and H Circuit]
    SAMPLE --> ADCCORE[12 or 16 bit ADC]
    ADCCORE --> DIG[Digital Code to MCU]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Closed-Loop Biofeedback Control for a FES Hand

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Desired Grip Strength Reference] -->|Subtract| SUM[Error Summation Node]
    FS[Force Sensor on Fingertip] -->|Actual force| SUM
    SUM -->|Error epsilon| CTRL[PID Controller G of s]
    CTRL -->|PWM drive| FES[FES Electrode Pair on Forearm]
    FES -->|Muscle contraction| HAND[Prosthetic Hand]
    HAND -->|Physical force| FS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The loop is *closed* because the **Force Sensor** reads the same physical variable that the **FES** drives. Any mismatch (ε ≠ 0) is amplified by the PID and corrects muscle activation within milliseconds.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Smart Cane (Visual Impairment Use Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USS[Ultrasonic Sensor HC SR04] -->|Distance pulse| MCU1[ESP32 Microcontroller]
    IRS[Infrared Obstacle Sensor] -->|Digital warn| MCU1
    IMUS[Tilt IMU MPU6050] -->|Fall risk angle| MCU1
    MCU1 -->|Threshold check| VIB[Eccentric Rotating Mass Vibration Motor]
    MCU1 -->|Audio cue| BUZ[Buzzer and Headphone]
    MCU1 -->|BLE link| APP[Smartphone Navigation App]
```

> This block-level architecture maps to a *physical-world drawing* (cane with sensors mounted near the grip and tip) and avoids attempting un-renderable 3-D graphics inside Mermaid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define an **instrumentation amplifier** and state **two reasons** why it is preferred over a single-op-amp difference amplifier in front-end ECG acquisition.

**Model Answer:**

An **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** is a precision differential amplifier with three op-amps offering very high input impedance, low noise, and adjustable gain via a single external resistor.

Two reasons it is preferred in ECG acquisition:
1. **High CMRR (> 80 dB):** Rejects 50 Hz mains interference coupled to the body, which a simple op-amp difference stage cannot adequately cancel.
2. **High input impedance on both inputs (GΩ range):** Prevents loading of the high-impedance skin-electrode interface, preserving signal fidelity.
3. *Bonus point:* Single-resistor gain setting keeps the patient-side circuit symmetric, improving common-mode rejection.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
What is the **minimum sampling frequency** required to digitize an EMG signal whose highest meaningful frequency is 450 Hz, assuming ideal anti-aliasing? State the theorem used.

**Model Answer:**

**Theorem Used:** Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem.

**Minimum sampling frequency:**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 450 = 900\,\text{Hz}$$

Therefore $f_{Nyquist} = \mathbf{900\,Hz}$. In practice, a $2.5\times$ oversampling factor would suggest $f_s = 2.25\,\text{kHz}$ for safer reconstruction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not omit units** in final numerical answers — even if the formula is correct, a quantity without units is docked **0.5 – 1 mark**.
> 2. **Do not confuse $f_s$ with $f_{Nyquist}$.** Examiners penalise this 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Always state the assumption** of an *ideal* ADC when using the $6.02n + 1.76$ formula; otherwise marks are deducted for missing justification.
> 4. **In derivation questions, do not jump steps** — examiners allocate 1 mark per logical transition.
> 5. **For feedback questions, draw the closed-loop block diagram** — failure to do so costs 2 marks.

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **four-stage architecture** of a sensor-integrated assistive device. Identify one example device for each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12-bit ADC with reference voltage $3.3\,\text{V}$ is used to digitize an ECG signal of peak amplitude 3 mV after a gain of 1000. Compute:
   (i) the LSB voltage,
   (ii) the peak-to-peak quantisation error,
   (iii) the SNR in dB assuming an ideal converter.

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four-stage architecture is:

1. **Sensing Element** — *Example:* Ag/AgCl electrode pair captures cardiac ionic potentials.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — *Example:* INA (e.g., AD620) with gain 1000, followed by a 0.05 – 150 Hz band-pass filter.
3. **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** — *Example:* 12-bit SAR ADC in an STM32 microcontroller sampling at 360 Hz.
4. **Feedback / Actuation** — *Example:* Visual LCD display showing heart rate; auditory alarm triggered when BPM > 120.

**Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 of these notes).** (Award full 7 marks for stage names, examples, and diagram.)

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) LSB voltage:**

$$V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{3.3}{4095} = 8.06 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V} \approx 806\,\mu\text{V}$$

**(ii) Peak-to-peak quantisation error:**

The amplified ECG peak is $3\,\text{mV} \times 1000 = 3\,\text{V}$, which is below the $3.3\,\text{V}$ rail, so no clipping. Maximum quantisation error:

$$e_{p-p} = V_{LSB} = 806\,\mu\text{V}$$

**(iii) SNR (ideal):**

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = \mathbf{74.00\,dB}$$

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** of a sensor-driven FES assistive system with forward gain $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$. State the special case when $H(s) = 1$ and explain its significance in a prosthetic hand.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An assistive knee-orthosis uses an MPU-6050 IMU whose output is sampled by a 14-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 2.5\,\text{V}$.
   (i) Find the smallest tilt angle (in degrees) that produces a measurable code change, given the sensor sensitivity is $16384\,\text{LSB/g}$ and gravity dominates.
   (ii) If the tilt-sampling rate is 100 Hz, what is the highest frequency component that can be reconstructed without aliasing?
   (iii) Comment on the suitability of this $f_s$ for a human walking cycle (~1.6 Hz fundamental).

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The derivation proceeds as in Section 3.3 of these notes. Final result:

$$T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Special case $H(s) = 1$:** $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$.

**Significance:** In a prosthetic hand, the sensor (force / position) directly feeds back the achieved grip. With unity feedback, the controller simply minimises the error between desired and actual grip, ensuring precise object handling without crushing — a critical safety feature.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Smallest measurable code change:**

1 LSB of the IMU = $1 / 16384$ g per code step. Therefore:

$$\Delta a = \dfrac{9.81}{16384} = 5.99 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m/s}^2 \approx 0.0006\,\text{m/s}^2$$

The corresponding tilt angle for small angles ($\sin\theta \approx \theta$):

$$\theta_{LSB} = \dfrac{0.0006}{9.81} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.0035^\circ$$

So the **resolution is ~0.0035°**, which is excellent for a knee orthosis.

**(ii) Highest reconstructable frequency:**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{f_s}{2} = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50\,\text{Hz}$$

**(iii) Suitability for walking:**

The fundamental gait frequency is ~1.6 Hz, with harmonics typically below 10 Hz. A 50 Hz Nyquist limit is **6× higher than the highest meaningful gait harmonic** — fully adequate. The choice of $f_s = 100\,\text{Hz}$ is *not wasteful* because it provides a comfortable margin against motion artefact and is within the bandwidth of a standard BLE link.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sensor integration = Transduction + Conditioning + DAQ + Feedback.** This is the canonical 4-stage chain examiners expect.
- **Nyquist rate is $2 \times$ the highest signal frequency**, not the same as the *actual* sampling rate.
- **ADC LSB formula:** $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$. Always state both **the value AND its units** (typically µV or mV).
- **SNR formula $6.02n + 1.76$** applies **only for an ideal N-bit ADC** sampling a full-scale sinusoid.
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** is mandatory for any biopotential front-end — gives high CMRR and high input impedance.
- **Right-Leg Driver (RLD)** actively cancels the body's common-mode voltage and boosts CMRR by another 20–30 dB.
- **Galvanic isolation** is non-negotiable for patient safety (IEC 60601-1).
- **Closed-loop feedback** turns a passive aid (open-loop) into a smart, adaptive one (closed-loop). Draw the block diagram whenever asked.
- **Common biosignal bandwidths to memorise:** ECG 0.05–150 Hz, EMG 20–500 Hz, EEG 0.5–100 Hz.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** must be placed *before* the ADC, not after.
- **Closed-loop transfer function:** $T(s) = G(s) / (1 + G(s)H(s))$.
- **Examiner pet-peeves to avoid:** missing units, confused Nyquist vs sample rate, no block diagram, skipping derivation steps, forgetting the "ideal" assumption in SNR calculations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Revision Hook:** *Sensor → INA → Filter → ISO → ADC → MCU → Actuator → User → (loop back to Sensor).* If you remember this reflex arc, you can answer 80% of any Part B question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Processing and Signal Interpretation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Data Processing and Signal Interpretation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Processing and Signal Interpretation** refers to the systematic acquisition, conditioning, digitization, transformation, analysis, and translation of raw physiological signals (such as ECG, EMG, EEG, EOG, GSR, SpO₂, body temperature, and bio-impedance) generated by a patient or a user of an assistive device into clinically meaningful, quantifiable, and actionable information that can be displayed, stored, transmitted, or used as a control input for a therapeutic/rehabilitative response. In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices (PEBMT744)**, signal interpretation is the final cognitive layer where the processed numerical data is converted into a **decision variable** — for example, detecting a fall, classifying a gait phase, identifying a seizure onset, decoding a user intent (blink, sip, breath), or activating a neuroprosthetic actuator. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Module 2 forms the bridge between the *sensors* (Module 1) and the *actuators / feedback systems* (Module 3/4). Without robust data processing, even the most expensive biomedical sensor becomes useless noise. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Foreign Correspondent" Imagine a war correspondent stationed in a noisy battlefield. He hears a faint, distorted radio transmission in a foreign language. Before he can broadcast it to the world, three things must happen: 1. **Cleaning the Audio (Signal Conditioning):** He filters out the explosions, static, and wind — analogous to **amplification and noise filtering** of the raw bio-signal. 2. **Translating the Words (Feature Extraction & Processing):** He converts the foreign tongue into his native language — analogous to **digitization, feature extraction, and algorithmic classification** of the cleaned signal. 3. **Delivering the Headline (Interpretation & Decision):** He summarizes the message into a single, meaningful sentence that the audience understands — analogous to **signal interpretation**, where complex waveforms are reduced to a clinical decision (e.g., "Bradycardia detected" or "Fall imminent"). > The "correspondent" is the **microprocessor / DSP chip**, the "battlefield" is the human body, and the "foreign language" is the physiological signal. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Examiner's Vocabulary) - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** Number of samples taken per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. - **Resolution (N-bit):** The number of discrete levels an ADC can output, governed by $2^N$ where **N = 8, 10, 12, 16 bits** are typical in medical devices. - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Expressed in **decibels (dB)**; biomedical devices typically require **> 60 dB**. - **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** Expressed in **dB**; bio-amplifiers demand **> 80 dB**. - **Heart Rate Range:** **30 – 220 bpm** (clinical standard). - **EEG Bandwidth:** **0.5 – 100 Hz** (clinical standard). - **EMG Bandwidth:** **20 – 500 Hz** (clinical standard). - **ECG Bandwidth:** **0.05 – 150 Hz** (clinical standard, diagnostic). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Stage Signal Processing Pipeline Every biomedical signal chain in an assistive device follows a deterministic pipeline. KTU examiners frequently test the *order* and *purpose* of these stages. ### Stage 1: Signal Acquisition - The biological signal is transduced by an electrode/sensor (e.g., Ag/AgCl electrode for ECG). - The output is a **low-amplitude (µV–mV)**, **high-impedance**, and **noise-contaminated** voltage. - **Why this step?** The human body is a hostile electrical environment (50/60 Hz mains interference, motion artifacts, electrochemical half-cell potentials). ### Stage 2: Signal Conditioning (Pre-processing) Four sub-operations occur here: - **Amplification** — usually via an **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** with high CMRR. - **Filtering** — **band-pass filtering** to isolate the frequency band of interest. - **Isolation** — optical or transformer-based isolation to protect the patient from leakage currents (per **IEC 60601-1** safety standard). - **Level Shifting** — raising the signal baseline to match the ADC input range (0 – 3.3 V or 0 – 5 V). ### Stage 3: Digitization (Analog-to-Digital Conversion) - An **ADC** samples the continuous signal at $f_s$ and quantizes it into $2^N$ discrete levels. - Two governing parameters: **Sampling Rate** and **Quantization Bit Depth**. ### Stage 4: Digital Processing - Performed by a **DSP processor / microcontroller / FPGA**. - Includes digital filtering (FIR/IIR), **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**, wavelet decomposition, feature extraction, and machine-learning-based classification. ### Stage 5: Interpretation & Decision - The processed features are mapped to a clinical or assistive action. - Example: RMS value of EMG $\rightarrow$ threshold crossing $\rightarrow$ activation of a prosthetic hand motor. ## 2.2 The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem (Foundational) A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ band-limited to a maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples $x[n]$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ If $f_s < 2 \cdot f_{max}$, the phenomenon of **aliasing** occurs, causing high-frequency content to falsely appear as low-frequency content — a catastrophic error in ECG/EEG diagnostics. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem | Hz | | 2 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ | Quantization Step Size (Resolution) | Volts/level | | 3 | $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | Theoretical SNR of an ideal N-bit ADC | dB | | 4 | $\text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (voltage form) | dB | | 5 | $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | dB | | 6 | $H(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | First-Order Low-Pass Filter Transfer Function | dimensionless | | 7 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Cut-off Frequency of RC Filter | Hz | | 8 | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) | complex | | 9 | $P_{signal} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2$ | Average Signal Power | W or V² | | 10 | $f_{alias} = \vert f - f_s \cdot \text{round}(f/f_s)\vert$ | Alias Frequency Formula | Hz | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Always quote the sampling theorem as **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** in exams. Calling it just "Nyquist Theorem" loses a half-mark.
