Use `\vert` in tables (not the bare pipe character) to avoid markdown table corruption. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pacemakers & ICDs** rely on real-time R-peak detection (Pan-Tompkins algorithm) — a direct application of Stage 4 and Stage 5. - **Cochlear Implants** decompose audio via **band-pass filter banks** (Stage 4) and stimulate auditory nerves. - **Prosthetic Limbs** decode surface EMG (sEMG) using **RMS, MAV, and Zero-Crossing** features for gesture classification. - **Wheelchair Control via EEG** uses **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** features in the µ-rhythm (8–12 Hz) band to infer motor imagery intent. - **Sleep Apnea Monitors** apply **autoregressive (AR) modeling** on SpO₂ signals to detect desaturation events. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem (Frequency-Domain Justification) **Starting Premise:** A real-world signal $x(t)$ is *band-limited*, meaning its Fourier Transform $X(f)$ is zero for all $\vert f\vert > f_{max}$. **Step 1 — Sampling as Impulse Modulation:** Sampling $x(t)$ at intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$ is mathematically equivalent to multiplying $x(t)$ by a Dirac comb $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$. $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \delta(t - nT_s)$$ **Step 2 — Convolution in the Frequency Domain:** By the duality of multiplication in time and convolution in frequency: $$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$ This means the sampled spectrum is an **infinite, periodic replica** of the original spectrum, centered at multiples of $f_s$. **Step 3 — The Reconstruction Condition:** The replicas must not overlap, otherwise information is lost permanently (aliasing). The non-overlap condition is: $$\frac{f_s}{2} \geq f_{max}$$ **Step 4 — Final Form:** $$\boxed{\,f_s \geq 2 f_{max}\,}$$ The minimum rate $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantization Step Size and Resolution **Step 1 — Define the Full-Scale Range:** Let $V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min}$ be the ADC input voltage range (e.g., 0 V to 3.3 V). **Step 2 — Number of Discrete Levels:** An N-bit ADC produces: $$L = 2^N \quad \text{discrete levels}$$ **Step 3 — Step Size (Quantum):** The voltage difference between two adjacent codes is the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** voltage: $$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$ **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Suppose a 12-bit ADC samples an ECG signal with $V_{FS} = 5\,\text{V}$. - Total levels: $L = 2^{12} = 4096$ - Step size: $$Q = \frac{5\,\text{V}}{4096} = 1.2207\,\text{mV/LSB}$$ This means the ADC can detect a minimum voltage change of **1.22 mV** — adequate for QRS complex detection in ECG. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SQNR) of an N-bit ADC **Step 1 — Quantization Noise as a Uniform Random Variable:** Quantization error $e_q = x - x_q$ is modeled as uniform between $-Q/2$ and $+Q/2$. **Step 2 — Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power:** $$P_{noise} = \frac{1}{Q} \int_{-Q/2}^{Q/2} e^2 \, de = \frac{Q^2}{12}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum Signal Power (Full-Scale Sinusoid):** For a sinusoid of peak-to-peak amplitude $V_{FS}$ (i.e., peak $V_{FS}/2$): $$P_{signal} = \frac{(V_{FS}/2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$ **Step 4 — Substituting $Q = V_{FS}/2^N$:** $$P_{noise} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}$$ **Step 5 — Form the Ratio:** $$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2/8}{V_{FS}^2/(12 \cdot 2^{2N})} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to Decibels:** $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20N \log_{10}(2)$$ Since $10\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$ and $20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$: $$\boxed{\,\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76\,\text{ dB}\,}$$ **Step 7 — Practical Interpretation:** For a 16-bit ADC: $\text{SQNR} = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB. This is why high-end ECG and EEG machines use 16-bit or 24-bit ADCs. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cut-off Frequency of a First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter **Step 1 — Circuit Equation (KVL):** For an RC series circuit with input $V_{in}$ across the series combination and output $V_{out}$ across the capacitor: $$V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t) - i(t) R \quad \text{where} \quad i(t) = C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$ **Step 2 — Form the Differential Equation:** $$RC \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out} = V_{in}$$ **Step 3 — Take the Laplace Transform:** $$RC \cdot s \cdot V_{out}(s) + V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s)$$ **Step 4 — Transfer Function:** $$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{RC \cdot s + 1}$$ **Step 5 — Frequency Response (substitute $s = j\omega$):** $$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Step 6 — Magnitude Response:** $$\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Define Cut-off Frequency ($-3$ dB Point):** At cut-off, $\vert H\vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$: $$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Sampling Rates for Major Bio-Signals | Bio-Signal | Bandwidth ($f_{max}$) | Nyquist Rate ($2 f_{max}$) | Recommended $f_s$ in Practice | Reason for Oversampling | |---|---|---|---|---| | ECG | 150 Hz | 300 Hz | **500 – 1000 Hz** | R-peak sharpness, pacemaker timing | | EEG | 100 Hz | 200 Hz | **256 – 512 Hz** | Capturing sleep spindles, µ-rhythms | | EMG | 500 Hz | 1000 Hz | **1000 – 2000 Hz** | Motor unit action potential fidelity | | EOG | 10 Hz | 20 Hz | **50 – 100 Hz** | Slow eye movements | | Body Temp | 0.1 Hz | 0.2 Hz | **1 – 10 Hz** | Very slow drift dominant | | Phonocardiogram (PCG) | 2000 Hz | 4000 Hz | **8000 – 44100 Hz** | Heart sound S3, murmurs | | Blood Pressure (invasive) | 50 Hz | 100 Hz | **200 – 500 Hz** | Dicrotic notch detection | > [!NOTE] > **Practical rule:** Engineers sample at **3× to 5×** the Nyquist rate to provide a *guard band* for the anti-aliasing filter roll-off. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End Biomedical Signal Processing Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BIOLOGICAL SOURCE
Heart, Brain, Muscle] --> B[TRANSDUCER / ELECTRODE
Ag-AgCl, PPG, Thermistor] B --> C[PRE-AMPLIFIER
Instrumentation Amplifier] C --> D[ANALOG FILTER
Band-pass 0.05 to 150 Hz] D --> E[ISOLATION STAGE
Optical Coupler IEC 60601] E --> F[ADC
12 to 16 bit Resolution] F --> G[DSP MICROPROCESSOR
FFT, FIR, Feature Extraction] G --> H{INTERPRETATION ENGINE
Threshold / ML Classifier} H --> I[DECISION OUTPUT
Alarm, Actuator, Display] H --> J[STORAGE / WIRELESS TX
SD Card, BLE, Wi-Fi] I --> K[ASSISTIVE RESPONSE
Prosthetic, Stim, Wheelchair] J --> L[REMOTE CLINICIAN
Telemedicine Dashboard] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram (for exam writing):** The arrows show the *unidirectional* flow of information. Each block performs one of the five stages from Section 2.1. The **Interpretation Engine** is the brain where signal interpretation occurs. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Digital Filtering Decision Tree (Choosing FIR vs IIR) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is linear phase
critical for
the diagnostic feature?} Q1 -- YES --> A1[Use FIR Filter
Windowed Sinc or Parks-McClellan] Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Can you tolerate
phase distortion
in real time?} Q2 -- YES --> A2[Use IIR Filter
Butterworth or Chebyshev] Q2 -- NO --> Q1 A1 --> Q3{Need sharp transition
with low order?} A2 --> Q4{Need computationally
cheap filter?} Q3 -- YES --> A3[Use Equiripple FIR
Higher order, sharp cutoff] Q3 -- NO --> A4[Use Moving Average FIR
Simple and stable] Q4 -- YES --> A5[Use Biquad IIR Cascade
Low memory footprint] Q4 -- NO --> A1 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Feature Extraction & Classification Workflow (sEMG → Prosthetic Hand) ```mermaid flowchart TB sEMG[Raw sEMG Signal
5 Channels, 1 kHz] --> Rect[Rectification
abs of signal] Rect --> Win[Sliding Window
200 ms with 50 percent overlap] Win --> Feat[Feature Extraction] Feat --> F1[MAV Mean Absolute Value] Feat --> F2[RMS Root Mean Square] Feat --> F3[ZC Zero Crossings] Feat --> F4[WL Waveform Length] F1 --> Vec[Feature Vector V] F2 --> Vec F3 --> Vec F4 --> Vec Vec --> Cls[Classifier
LDA or SVM or ANN] Cls --> Out[Predicted Gesture
Open, Close, Pinch, Rest] Out --> Motor[DC Motor Driver of
Prosthetic Hand] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Aliasing & Anti-Aliasing Filter Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR Sin[Analog Sine Wave
f = 800 Hz] --> Samp[ADC Sampler
fs = 1000 Hz] Samp --> Aliased[Apparent Output
f apparent = 200 Hz] AAF[Anti-Aliasing Analog
Low-Pass Filter fc = 400 Hz] --> Samp Sin -. filtered before sampling .-> AAF Samp --> Correct[Output Sampled at
1000 Hz retains 400 Hz content] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "How do you prevent aliasing?", the correct two-part answer is: (1) Place a **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** before the ADC with $f_c \leq f_s/2$, and (2) sample at **at least twice** the highest signal frequency. Many students forget part (1) and lose 2 marks. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in the context of biomedical signal processing. Why is a high CMRR essential in a bio-amplifier?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is the ratio of the desired physiological signal power to the unwanted noise power, expressed in decibels (dB): > $$\text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$ > **(1 mark)** > A higher SNR implies a cleaner, more diagnostically reliable signal. Biomedical signals like ECG are typically in the **mV range**, while environmental and thermal noise can be of comparable amplitude, making high SNR crucial. **(1 mark)** > **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** quantifies an amplifier's ability to reject signals common to both inputs (e.g., 50/60 Hz mains pickup) while amplifying the differential bio-signal. A high CMRR (>80 dB) is essential because the human body acts as an antenna picking up electromagnetic interference, and the differential ECG signal is much smaller than the common-mode noise. **(1 mark)** --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem. What happens if it is violated?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A continuous-time, band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely and perfectly reconstructed from its discrete samples provided: > $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max} \quad \text{(Nyquist Rate)}$$ > **(1.5 marks)** > If this condition is violated (i.e., $f_s < 2 f_{max}$), the phenomenon of **aliasing** occurs, where high-frequency components in the original signal falsely appear as lower-frequency components in the digitized version. **(1 mark)** > **Example:** A 70 Hz EMG signal sampled at 100 Hz will appear as a 30 Hz signal, leading to misdiagnosis. **(0.5 marks)** --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the various stages of biomedical signal processing in an assistive device. Describe the function of each stage. **(b) [7 Marks]** An ECG signal with a maximum frequency component of 150 Hz is to be digitized. (i) Determine the minimum sampling frequency to avoid aliasing. (ii) If a 12-bit ADC with a full-scale range of 5 V is used, calculate the resolution (LSB) and theoretical SQNR in dB. (iii) If a first-order RC low-pass filter with $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ is used as the anti-aliasing filter, calculate the required value of $C$. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):** The five stages are: | Stage | Block | Function | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Acquisition** | Electrode/sensor transduces bioelectric potential into voltage. | 1 | | 2 | **Pre-amplification** | INA boosts µV/mV signals; rejects common-mode noise via high CMRR. | 1 | | 3 | **Analog Filtering** | Band-pass filter restricts spectrum (e.g., 0.05–150 Hz for ECG). | 1 | | 4 | **Isolation** | Optical isolation ensures patient safety per IEC 60601-1. | 1 | | 5 | **ADC** | Converts analog to digital; sampling rate & bit depth selected per Nyquist. | 1 | | 6 | **Digital Processing** | DSP performs FFT, FIR/IIR filtering, feature extraction. | 1 | | 7 | **Interpretation** | Classifier (threshold, ML) maps features to clinical/assistive decisions. | 1 | **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Minimum Sampling Frequency (2 marks):** By Nyquist-Shannon: $$f_s = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 150 = 300\,\text{Hz}$$ However, in practice a **guard band** is added, so the typical $f_s$ used is **500 Hz** for ECG. The minimum theoretical value is **300 Hz**. **(ii) Resolution and SQNR (3 marks):** Resolution (step size): $$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{5\,\text{V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} = 1.2207 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 1.22\,\text{mV}$$ Theoretical SQNR: $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76 = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0\,\text{dB}$$ **(iii) Capacitor Value (2 marks):** For anti-aliasing, $f_c = f_s/2 = 300/2 = 150\,\text{Hz}$ (use the minimum $f_s$). $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C = \frac{1}{2\pi R f_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 150}$$ $$C = \frac{1}{9.4248 \times 10^6} = 1.061 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{F} \approx 106\,\text{nF}$$ A standard value of **100 nF or 120 nF** would be selected. --- ### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various sources of noise in biomedical signal acquisition. Discuss the techniques used to minimize each. **(b) [7 Marks]** With relevant mathematical expressions, explain the operation of: (i) A first-order low-pass RC filter (derive the cut-off frequency). (ii) The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) and its significance in interpreting periodic bio-signals such as ECG. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Sources of Noise and Mitigation (7 marks):** | Source | Description | Mitigation Technique | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | **Thermal (Johnson) Noise** | Random motion of charge carriers in electrodes/resistors. Bandwidth-limited. | Lower bandwidth, use low-noise op-amps, cool sensors. | 1.5 | | **Shot Noise** | Discrete nature of charge carrier flow across PN junctions. | Use low-leakage components, good PCB design. | 1 | | **Mains Interference (50/60 Hz)** | Electromagnetic pickup from power lines; dominant in ECG/EEG. | Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit, shielded cables, notch filter at 50/60 Hz, high CMRR amplifier. | 1.5 | | **Motion Artifacts** | Electrode-skin interface changes due to patient movement. | Adhesive Ag/AgCl electrodes, skin preparation (alcohol abrasion), adaptive filtering using accelerometers. | 1.5 | | **Baseline Wander** | Low-frequency drift from respiration or electrode polarization. | High-pass filter with $f_c \approx 0.05\,\text{Hz}$ (ECG) or 0.5 Hz (EEG). | 1 | | **Quantization Noise** | Introduced by ADC; $\pm Q/2$ error per sample. | Increase N (bit depth), oversample and average. | 0.5 | **(b) Filter and DFT (7 marks):** **(i) First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter (3.5 marks):** The transfer function (as derived in Section 3.4) is: $$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$ The magnitude response: $$\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ At the **$-3\,\text{dB}$ point**, magnitude $= 1/\sqrt{2}$, giving the cut-off frequency: $$\boxed{\,f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}$$ **Significance:** Used as the anti-aliasing filter before the ADC. At $f = f_c$, signal power is halved, and above $f_c$, it rolls off at $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$. **(ii) DFT and ECG Interpretation (3.5 marks):** The Discrete Fourier Transform of a finite sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$ is: $$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$ **Significance for ECG Interpretation (2.5 marks):** - The DFT converts the time-domain ECG signal into the **frequency domain**, revealing dominant frequency components. - A normal ECG's energy is concentrated below **40 Hz**, with most power around **0.5 – 10 Hz** (P, QRS, T wave bandwidths). - Fibrillation or noisy signals show high-frequency content (>50 Hz) — useful for **automated arrhythmia detection**. - The **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** is the efficient $O(N \log N)$ algorithm used in real-time DSP implementations. - DFT also enables **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** estimation, crucial for **HRV (Heart Rate Variability)** analysis to assess autonomic nervous system health. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read before writing your exam!)** > > 1. **Forgetting the $-3$ dB condition:** When asked for the cut-off frequency, students often write $f_c = 1/RC$ (the *angular* cut-off $\omega_c$) and forget the $2\pi$ factor. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > 2. **Confusing SNR and SQNR:** SNR is a general concept; SQNR (Signal-to-Quantization-Noise-Ratio) is specific to ADC. Using them interchangeably in derivations costs marks. > 3. **Wrong ADC formula:** Writing $Q = V_{FS} \cdot 2^N$ instead of $V_{FS}/2^N$ is the **most common sign error** in KTU numericals. Double-check the denominator. > 4. **Skipping the unit Hz:** Always append **Hz (or V, dB)** in your final answer. Bare numbers without units often get penalized 0.5 marks. > 5. **Aliasing direction:** Some students think aliasing *creates* new frequencies. It does not — it *folds* existing high frequencies into the lower band. This conceptual error costs 1 mark on "explain" questions. > 6. **Bandwidth mismatch:** If a numerical gives EMG bandwidth (500 Hz) but asks for ECG sampling rate (150 Hz), students blindly apply one to the other. **Read carefully.** > 7. **The 6.02 vs 4.77 trap:** The 6.02N + 1.76 formula is for a *full-scale sine wave input*. For a *uniform random input*, it is 6.02N. Use the correct one. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam!)** > > - ✅ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. **Aliasing** is irreversible. > - ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter:** Analog low-pass, placed *before* the ADC, with $f_c = f_s/2$. > - ✅ **Quantization step size:** $Q = V_{FS} / 2^N$. Smaller $Q$ = better resolution. > - ✅ **Theoretical SQNR:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$ (for full-scale sinusoid). > - ✅ **SNR (general):** $20 \log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ in dB. > - ✅ **CMRR:** Measures amplifier's rejection of common-mode noise; aim **> 80 dB**. > - ✅ **RC low-pass cut-off:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$. Roll-off is **$-20$ dB/decade** (first-order). > - ✅ **Standard bio-signal bandwidths to memorize:** ECG 0.05–150 Hz; EEG 0.5–100 Hz; EMG 20–500 Hz; EOG 0.1–10 Hz. > - ✅ **Practical sampling rates:** ECG 500 Hz; EEG 256–512 Hz; EMG 1–2 kHz (3× to 5× Nyquist). > - ✅ **DFT equation:** $X[k] = \sum x[n] e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ — used in FFT for frequency-domain ECG analysis. > - ✅ **Feature extraction in prosthetics:** MAV, RMS, ZC, WL — fed to LDA/SVM/ANN classifiers. > - ✅ **Safety standard:** **IEC 60601-1** mandates patient isolation via optocouplers. > - ✅ **Pipeline order:** Acquire → Amplify → Filter → Isolate → ADC → DSP → Interpret → Act. > - ✅ **Most-tested formula in KTU:** The SQNR = 6.02N + 1.76 dB relation. Memorize it cold.

topics.title: Mobility Aids: Wheelchairs, Scooters, Walkers, Prosthetics, and Orthotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mobility Aids: Wheelchairs, Scooters, Walkers, Prosthetics, and Orthotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mobility Aids — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobility Aids** are externally applied assistive devices, instruments, apparatus, or implants that substitute, augment, or support impaired locomotive functions of the human body, enabling individuals with temporary or permanent disabilities to perform **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** with dignity, safety, and energy efficiency.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT744)**, mobility aids are broadly classified by the **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** framework of the WHO into:

- **Locomotor Aids** — devices that assist ambulation or seated mobility (wheelchairs, scooters, walkers, canes, crutches).
- **Replacement Aids (Prosthetics)** — devices that substitute a missing limb or body part.
- **Support Aids (Orthotics)** — devices that support, align, prevent, or correct deformities of existing body segments.

The standard **maximum body mass supportable** by a typical adult mobility device ranges from **100 kg to 150 kg**, while bariatric-grade equipment supports up to **300 kg**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of the human skeletal-muscular system as a *car's drivetrain*. When the drivetrain fails (broken axle, missing wheel, weak engine), you don't scrap the car — you fit a **jack** (walker), a **replacement tire** (prosthetic leg), or a **steering stabilizer** (orthotic brace) so it keeps running. Mobility aids are exactly that — the *workshop tools* that keep a person's body "rolling" when nature's original parts are damaged, missing, or weak.

- A **wheelchair** = a *replacement chassis with wheels* when the legs cannot bear weight.
- A **scooter** = a *motorized delivery trike* for long outdoor trips when endurance is limited.
- A **walker** = a *rolling safety frame* that turns an unstable walker into a confident one.
- A **prosthetic** = an *artificial spare part* (like a screw-in replacement joint).
- An **orthotic** = a *chassis alignment tool* that straightens, supports, or offloads a weak structure.

---

## 1.3 Need and Scope

Mobility disability is the **second most common disability worldwide** after hearing impairment. The WHO estimates that approximately **1 billion people (15% of the global population)** experience some form of disability, and around **70 million people** require a wheelchair for daily mobility.

| Statistic | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Global wheelchair need (WHO estimate) | **70 million users** |
| Global prosthetic limb users | **~ 0.5% of population** |
| Energy cost saving (wheelchair vs. crutch ambulation) | **Up to 75%** |
| Standard wheelchair mass (manual) | **10 – 15 kg** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Wheelchairs — Engineering Taxonomy

A wheelchair is a **wheeled mobility device** in which the user sits. It is prescribed when the user cannot ambulate functionally or when ambulation presents a high risk of secondary injury.

### 2.1.1 Classification of Wheelchairs

**A. Based on Propulsion Method**
- **Manual Wheelchairs (MWC)** — human-powered; require **upper-limb strength**.
  - *Self-propelled* (large rear wheels, push rims).
  - *Attendant-propelled* (small rear wheels, transit version).
- **Powered Wheelchairs (PWC)** — battery-driven DC motors (typically **24 V, 50–75 Ah**), joystick or sip-and-puff control.
- **Hybrid / Power-Assisted** — manual frame with electric servo-assist on the wheel hub.

**B. Based on Postural Support**
- **Standard / Depot** — foldable steel frame; mass ≈ **18 kg**; for short-term use.
- **Lightweight / Active** — aluminium or titanium frame; mass **8–12 kg**; custom-fitted.
- **Tilt-in-Space** — entire seat back tilts (typically **0°–45°**) to redistribute pressure.
- **Reclining** — backrest angle adjustable; for users unable to sit upright.
- **Standing Wheelchair** — hydraulically elevates the user to a standing posture; physiological benefits include pressure relief, bone density, circulation.

**C. Based on Drive Configuration (Powered)**

| Drive Type | Symbol | Turning Radius | Indoor Use | Outdoor Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rear-Wheel Drive | RWD | Large | Moderate | Excellent |
| Mid-Wheel Drive | MWD | Smallest | Excellent | Moderate |
| Front-Wheel Drive | FWD | Medium | Good | Good |

### 2.1.2 Mechanical Operating Principle

For a manual wheelchair, the **rolling resistance** is governed by:

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N$$

where $C_{rr}$ is the coefficient of rolling resistance (rubber tyre on smooth floor ≈ **0.015**), and $N$ is the normal load on the wheel.

The **propulsive moment** applied by the user on the push rim must overcome this plus the inertia of the system:

$$\tau_{prop} \geq F_{rr} \cdot r_{wheel} + I \cdot \alpha$$

where $r_{wheel}$ is the rear wheel radius, $I$ is the moment of inertia, and $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration.

---

## 2.2 Mobility Scooters

A **mobility scooter** is a **3- or 4-wheeled battery-powered vehicle** with a bicycle-style steering tiller. Unlike a powered wheelchair, the user typically does not require postural support — they sit in a standard chair-like seat.

> [!NOTE]
> Scooters are designed for **outdoor / community use** by users with reasonable trunk control, upper-limb function, and adequate vision and cognition. They are *not* prescribed for users with complex postural needs — that is the role of the PWC.

| Parameter | Class 2 (Pavement) | Class 3 (Road) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Max Speed | **6.4 km/h (4 mph)** | **12.8 km/h (8 mph)** |
| Use Zone | Pavements only | Pavement + Road |
| Lights / Indicators | Optional | Mandatory |
| Max Range per Charge | **15 – 40 km** | **25 – 50 km** |

Scooter drive is typically via a **24 V or 48 V DC permanent-magnet motor** coupled to a transaxle, with speed governed by a **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) controller**.

---

## 2.3 Walkers

A **walker (walking frame)** is a lightweight, four-legged metal frame that provides a **wide base of support** for users with balance, strength, or endurance deficits. They are the most prescribed ambulation aid in geriatric and post-stroke rehabilitation.

### 2.3.1 Types of Walkers

- **Standard (Pick-Up) Walker** — four rubber-tipped legs; no wheels. Requires the user to lift and place the frame.
- **Two-Wheeled Walker** — front castors, rear tips; easier to advance.
- **Rollator (Four-Wheeled Walker)** — four wheels, hand brakes, padded seat, storage basket. The most popular community walker.
- **Hemi-Walker** — one-handed version, used in hemiplegia.
- **Platform / Gait-Trainer Walker** — forearm platform; for users with weight-bearing restrictions (e.g., rheumatoid hand arthritis).

### 2.3.2 Biomechanical Principle — Stability Triangle

The walker functions by **expanding the user's base of support (BoS)**. A normal adult's BoS is the foot contact area (≈ **30 cm × 20 cm**). Adding the walker expands this by a factor of **3× to 4×**, drastically reducing the risk of lateral falls.

For rollators, the **brake-clutch interlock** prevents rearward rolling on slopes — a safety feature governed by:

$$F_{brake} \geq m g \sin\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the slope angle and $m$ is the user + rollator mass.

---

## 2.4 Prosthetics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **prosthesis** is an *artificial substitute* that **replaces** a missing body part, lost through trauma, disease, or congenital absence. The branch of medicine is **prosthetics**; the device itself is a **prosthesis** (plural: **prostheses**).

### 2.4.1 Upper-Limb Prostheses

| Level of Amputation | Typical Prosthetic Solution | Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Partial Hand | Cosmetic fingers / silicone glove | Passive |
| Wrist Disarticulation | Wrist unit + terminal device | Cable / Myoelectric |
| Transradial (below elbow) | Socket + cable-driven or myoelectric hand | Body-powered / Myo |
| Transhumeral (above elbow) | Socket + elbow unit + terminal device | Cable + Electric |
| Shoulder Disarticulation | Shoulder unit + elbow + hand | Hybrid |

**Control Methods:**
- **Body-Powered** — pull cable actuated by shoulder/scapular motion. Cosmesis lower, but reliable, cheap, and high feedback.
- **Myoelectric** — surface EMG electrodes detect residual-muscle contractions. Two-muscle-site control (flexor = close; extensor = open).
- **Hybrid** — body-powered elbow + myoelectric terminal device.

### 2.4.2 Lower-Limb Prostheses

Levels match those of upper limb — *transfemoral (above knee)* being the most biomechanically demanding due to the missing knee.

**Sach Foot (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel):**
- Wooden or plastic core with compressible rubber heel wedge.
- Simulates plantarflexion by heel compression.

**Energy-Storing Feet (Carbon-Fiber):**
- E.g., *Flex-Foot, Seattle Foot, Carbon Copy II*.
- The carbon pylon stores strain energy during loading and releases it at toe-off.

> [!NOTE]
> The **swing-phase** of gait in a transfemoral prosthesis is governed by a **prosthetic knee unit**. Single-axis constant-friction knees are the simplest; microprocessor-controlled knees (e.g., C-Leg, Genium) use **3-axis gyroscopes and accelerometers** to vary damping up to **50 times per second** for stumble-recovery.

### 2.4.3 Socket Biomechanics

The **socket** is the interface that transmits load between residual limb and prosthesis. Stresses at the bone–soft-tissue–socket interface must remain below **~ 200 kPa** to avoid tissue breakdown.

For a transfemoral socket (ischial-containment design), the load is shared as:

$$L_{total} = L_{ischial} + L_{soft\ tissue} + L_{distal\ end-bearing}$$

---

## 2.5 Orthotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **orthosis** (plural: **orthoses**) is an *externally applied device* that **supports, aligns, prevents, or corrects** the function of movable parts of the body. *Orthotics* is the medical field; *orthosis* is the device.

### 2.5.1 Common Lower-Limb Orthoses

- **AFO (Ankle-Foot Orthosis)** — controls ankle motion in drop-foot, post-stroke spasticity.
- **KAFO (Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis)** — used in poliomyelitis, spinal cord injury.
- **HKAFO (Hip-KAFO)** — adds a hip joint; for paraplegic standing.
- **Knee Orthosis (KO)** — e.g., *Swedish knee cage, Lenox Hill derotation brace* for ACL-deficient knees.
- **Hip Orthosis (HO)** — post-hip arthroplasty abduction brace.

### 2.5.2 Common Upper-Limb & Spinal Orthoses

- **Wrist-Hand Orthosis (WHO)** — for carpal tunnel, rheumatoid positioning.
- **Cervical Orthosis (CO)** — *soft collar, Philadelphia collar, Miami-J, halo vest*.
- **Thoracolumbosacral Orthosis (TLSO)** — for scoliosis (e.g., **Boston brace, Milwaukee brace, Charleston bending brace**).
- **Lumbosacral Orthosis (LSO)** — for low-back pain, post-disc surgery.

### 2.5.3 Three-Point Pressure Principle

> [!NOTE]
> Every orthosis that corrects a deformity works on the **Three-Point Pressure Principle**: one force is applied at the **apex of the deformity**, and two counter-forces are applied proximally and distally. The middle force must equal the sum of the two outer forces to maintain static equilibrium.

$$F_2 = F_1 + F_3$$

This is the foundational biomechanical law of orthotic design.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Formula / Value | Units | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} N$ | N | Wheelchair design |
| PWC Battery | **24 – 48 V** | V | Electrical spec |
| Scooter max speed (Class 3) | **12.8** | km/h | Regulatory |
| PWC max user mass (bariatric) | **300** | kg | Standard |
| AFO control plane | Sagittal / Frontal / Transverse | — | Biomechanics |
| Three-point pressure | $F_2 = F_1 + F_3$ | N | Orthotic law |
| SACH foot | Plantarflexion mimic | — | Lower-limb prosthetic |
| EMG threshold (myoelectric) | **20 – 200 µV** | µV | Control signal |
| Walker base of support multiplier | **3 – 4 ×** foot BoS | — | Stability |
| Socket interface pressure limit | **≤ 200** | kPa | Tissue safety |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Quantitative Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Manual Wheelchair Rolling Resistance

**Problem:** A 70-kg user sits in a manual wheelchair of total mass 18 kg on a flat, smooth indoor floor. The rear wheel diameter is 0.6 m (radius = 0.3 m) and the coefficient of rolling resistance $C_{rr} = 0.015$. Calculate the **force required to push the wheelchair at constant velocity**, and the **propulsive torque at the push-rim** (push-rim radius = 0.27 m).

### Step 1 — Identify Loads

The normal load on the rear wheels is supported by the user's weight plus chair mass, with the rear wheels typically carrying **70%** of the total on a level surface:

$$N_{rear} = 0.7 \times (m_{user} + m_{chair}) \times g$$

$$N_{rear} = 0.7 \times (70 + 18) \times 9.81$$

$$N_{rear} = 0.7 \times 88 \times 9.81$$

$$N_{rear} = 604.3\ \text{N}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Resistive Force

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N_{rear}$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 604.3$$

$$F_{rr} = 9.06\ \text{N}$$

> The horizontal push force must equal this at constant velocity (Newton's 1st law).

### Step 3 — Compute the Wheel-Axle Torque

$$\tau_{axle} = F_{rr} \times r_{wheel}$$

$$\tau_{axle} = 9.06 \times 0.3 = 2.72\ \text{N·m}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Push-Rim Torque (Mechanical Advantage)

Because the push-rim is *smaller* than the wheel, the user must apply *more* torque at the rim. The mechanical disadvantage ratio is:

$$MA = \frac{r_{rim}}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{0.27}{0.3} = 0.9$$

$$\tau_{rim} = \frac{\tau_{axle}}{MA} = \frac{2.72}{0.9} = 3.02\ \text{N·m}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Hand Force (Two-Handed)

Two hands act on the push-rim, so the force per hand is:

$$F_{hand} = \frac{\tau_{rim}}{2 \cdot r_{rim}} = \frac{3.02}{2 \times 0.27} = 5.6\ \text{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 70-kg adult thus needs only **5.6 N per hand** on level floor — less than the weight of a laptop. This explains why manual wheelchairs remain the energy-efficient gold standard for community ambulation in upper-limb-intact users.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Walker Brake Holding on a Slope

**Problem:** A rollator + user has a total mass of 90 kg. What minimum brake friction force is needed to hold the rollator stationary on a 10° slope?

### Step 1 — Resolve Gravity Along the Slope

$$F_{parallel} = m g \sin\theta = 90 \times 9.81 \times \sin(10°)$$

$$\sin(10°) = 0.1736$$

$$F_{parallel} = 90 \times 9.81 \times 0.1736 = 153.3\ \text{N}$$

### Step 2 — Brake Requirement

The brake pads must supply a horizontal friction force (assuming rear-wheel braking) of at least this magnitude. If the brake is applied to both rear wheels, each brake must provide:

$$F_{brake,\ each} = \frac{F_{parallel}}{2} = \frac{153.3}{2} = 76.6\ \text{N}$$

> For static friction with a rubber pad on rubber tyre ($\mu \approx 0.6$), the *normal pinch force* required on each brake lever is:
>
> $$F_{lever} = \frac{F_{brake,\ each}}{\mu} = \frac{76.6}{0.6} = 127.7\ \text{N}$$
>
> A typical hand grip can comfortably produce **200 – 300 N**, so safety is maintained.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Three-Point Pressure in a Scoliosis TLSO

**Problem:** A Boston-type TLSO is designed to correct a 30° lumbar scoliosis. The corrective pad at the apex applies a force of 50 N. The counter-pads are placed 25 cm proximal and 25 cm distal to the apex. Calculate the counter-force required at each pad.

### Step 1 — Apply the Three-Point Pressure Principle

$$F_{apex} = F_{proximal} + F_{distal}$$

For a symmetric orthosis, the two counter-pads share the load equally:

$$F_{proximal} = F_{distal} = \frac{F_{apex}}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25\ \text{N}$$

### Step 2 — Verify Pressure Under Pads

If each counter-pad has a contact area of 25 cm² = 25 × 10⁻⁴ m², the interface pressure is:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{25}{25 \times 10^{-4}} = 10{,}000\ \text{Pa} = 10\ \text{kPa}$$

This is well below the **200 kPa** tissue safety limit, ensuring no skin breakdown during the **18 – 23 hours/day** of wear prescribed for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Mobility Aid

| Patient Profile | Recommended Aid | Justification (CO Mapped) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bilateral amputation, good UE strength, community ambulator | **Lightweight manual wheelchair** | Lowest energy cost; preserves shoulder integrity |
| C4 spinal cord injury, limited hand function | **PWC with chin / sip-and-puff control** | No UE strength; needs powered mobility |
| Post-stroke hemiplegia with balance deficit | **Hemi-walker or quad cane** | One-hand support; expanded BoS |
| Transfemoral amputee, K3 activity level | **Microprocessor knee + carbon foot** | Variable cadence; energy return |
| Diabetic Charcot foot, severe deformity | **Patellar-tendon-bearing (PTB) AFO** | Off-loads foot, prevents ulceration |
| Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis, Cobb 25° | **Boston TLSO** | Three-point correction during growth |
| Rheumatoid arthritis, painful weight-bearing | **Platform walker + forearm support** | Removes load from wrist/hand joints |
| Congenital below-elbow limb deficiency, age 3 | **Myoelectric hand (Otto Bock / bebionic)** | Cosmetic + functional grasp |

---

## 3.5 Lower-Limb Prosthetic Alignment Geometry

The **TKA line (Trochanter-Knee-Ankle line)** is the reference for socket alignment. A well-aligned transtibial socket has the *knee centre* anterior to this line by **5 – 15 mm** for sagittal stability.

The **shank angle** (between socket and pylon) is typically set at **3° – 5° of inset** for the sound-side limb to match a normal gait line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Cost of Amputee Walking vs. Able-Bodied:**
> | Level | Energy Cost Increase vs. Normal |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | Transtibial (below knee) | **+ 25 %** |
> | Transfemoral (above knee) | **+ 60 – 100 %** |
> | Bilateral transfemoral | **+ 200 % or more** |
>
> This is why KTU examination questions on prosthetics often ask you to *justify* the prescription of microprocessor-controlled knees — they reduce compensatory effort and metabolic demand by **up to 20 %**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mobility-Aid Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Patient with Mobility Deficit] --> B{Postural Control}
    B -- Good trunk control --> C{Endurance / Distance}
    B -- Poor trunk control --> D[Powered Wheelchair PWC]
    C -- Short indoor trips --> E[Manual Wheelchair MWC]
    C -- Long outdoor trips --> F[Mobility Scooter]
    A --> G{Ambulation Possible}
    G -- Yes, weak balance --> H[Walker / Rollator]
    G -- Yes, one-hand support --> I[Cane Hemi-Walker]
    G -- Lower-limb amputation --> J[Prosthesis]
    G -- Joint deformity / weakness --> K[Orthosis]
    H --> H1[Standard Pick-Up]
    H --> H2[Two-Wheeled]
    H --> H3[Four-Wheeled Rollator]
    J --> J1[Upper-Limb Prosthesis]
    J --> J2[Lower-Limb Prosthesis]
    K --> K1[Spinal Orthosis]
    K --> K2[Upper-Limb Orthosis]
    K --> K3[Lower-Limb Orthosis]
```

## 4.2 Wheelchair Drive-Configuration Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[User Input: Joystick / Sip-Puff / Switch] --> B[PWM Motor Controller]
    B --> C1[Right Motor Driver]
    B --> C2[Left Motor Driver]
    C1 --> D1[Rear-Wheel Motor]
    C2 --> D2[Rear-Wheel Motor]
    D1 --> E[Wheelchair Frame and Seat]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Feedback Encoders per Wheel]
    F --> B
    E --> G[Battery Pack 24-48 V]
    G --> B
```

## 4.3 Lower-Limb Prosthetic Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Residual Limb Interface] --> B[Custom Socket]
    B --> C[Suspension Sleeve or Suction Valve]
    C --> D[Shank Pylon Carbon or Aluminium]
    D --> E[Prosthetic Foot Module]
    E --> E1[SACH Foot]
    E --> E2[Single-Axis Foot]
    E --> E3[Multi-Axis Foot]
    E --> E4[Energy-Storing Carbon Foot]
    B --> F[Knee Module]
    F --> F1[Single-Axis Friction Knee]
    F --> F2[Polycentric Knee]
    F --> F3[Microprocessor Knee]
    F --> F4[Hydraulic Knee]
```

## 4.4 Three-Point Pressure System for Orthotic Correction

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Proximal Counter-Force F1] --> C[Deformed Segment Apex]
    C --> B[Distal Counter-Force F3]
    C -- Corrective Force F2 --> D[Aligned Segment]
    A -. equilibrium .- B
```

> The diagram above encodes the **Three-Point Pressure Principle**: $F_2 = F_1 + F_3$. The proximal pad (F1) and distal pad (F3) compress around the apex corrective pad (F2), with F2 supplying the net corrective load.

## 4.5 Myoelectric Prosthetic Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Residual Muscle Flexor] --> B[Surface EMG Electrode]
    A2[Residual Muscle Extensor] --> B2[Surface EMG Electrode]
    B --> C[Differential Amplifier]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Band-Pass Filter 20-500 Hz]
    D --> E[Rectifier and Envelope Detector]
    E --> F[Threshold Comparator 20-200 uV]
    F --> G1[Hand Close Relay]
    F --> G2[Hand Open Relay]
    G1 --> H[Motorised Hand or Hook]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Position / Force Feedback]
    I --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a "mobility aid" and classify mobility aids under the WHO-ICF framework. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A mobility aid is any externally applied device that substitutes, augments, or supports impaired locomotive function to enable Activities of Daily Living (ADL).

Under the WHO-ICF classification, mobility aids are categorized as:

1. **Locomotor Aids** — wheelchairs, scooters, walkers, canes, crutches (assist existing or absent ambulation).
2. **Replacement Aids (Prosthetics)** — substitute a missing body segment (e.g., transtibial prosthesis).
3. **Support Aids (Orthotics)** — support, align, or correct existing body segments (e.g., AFO, TLSO).

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for classification)

### Q2. Differentiate between a "prosthesis" and an "orthosis" with one example each. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Prosthesis | Orthosis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Function | **Replaces** a missing body part | **Supports** an existing body part |
| Example | Transfemoral artificial leg | Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO) |
| Body status | Amputation / congenital absence | Intact but weak / deformed |

(3 marks: 1 distinction + 1 example + 1 medical-field name)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Provide diagrams where applicable — they carry **1 to 2 marks** each as per KTU valuation key.

---

### Question A (Choice 1)

#### Q.A (a) **Describe the classification of manual and powered wheelchairs. With neat sketches, explain the operating principle of a powered wheelchair using a joystick and PWM controller. [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification of Manual Wheelchairs (2 marks):**

- **Standard / Depot** — foldable steel, mass ≈ 18 kg, mass-produced.
- **Lightweight / Active** — aluminium/titanium, mass 8–12 kg, customized.
- **Ultralight / Rigid** — for active users, no folding.
- **Tilt-in-Space** — whole-seat rotation to relieve pressure.
- **Reclining** — back-angle adjustment.
- **Standing** — hydraulic elevation.

**2. Classification of Powered Wheelchairs (2 marks):**

- **Drive configuration:** Rear-Wheel (RWD), Mid-Wheel (MWD), Front-Wheel (FWD).
- **Wheel type:** Pneumatic, solid, foam-filled.
- **Controller:** Joystick, chin, sip-and-puff, head-array.
- **Seating:** Standard captain seat or custom-molded postural seating.

**3. Operating Principle of Joystick-Controlled PWC (3 marks):**

The joystick is a **2-axis potentiometer** that produces a DC voltage proportional to deflection in X and Y. The PWM controller interprets this signal as a *desired velocity vector*:

$$V_{motor,\ left} = k \cdot (V_{y} - V_{x})$$

$$V_{motor,\ right} = k \cdot (V_{y} + V_{x})$$

where $V_x$ and $V_y$ are the X (steering) and Y (forward) joystick voltages, and $k$ is a gain constant. The PWM signal at duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{V_{motor}}{V_{battery}}$$

drives the DC motors via an **H-bridge MOSFET driver**. Wheel encoders feed back speed to the controller, which closes the loop using **PID control** to maintain commanded velocity despite load changes (ramps, carpet).

**Diagram Expected:** Block diagram of PWC (refer to SECTION 4.2 of these notes). **(1 mark)**

---

#### Q.A (b) **Explain in detail the biomechanical operating principle of a transtibial (below-knee) prosthesis. Discuss the SACH foot and the energy-storing carbon-fiber foot. [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Transtibial Prosthesis — Functional Components (2 marks):**

- **Socket** — Patellar-Tendon-Bearing (PTB) design transfers 60% of load through the patellar tendon.
- **Suspension** — Suction, pin-lock, or sleeve.
- **Shank (Pylon)** — aluminium or carbon-fibre connecting socket to foot.
- **Foot module** — SACH, single-axis, multi-axis, or energy-storing.

**2. SACH Foot (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel) — 2 marks:**

- A wooden or plastic core encased in rubber.
- The **heel wedge** compresses during heel-strike, mimicking ankle plantarflexion of **~ 15°**.
- Provides **plantarflexion-dorsiflexion rollover** but no inversion-eversion motion.
- **Advantages:** cheap (~$50), waterproof, low maintenance.
- **Disadvantages:** no energy return, limited to K1–K2 activity levels.

**3. Energy-Storing Carbon-Fibre Foot — 3 marks:**

- Carbon-composite leaf springs form the keel.
- During **loading response**, the keel bends and **stores strain energy**:

$$U_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} k \delta^2$$

where $k$ is the leaf-spring stiffness (N/m) and $\delta$ is the deflection (m).

- During **terminal stance / toe-off**, the stored energy is **released**, providing an active push-off (return ≈ **80 – 95 %** of input energy for top-end feet).
- Examples: *Össur Flex-Foot, Ottobock Triton, Fillauer AllPro*.
- Suitable for **K3 – K4** activity levels (community ambulators, athletes).
- Reduces oxygen consumption by **10 – 20 %** vs. SACH.

**Diagram Expected:** Labelled sketch of SACH foot cross-section. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Set)

#### Q.B (a) **Classify walkers. Explain the biomechanical principle of base of support and how a rollator enhances safety during ambulation. [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification of Walkers (3 marks):**

- **Standard (Pick-Up) Walker** — 4 rubber-tipped legs, no wheels, lifted with each step.
- **Two-Wheeled Walker** — front castors, rear glides.
- **Rollator (Four-Wheeled)** — 4 wheels, hand brakes, seat, basket.
- **Hemi-Walker** — one-handed version.
- **Platform / Forearm Walker** — for non-weight-bearing hands.
- **Reciprocal / Gait-Trainer** — hinged frame for stepping pattern.

**2. Biomechanical Principle — Base of Support (BoS) (2 marks):**

The **BoS** is the area bounded by the points of contact with the ground. A normal adult's BoS is the area between and including the feet (≈ 30 cm × 20 cm). When the **centre of mass (CoM)** projects outside this BoS, the person falls.

Adding a walker extends the BoS by a factor of **3× to 4×**, encompassing both feet and the walker's contact points. This greatly reduces the angular excursion of the CoM relative to the BoS perimeter, decreasing fall risk.

**3. Rollator Safety Features (2 marks):**

- **Locking hand brakes** — prevent runaway on slopes.
- **Padded seat** — allows the user to rest on long walks.
- **Storage basket** — reduces load carried in hands.
- **Loop brakes** can be used as a *parking brake* — locking both rear wheels.
- Brake-hold condition: $F_{brake} \geq m g \sin\theta$ (see Worked Example 2 above).

**Diagram Expected:** Labelled rollator. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Q.B (b) **Describe the classification of orthoses. With a suitable example, explain the Three-Point Pressure Principle as applied to orthotic correction of scoliosis. [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification of Orthoses (3 marks):**

By body region, the *acronym-based* KTU-recommended classification is:

- **FO** – Foot Orthosis
- **AFO** – Ankle-Foot Orthosis
- **KAFO** – Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis
- **HKAFO** – Hip-KAFO
- **KO** – Knee Orthosis
- **HO** – Hip Orthosis
- **WHO / WHFO** – Wrist-Hand (Finger) Orthosis
- **EO** – Elbow Orthosis
- **SO** – Shoulder Orthosis
- **CO** – Cervical Orthosis
- **LSO** – Lumbosacral Orthosis
- **TLSO** – Thoracolumbosacral Orthosis
- **CTLSO / Milwaukee** – Cervico-Thoraco-Lumbo-Sacral Orthosis

**2. Three-Point Pressure Principle (2 marks):**

$$F_2 = F_1 + F_3$$

A **corrective force F₂** is applied at the **apex of the deformity**, and **two counter-forces F₁ (proximal) and F₃ (distal)** act in the opposite direction. Static equilibrium of forces and moments is achieved, producing controlled correction of the angular deformity without slipping.

**3. Application to Scoliosis (Boston Brace) — 2 marks:**

For a right-thoracic curve:
- **F₂ (apex corrective pad)** — placed on the right thoracic rib hump.
- **F₁ (proximal counter-pad)** — left shoulder / axillary region.
- **F₃ (distal counter-pad)** — left lumbar / pelvic region.
- The corrective pad derotates the vertebral column and reduces Cobb angle during the growth window.
- Worn **18 – 23 hours/day**; corrective force per pad ≈ 25 – 50 N (see Worked Example 3).

**Diagram Expected:** Three-point pressure diagram on a scoliotic spine. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "prosthesis" and "prosthetics."** *Prosthetics* is the medical field; *prosthesis* is the device. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Skipping units.** Always write N, kg, km/h, kPa, µV. Numerical answers without units → **0.5 mark** penalty per KTU key.
> 3. **Forgetting to label diagrams.** Unlabelled sketches fetch **0 marks** even if the content is correct. Label every part in **CAPS**.
> 4. **Missing the "why."** KTU 2024 Scheme follows OBE — examiners look for the *justification* of a design choice, not just the description. "SACH foot is cheaper" gets 1 mark; "SACH foot is prescribed for K1–K2 geriatric users who need a low-maintenance waterproof foot" gets full marks.
> 5. **Confusing PWC drive types** (RWD/MWD/FWD) — many students mix up the indoor/outdoor suitability.
> 6. **Incorrect formula for three-point pressure** — students sometimes write $F_1 + F_2 + F_3 = 0$ (true) but forget to state $F_2 = F_1 + F_3$ (the principle).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Mobility aids** = **3 classes** under WHO-ICF: *Locomotor*, *Prosthetic*, *Orthotic*.
- [ ] **Manual wheelchair** rolling resistance: $F_{rr} = C_{rr} N$ where $C_{rr} \approx 0.015$.
- [ ] **PWC battery** standard: **24 V / 48 V DC**; control: **PWM joystick** with PID loop.
- [ ] **Drive configurations:** RWD (outdoor), MWD (indoor), FWD (climb curbs).
- [ ] **Mobility scooter** class 2 = **6.4 km/h**, class 3 = **12.8 km/h**.
- [ ] **Walker** expands **BoS by 3–4×** the foot-only BoS.
- [ ] **Rollator brake** must satisfy $F_{brake} \geq m g \sin\theta$ on slopes.
- [ ] **Prosthetic** *replaces* a missing part; **Orthotic** *supports* an existing part.
- [ ] **Myoelectric control** uses surface **EMG** at thresholds of **20–200 µV**.
- [ ] **SACH foot** = cheap, waterproof, plantarflexion mimic; **carbon foot** = energy return ≈ 80–95 %.
- [ ] **Transfemoral amputee** walking costs **+60 to 100 %** metabolic energy vs. able-bodied.
- [ ] **Three-Point Pressure Law** for orthoses: $F_2 = F_1 + F_3$.
- [ ] **Boston TLSO** worn **18–23 hours/day** for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis.
- [ ] **Tissue safety pressure limit** at socket/skin interface: **≤ 200 kPa**.
- [ ] **Microprocessor knees** vary damping **up to 50 times per second** for stumble recovery.
- [ ] **Energy-storing foot** stores strain energy $U = \frac{1}{2} k \delta^2$ during stance.
- [ ] KTU 2024 valuation rewards **units, diagrams, and engineering "why."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensory Aids: Hearing Aids, Cochlear Implants, Visual Aids, and Braille Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT744 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensory Aids: Hearing Aids, Cochlear Implants, Visual Aids, and Braille Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sensory Aids — The Foundation

**Sensory Aids** are a class of *assistive medical devices* designed to substitute, augment, or restore a compromised human sensory pathway — specifically the auditory (hearing) and visual (sight) channels. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these are **biomedical electro-mechanical instruments** that interface directly with a residual physiological function or, when residual function is absent, bypass the natural sensory organ and electrically stimulate the central nervous system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Sensory aids encompass both *non-invasive amplification/optic systems* (conventional hearing aids, magnifiers) and *invasive neural-prosthetic systems* (cochlear implants, retinal implants) along with *tactile substitution systems* (Braille displays and embossers) that translate one sensory modality into another.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sensory Bridge" Concept

Imagine your **ear as a microphone and your eye as a camera**. Both are transducer organs that convert one form of energy (sound waves, photons) into bio-electrical nerve impulses.

| Natural Organ | Failure Mode | Sensory Aid "Bridge" |
|---|---|---|
| Microphone (Ear) | Damaged hair cells, eardrum perforation | **Hearing Aid** (loudspeaker + amplifier) |
| Microphone (Ear) | Complete hair-cell death | **Cochlear Implant** (direct nerve stimulator) |
| Camera (Eye) | Lens clouding, retinal damage | **Visual Aid** (magnifier / CCTV / screen reader) |
| Camera (Eye) | Total blindness | **Braille Device** (tactile pixel translator) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Hook:** A hearing aid is like turning up the volume on a TV whose speaker is fine but whose listener is sitting far away. A cochlear implant is like replacing the TV's broken speaker entirely with a separate set of vibrating patches pressed directly against the listener's skin (the auditory nerve). A Braille device is like reading a webpage by feeling raised dots instead of seeing text.

### 1.3 Classification of Sensory Aids (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies sensory aids into **two super-families** based on the residual function they exploit:

**A. Auditory (Hearing) Aids**
- Conventional Acoustic Hearing Aids (Body-level, Behind-The-Ear, In-The-Ear, Completely-In-Canal)
- Bone-Anchored Hearing Aids (BAHA)
- Middle Ear Implants (Vibrant Soundbridge)
- **Cochlear Implants** (CI) — neural prosthetic

**B. Visual (Sight) Aids**
- Optical Low-Vision Aids (magnifiers, telescopes)
- Non-Optical Aids (large-print, high-contrast, filters)
- Electronic/Video Magnifiers (CCTV readers)
- **Braille Displays and Embossers** — tactile substitution
- Screen readers (software-based, included for completeness)

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded for Memory Retention)

- **Speech Frequency Band: 250 Hz to 8 kHz** (the diagnostic audiometric range)
- **Threshold of Human Hearing: 0 dB SPL = 20 µPa** (sound pressure level reference)
- **Standard Conversational Speech Level: ~60 dB SPL** at 1 meter
- **Mild Hearing Loss: 26–40 dB HL**, **Moderate: 41–55 dB HL**, **Severe: 71–90 dB HL**, **Profound: >91 dB HL**
- **Braille Cell: 2×3 dot matrix (6 dots)** raised from a base plane; **8-dot Braille = 2×4 matrix** for computer use
- **Refreshable Braille Pin Spacing: 2.5 mm center-to-center** (standard Library of Congress specification)
- **Visual Acuity Reference: 6/6 (Snellen 20/20)** corresponding to a resolving angle of **1 arc-minute (1′)**

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hearing Aid — Operational Decomposition

A conventional hearing aid is a **four-stage electroacoustic signal processor**. Each stage performs a single, well-defined transformation.

**Stage 1 — Microphone (Transducer)**
- A miniature electret or MEMS microphone converts acoustic pressure variations $p(t)$ into an equivalent analog voltage $v_{mic}(t)$.
- Sensitivity is typically quoted in **dB re 1 V/Pa**, with values between **−46 dB and −38 dB** for modern devices.

**Stage 2 — Analog Pre-amplifier + AGC**
- The weak microphone signal (in microvolts) is amplified.
- An **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** loop dynamically compresses the dynamic range so that soft sounds (e.g., a whisper at 30 dB SPL) become audible while loud sounds (e.g., a door slam at 100 dB SPL) are not painfully amplified.
- Compression ratio $CR$ is defined below in the formula table.

**Stage 3 — Digital Signal Processor (DSP)**
- The analog signal is digitized by an ADC.
- Algorithms applied: **noise reduction, feedback cancellation, directional beamforming, frequency-shaping (tone control)**.
- Output is converted back to analog via a DAC.

**Stage 4 — Receiver (Loudspeaker)**
- The amplified electrical signal drives a miniature balanced-armature receiver, reconverting it to acoustic pressure $p_{out}(t)$ delivered into the ear canal.

### 2.2 The Cochlear Implant — Bypassing the Cochlea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A cochlear implant is the **first successful neural prosthetic** to routinely restore a human sense. It does NOT amplify sound — it directly electrically stimulates the spiral ganglion neurons of the auditory nerve, bypassing the damaged hair cells of the organ of Corti.

**External Components (Worn on the head):**
- Microphone array (sits behind the ear)
- Sound Processor (DSP — extracts envelope of filterbank outputs)
- Transmitter Coil (sends power + data via RF link at ~5 MHz)

**Internal Components (Surgically implanted):**
- Receiver/Stimulator (decodes RF, generates biphasic current pulses)
- Electrode Array (12–22 platinum-iridium electrodes inserted into the scala tympani of the cochlea)

**Why the Filterbank?** The cochlea normally performs a *mechanical Fourier analysis*, with high frequencies resonating at the base and low frequencies at the apex. The CI simulates this by splitting the incoming audio (typically 100 Hz – 8 kHz) into **N band-pass channels** and assigning each channel to a specific electrode position (tonotopic mapping).

### 2.3 Visual Aids — Magnification, Substitution, and Enhancement

**Optical Magnification Principle:** The angular magnification $M$ of a simple magnifier is given by:

$$M = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$$

where $D$ is the *least distance of distinct vision* (conventionally **25 cm**) and $f$ is the focal length of the magnifying lens in **cm**.

For a Keplerian telescope (used in distance low-vision aids):

$$M_{tel} = \frac{f_{obj}}{f_{eye}}$$

**Electronic Video Magnifiers (CCTVs):** A zoom camera captures a printed page, and a high-contrast LCD displays the magnified image. Magnification of **4× to 50×** is typical.

**Tactile Substitution (Braille):** Since vision provides 2D spatial information, a tactile display must encode text into a 2D raised-dot grid. The 6-dot Braille cell, invented by Louis Braille in 1824, provides **$2^6 = 64$** unique patterns, which is enough for the Latin alphabet, punctuation, and numerals (with a number-sign prefix). Extended 8-dot Braille provides **$2^8 = 256$** patterns, sufficient for the full ASCII set.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbol | Unit / Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$ | dB SPL, $p_{ref} = 20\,\mu Pa$ | Audiometric reference |
| 2 | Hearing Loss Classification | $HL = L_{thresh} - 0$ | dB HL | Device prescription |
| 3 | Acoustic Gain (dB) | $G = 20\log_{10}(p_{out}/p_{in})$ | dB | Hearing aid spec |
| 4 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \frac{\Delta L_{in}}{\Delta L_{out}}$ | dimensionless | AGC tuning |
| 5 | Channel Center Frequencies | $f_c(i) = f_{min} \cdot \alpha^{(i-1)}$ | Hz, $\alpha$ = step factor | CI filterbank design |
| 6 | Magnifier Power | $M = 1 + D/f$ | dimensionless | Visual aid spec |
| 7 | Telescope Magnification | $M = f_{obj}/f_{eye}$ | dimensionless | Distance aid spec |
| 8 | Visual Angle (Snellen) | $\theta = h/d$ | arc-minutes | Acuity testing |
| 9 | Braille Cell Capacity (6-dot) | $N = 2^6 = 64$ | patterns | Tactile encoding |
| 10 | Braille Cell Capacity (8-dot) | $N = 2^8 = 256$ | patterns | Computer Braille |
| 11 | Logarithmic Frequency Spacing | $f_n = f_0 \cdot k^n$ | Hz | Cochlear tonotopy |
| 12 | Insertion Gain (probe mic) | $IG = SPL_{earcanal} - SPL_{ref}$ | dB | Real-ear measurement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the cochlear implant uses *logarithmic* frequency spacing — this mimics the basilar membrane's *tonotopic* (place-frequency) mapping. The high-frequency end of the filterbank has wider absolute bandwidth, but constant *critical-band* (Bark) width in perceptual terms.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hearing aids** are now among the most prescribed medical devices worldwide, with over **15 million units** sold annually.
- **Cochlear implants** are the gold standard for bilateral severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss, with over **700,000 recipients globally**.
- **Refreshable Braille displays** (e.g., BrailleNote, Orbit Reader) use **piezoelectric bimorph actuators** to raise and lower pins at >100 Hz update rates, enabling real-time tactile reading of digital text.
- **CCTV magnifiers** are deployed in libraries and examination centers under India's *Right of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act)* — a direct KTU exam-relevant social-engineering link.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Acoustic Gain of a Hearing Aid in Decibels

**Problem Setup:** A patient with a 50 dB HL hearing loss at 1 kHz requires a conversational speech level of 60 dB SPL to be perceived at 40 dB SL (sensation level) above their threshold. The hearing aid microphone receives the speech at 60 dB SPL and must deliver it into the ear canal at a level that compensates for the loss.

**Step 1 — Identify the threshold and the required output SPL.**

The patient's threshold at 1 kHz is 50 dB HL. To make the speech intelligible, we need it to be perceived at 40 dB SL above threshold.

$$L_{thresh} = 50 \text{ dB HL}$$
$$L_{required} = L_{thresh} + SL = 50 + 40 = 90 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the input SPL at the microphone.**

The microphone of a BTE (Behind-The-Ear) hearing aid sits roughly at the entrance of the ear canal and therefore sees essentially the same acoustic field as the unaided ear would see.

$$L_{input} = 60 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the gain equation.**

The required acoustic gain $G$ is the difference between output and input SPL:

$$G = L_{output} - L_{input}$$
$$G = 90 \text{ dB SPL} - 60 \text{ dB SPL} = 30 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Convert gain from dB into a linear pressure ratio (for sanity check).**

The dB SPL formula is $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$, so a gain of 30 dB corresponds to:

$$30 = 20 \log_{10}(p_{out}/p_{in})$$
$$\log_{10}(p_{out}/p_{in}) = 1.5$$
$$p_{out}/p_{in} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.62$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

A linear pressure amplification of ~32× is required. In practice, the hearing aid would provide slightly more gain to account for the *insertion loss* (typically 5–10 dB in the high frequencies due to the earmold occluding the canal). So a target gain of ~35–40 dB is realistic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the gain in **dB** (logarithmic), not as a linear ratio. The KTU marking scheme explicitly deducts marks for using linear units when decibel units are asked for, since dB compression is the entire engineering rationale for the device.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cochlear Implant Filterbank Frequency Allocation

**Problem:** Design a 16-channel filterbank for a CI that covers 200 Hz to 8000 Hz using logarithmic (constant-Q) spacing.

**Step 1 — Define the boundary conditions.**

$$f_{min} = 200 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_{max} = 8000 \text{ Hz}, \quad N = 16 \text{ channels}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric (logarithmic) spacing factor.**

For $N$ channels to span a frequency ratio $R = f_{max}/f_{min}$, the multiplicative step $\alpha$ between adjacent center frequencies satisfies:

$$\alpha^{N} = \frac{f_{max}}{f_{min}}$$

Substituting:

$$\alpha^{16} = \frac{8000}{200} = 40$$
$$\alpha = 40^{1/16}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$\ln(\alpha) = \frac{\ln(40)}{16} = \frac{3.6889}{16} = 0.2306$$
$$\alpha = e^{0.2306} \approx 1.2593$$

So each successive channel center is approximately **1.259×** the previous (i.e., about a **1 octave** spans 3.32 channels, which matches psychoacoustic critical-band data).

**Step 4 — Compute the first few center frequencies.**

$$f_c(1) = 200 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_c(2) = 200 \times 1.2593 = 251.9 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_c(3) = 251.9 \times 1.2593 = 317.2 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_c(4) = 317.2 \times 1.2593 = 399.4 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_c(8) = 200 \times 1.2593^7 = 200 \times 4.768 = 953.6 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_c(16) = 200 \times 1.2593^{15} = 200 \times 38.05 = 7610 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Verify monotonic coverage and bandwidth.**

Each channel is a band-pass filter. The lower 3-dB cutoff of channel $i+1$ overlaps the upper 3-dB cutoff of channel $i$ by design (50% overlap is common in CI processors for smooth perceptual coverage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** This exact logarithmic allocation is used in commercial devices like the Cochlear™ Nucleus and MED-EL systems. The implementation in DSP is a *polyphase filterbank* with 16 sub-bands, followed by envelope extraction (half-wave rectification + low-pass filter at ~400 Hz) for each band.

### 3.3 Derivation: Visual Magnifier Power (Worked Numerical)

**Problem:** A low-vision patient has a visual acuity of 6/60 (i.e., they can read at 6 m what a normal eye reads at 60 m). They wish to read a newspaper at 25 cm. What magnifier power $M$ is required?

**Step 1 — Translate Snellen acuity to the required angular magnification.**

The patient's resolution is 10× worse than normal (60/6 = 10). To read the same letter size as a normally-sighted person, the patient needs to view the text at **10× larger angular size**.

$$M_{required} = \frac{60}{6} = 10\times$$

**Step 2 — Design a simple magnifier with this power.**

The magnifier formula is $M = 1 + D/f$, with $D = 25$ cm.

$$10 = 1 + \frac{25}{f}$$
$$\frac{25}{f} = 9$$
$$f = 25/9 \approx 2.78 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering check.**

A convex lens of focal length ~2.8 cm is physically realizable (about +35 diopter spectacle lens). However, the working distance of such a strong magnifier is only ~3 cm, which is uncomfortable and restricts lighting. In practice, a **stand magnifier** or **CCTV** with adjustable zoom would be clinically preferred for sustained reading.

This is why Braille displays remain expensive (~$3,000–$5,000 for a 40-cell unit).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define a cochlear implant. List its external and internal components, and state the function of the electrode array in two lines.*

**Model Answer:**

A **cochlear implant (CI)** is an implantable neural prosthetic device that restores the sensation of sound in individuals with severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss by directly electrically stimulating the spiral ganglion cells of the auditory nerve, thereby bypassing the damaged hair cells of the cochlea.

**External components:** (1) Microphone, (2) Sound processor (DSP-based), (3) Transmitter coil with magnet.

**Internal components:** (1) Receiver-stimulator, (2) Decoding/demodulation circuitry, (3) Multi-channel electrode array.

**Function of the electrode array:** The electrode array, typically containing 12–22 platinum-iridium contacts, is surgically inserted into the scala tympani of the cochlea. Each electrode delivers biphasic current pulses to a specific tonotopic location, with basal electrodes stimulating high-frequency neurons and apical electrodes stimulating low-frequency neurons, thereby mimicking the cochlea's natural place-frequency mapping. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
*Differentiate between a 6-dot Braille cell and an 8-dot Braille cell. State one application where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 6-Dot Braille | 8-Dot Braille |
|---|---|---|
| Dot matrix | 2 columns × 3 rows | 2 columns × 4 rows |
| Unique patterns | $2^6 = 64$ | $2^8 = 256$ |
| Character set covered | Latin alphabet + punctuation + numerals (with number-sign prefix) | Full 8-bit ASCII set |
| Application | Standard literary Braille (books, labels) | Computer Braille, mathematical notation (Nemeth code) |

**6-dot** is preferred for **printed literary material** and embossed signage, where compactness and tactile fatigue are concerns. **8-dot** is preferred for **computer screen-reader output and STEM content** where lowercase letters, accented characters, and mathematical symbols must be reproduced unambiguously. **(3 marks)**

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Module-Level)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Regular Exam, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Behind-The-Ear (BTE) digital hearing aid**. Discuss the role of Automatic Gain Control (AGC) and feedback cancellation in its operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Behind-The-Ear (BTE) digital hearing aid consists of four main functional blocks in cascade:

**Block 1 — Microphone:** A miniature electret or MEMS microphone transduces the acoustic pressure wave $p_{in}(t)$ into a small analog voltage (typically in the millivolt range). Modern BTE aids use **directional microphone arrays** (often a front–back pair) to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in noisy environments by spatially attenuating sounds arriving from behind the listener.

**Block 2 — Pre-amplifier and AGC:** The pre-amplifier raises the microphone signal to a level suitable for the analog-to-digital converter. The Automatic Gain Control (AGC) circuit is a non-linear gain element that automatically reduces gain for high-level inputs and increases gain for low-level inputs. The compression ratio $CR = \Delta L_{in}/\Delta L_{out}$ is set by the audiologist (e.g., $CR = 2:1$ for moderate loss) so that the wide dynamic range of natural sound (~120 dB) is compressed into the patient's residual dynamic range (~30–50 dB). This prevents loud sounds from causing discomfort and amplifies soft sounds to audibility.

**Block 3 — DSP and Feedback Cancellation:** The signal is digitized and processed by a digital signal processor. Algorithms applied include (i) **multi-channel compression** (separate compression for low/mid/high bands), (ii) **adaptive feedback cancellation** using an LMS or NLMS adaptive filter that estimates the acoustic feedback path (leakage from receiver back to microphone) and subtracts it, allowing higher gain before whistling, and (iii) **noise reduction** using spectral subtraction or modulation-based detection.

**Block 4 — Receiver and Earmold:** The processed signal is converted back to analog and delivered by a balanced-armature receiver through a custom earmold into the ear canal. The earmold's vent diameter controls the low-frequency response (open fit vs. occluded fit).

**Working Principle (Summary):** The hearing aid does NOT restore normal hearing. It applies a **frequency-specific, level-dependent gain** matched to the patient's audiogram, using a prescriptive formula such as **NAL-NL2** or **DSL-v5**, so that amplified speech falls within the patient's audible and comfortable range.

**Diagrammatic Representation:** Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram (mermaid flowchart). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A patient has a **70 dB HL hearing loss at 2 kHz**. Calculate the required acoustic gain at 2 kHz if conversational speech (60 dB SPL at the microphone) must be delivered at 35 dB SL. What physical amplifier gain (voltage ratio) does this correspond to? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine the required output SPL in the ear canal.**

$$L_{out} = L_{thresh} + SL = 70 \text{ dB HL} + 35 \text{ dB} = 105 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the input SPL at the microphone.**

$$L_{in} = 60 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required acoustic gain in dB.**

$$G = L_{out} - L_{in} = 105 - 60 = 45 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Convert acoustic dB gain to linear pressure ratio.**

The dB SPL scale uses 20·log₁₀ for pressure ratios:

$$45 = 20 \log_{10}(A_v)$$
$$A_v = 10^{45/20} = 10^{2.25}$$
$$A_v \approx 177.8$$

**Step 5 — Convert pressure ratio to voltage ratio for an electroacoustic amplifier.**

For a typical BTE receiver with sensitivity $S_{rec}$ (dB SPL for 1 V input, typically ~110 dB SPL/V) and microphone sensitivity $S_{mic}$ (typically −40 dB V/Pa), the required voltage gain is:

$$A_{V,elec} = A_v \cdot \frac{S_{rec}}{S_{mic}} \text{ (in linear units)}$$

Numerically, assuming $S_{rec} = 316$ (i.e., 110 dB SPL/V ≈ 0.316 Pa/V) and $S_{mic} = 0.01$ V/Pa (−40 dB V/Pa):

$$A_{V,elec} \approx 177.8 \times \frac{316}{0.01} \approx 177.8 \times 31600 \approx 5.62 \times 10^6$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

A 45 dB acoustic gain is **very high** and approaches the limit where acoustic feedback becomes uncontrollable. This is precisely why **directional microphones, feedback-cancellation DSP, and well-fitted earmolds** are critical in BTE aids for severe losses. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice, Same Module**

**(a)** Describe the **anatomical and physiological basis** for designing a cochlear implant. Explain the **filterbank, envelope extraction, and tonotopic mapping** steps in a modern CI sound-processing strategy (e.g., ACE or CIS). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Anatomical Basis:** The human cochlea is a snail-shaped, fluid-filled organ in the inner ear. Sound waves travelling through the oval window set up travelling waves along the basilar membrane (BM). Because the BM varies in stiffness and mass from base (stiff, narrow) to apex (floppy, wide), each location on the BM has a characteristic resonance frequency — the **place theory of hearing**. High frequencies (~20 kHz) peak at the base; low frequencies (~20 Hz) peak at the apex. The BM's motion deflects the **hair cells** of the organ of Corti, which transduce mechanical motion into neural spikes in the **spiral ganglion neurons** of the cochlear nerve (CN VIII).

In **sensorineural hearing loss**, the hair cells are damaged or destroyed, but the spiral ganglion neurons typically survive (at least in reduced numbers). This survival is what makes a CI feasible: the implant must bypass the dead hair cells and directly stimulate the surviving neurons with electrical current.

**Filterbank:** A typical modern CI processor uses 16–22 band-pass filters whose center frequencies are spaced **logarithmically** (constant-Q) along the audio range (~200 Hz – 8 kHz). This mimics the cochlea's place-frequency mapping with a finite number of channels.

**Envelope Extraction:** For each filterbank channel, the processor computes the **slowly-varying temporal envelope** of the band-limited signal using half-wave rectification followed by a low-pass filter (cutoff ~200–400 Hz). The fine-structure (rapid oscillations) within each band is discarded because the CI cannot reliably transmit it through pulsatile stimulation; only the envelope information is preserved.

**Coded Strategy (CIS — Continuous Interleaved Sampler):** In CIS, channels are stimulated sequentially (not simultaneously, to avoid channel interaction due to current summation in the cochlear fluids). Biphasic current pulses are delivered at a fixed rate per channel (e.g., 900 pulses/sec/channel), and the **amplitude** of each pulse is modulated by the corresponding channel's envelope. The most apical (lowest-frequency) channel stimulates the most apical electrode, and so on — implementing **tonotopic mapping**.

**ACE (Advanced Combination Encoder):** A variant of CIS used in Cochlear™ devices where only the $n$ channels with the highest envelope amplitudes in each frame are selected for stimulation, reducing power consumption and channel interaction.

**Diagrammatic Reference:** See Section 4.2 block diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A 16-channel CI uses logarithmic filter spacing from 200 Hz to 8000 Hz. **(i)** Calculate the geometric step factor $\alpha$. **(ii)** Compute the center frequencies of channels 1, 4, 8, and 16. **(iii)** State why a logarithmic (not linear) spacing is used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Step factor:**

$$\alpha^{16} = \frac{8000}{200} = 40$$
$$\alpha = 40^{1/16} = e^{(\ln 40)/16} = e^{0.2306} \approx 1.2593$$

**(ii) Center frequencies:**

$$f_c(1) = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_c(4) = 200 \times 1.2593^3 = 200 \times 1.9963 = 399.3 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_c(8) = 200 \times 1.2593^7 = 200 \times 4.768 = 953.6 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_c(16) = 200 \times 1.2593^{15} = 200 \times 38.05 = 7610 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Justification for logarithmic spacing:**

The human cochlea exhibits **logarithmic frequency selectivity** along the basilar membrane — that is, each location on the BM responds to a *constant fractional bandwidth* (constant number of semitones), not a constant absolute bandwidth. This is also reflected in the **Bark scale** of critical bands used in psychoacoustics. Linear spacing would over-sample the high-frequency end (wasting electrodes on frequencies the patient cannot discriminate) and under-sample the low-frequency end (which carries vital pitch and vowel-formant information). Logarithmic spacing therefore matches the implant's channel allocation to the auditory system's perceptual resolution, maximizing speech-intelligibility performance for a finite number of electrodes. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Always quote hearing-aid gain in **dB**, NOT as a linear ratio, unless explicitly asked. A common mistake is to write "$A_v = 31.6$" without the "×" multiplier symbol or to omit the dB scale altogether. **Penalty: −1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing hearing aid vs cochlear implant:** A hearing aid **amplifies** sound. A cochlear implant **electrically stimulates the auditory nerve** with current pulses derived from extracted envelopes. Mixing these up is a **−2 mark** deduction in any 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Omitting the formula reference:** If a question says "calculate the gain," you **must** show the dB formula $G = L_{out} - L_{in}$ first, then substitute values. Skipping the formula statement loses the "method" mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the 20·log₁₀ vs 10·log₁₀ distinction:** dB SPL uses **20·log₁₀** (pressure), dB W uses **10·log₁₀** (power). This is a common trap; in a CI/binaural cochlear stimulation question, using 10·log₁₀ when the question specifies pressure ratios will lose 1–2 marks.
